**Explanation**: When $y=1$, the second factor becomes 1; when $y=0$, the first factor becomes 1. This single equation handles both cases.

**Step 3**: Assume i.i.d. samples and write the likelihood.

$$\mathcal{L}(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \prod_{i=1}^{m} \left( h_{\theta}\!\left(\mathbf{x}^{(i)}\right) \right)^{y^{(i)}} \left( 1 - h_{\theta}\!\left(\mathbf{x}^{(i)}\right) \right)^{1-y^{(i)}}$$

**Explanation**: Independence across samples lets us multiply individual probabilities to get joint likelihood.

**Step 4**: Take the log-likelihood to convert products into sums.

$$\ell(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \log \mathcal{L}(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left[ y^{(i)} \log h_{\theta}\!\left(\mathbf{x}^{(i)}\right) + \left(1 - y^{(i)}\right) \log\!\left(1 - h_{\theta}\!\left(\mathbf{x}^{(i)}\right)\right) \right]$$

**Explanation**: The logarithm is a monotonic transformation, so maximizing $\ell$ is equivalent to maximizing $\mathcal{L}$. The product-to-sum conversion is the key mathematical trick.

**Step 5**: Convert maximization to minimization by negating.

$$J(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = -\frac{1}{m} \ell(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = -\frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left[ y^{(i)} \log h + (1-y^{(i)}) \log(1-h) \right]$$

**Explanation**: Optimization frameworks typically minimize. The $\tfrac{1}{m}$ factor scales the cost by sample size, making the loss comparable across datasets.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style 7-Mark Problem)

**Problem**: Given the dataset $\{(1, 2), (2, 3), (3, 5), (4, 7)\}$, compute one step of gradient descent for $\theta_{0}, \theta_{1}$ initialized to $\theta_{0} = 0, \theta_{1} = 0$ with $\alpha = 0.01$.

**Step 1**: Construct the design matrix and target.

$$\mathbf{X} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 \\ 1 & 3 \\ 1 & 4 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{y} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ 3 \\ 5 \\ 7 \end{bmatrix}, \quad m = 4$$

**Step 2**: Compute initial predictions. With $\theta_{0} = \theta_{1} = 0$, every prediction is 0.

$$h_{\theta}(x^{(i)}) = 0 + 0 \cdot x^{(i)} = 0$$

**Step 3**: Compute the error vector.

$$\mathbf{h} - \mathbf{y} = \begin{bmatrix} -2 \\ -3 \\ -5 \\ -7 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4**: Compute the gradient for $\theta_{0}$.

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_{0}} = \frac{1}{4} \sum_{i=1}^{4} \left(0 - y^{(i)}\right) \cdot 1 = \frac{1}{4} \left(-2 - 3 - 5 - 7\right) = \frac{-17}{4} = -4.25$$

**Step 5**: Compute the gradient for $\theta_{1}$.

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_{1}} = \frac{1}{4} \sum_{i=1}^{4} \left(0 - y^{(i)}\right) \cdot x^{(i)} = \frac{1}{4} \left(-2 - 6 - 15 - 28\right) = \frac{-51}{4} = -12.75$$

**Step 6**: Apply the gradient descent update.

$$\begin{aligned}
\theta_{0} &:= 0 - 0.01 \cdot (-4.25) = 0.0425 \\
\theta_{1} &:= 0 - 0.01 \cdot (-12.75) = 0.1275
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 7**: Verify by computing new predictions.

$$\hat{y}_{1} = 0.0425 + 0.1275(1) = 0.17, \quad \hat{y}_{2} = 0.2975, \quad \hat{y}_{3} = 0.425, \quad \hat{y}_{4} = 0.5525$$

**Interpretation**: After one step, predictions are still well below true values. With more iterations (typically thousands), the parameters will converge toward $\theta_{0} \approx -0.5, \theta_{1} \approx 1.7$, which closely fits the data trend.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Regression Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Raw Dataset] --> nodeB[Data Preprocessing]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Feature Engineering]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Train Test Split]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Initialize Parameters Theta]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Compute Hypothesis h_x]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Compute Cost J_Theta]
    nodeG --> nodeH{Convergence Check}
    nodeH -- No --> nodeI[Update Parameters via Gradient Descent]
    nodeI --> nodeF
    nodeH -- Yes --> nodeJ[Final Model h_x]
    nodeJ --> nodeK[Evaluate using RMSE and R2]
    nodeK --> nodeL[Deploy for Continuous Prediction]
```

> **Diagram Walkthrough**: The pipeline begins with raw data, normalizes features, partitions data into training and test sets, then iteratively optimizes parameters until convergence. The final model produces continuous numerical predictions evaluated by RMSE and $R^{2}$.

---

## 4.2 Logistic Regression Classification Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Labeled Training Data] --> nodeB[Feature Scaling]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Add Bias Term x0 equals 1]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Compute Linear Combination z equals Theta transpose x]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Apply Sigmoid g of z]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Output Probability P of y equals 1]
    nodeF --> nodeG{Threshold 0.5}
    nodeG -- Yes --> nodeH[Predict Class 1]
    nodeG -- No --> nodeI[Predict Class 0]
    nodeF --> nodeJ[Compute Cross Entropy Loss]
    nodeJ --> nodeK[Backpropagate Gradient]
    nodeK --> nodeL[Update Theta via Gradient Descent]
    nodeL --> nodeD
```

> **Diagram Walkthrough**: Inputs are transformed via a linear combination, passed through the sigmoid for probability estimation, thresholded at 0.5 to produce discrete class labels, and the cross-entropy loss drives iterative parameter updates.

---

## 4.3 Decision Boundary Visualization (Conceptual Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph InputSpace[FEATURE SPACE]
        node1[Feature x1]
        node2[Feature x2]
    end
    subgraph ModelRegion[MODEL REGION]
        node3[Linear Combination Theta transpose x equals 0]
        node4[Sigmoid Activation]
        node5[Probability Score 0 to 1]
    end
    subgraph OutputRegion[OUTPUT REGION]
        node6[Class 0 Region h less than 0.5]
        node7[Decision Boundary h equals 0.5]
        node8[Class 1 Region h greater than 0.5]
    end
    node1 --> node3
    node2 --> node3
    node3 --> node4
    node4 --> node5
    node5 --> node6
    node5 --> node7
    node5 --> node8
```

> **Diagram Walkthrough**: Features enter the linear combiner, get squashed by the sigmoid into probabilities, and the threshold at 0.5 partitions the input space into two half-planes separated by the **decision boundary** (a line in 2D, a hyperplane in higher dimensions).

---

## 4.4 Conceptual Comparison Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Supervised Learning] --> nodeB{Output Type Question}
    nodeB -- Continuous --> nodeC[REGRESSION]
    nodeB -- Discrete --> nodeD[CLASSIFICATION]
    nodeC --> nodeE[Linear Regression]
    nodeC --> nodeF[Polynomial Regression]
    nodeC --> nodeG[Ridge and Lasso Regression]
    nodeD --> nodeH[Logistic Regression]
    nodeD --> nodeI[K Nearest Neighbors]
    nodeD --> nodeJ[Support Vector Machines]
    nodeD --> nodeK[Decision Trees]
    nodeE --> nodeL[Predict Numeric Value]
    nodeF --> nodeL
    nodeG --> nodeL
    nodeH --> nodeM[Predict Class Label]
    nodeI --> nodeM
    nodeJ --> nodeM
    nodeK --> nodeM
```

> **Diagram Walkthrough**: Supervised learning bifurcates based on the nature of the target variable. Continuous targets lead to regression models; discrete targets lead to classification models. The rightmost column distinguishes what each branch ultimately predicts.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2]`

**Q: Differentiate between Regression and Classification with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer**:

| Aspect | Regression | Classification |
|---|---|---|
| **Target variable** | Continuous (real-valued) | Discrete (categorical labels) |
| **Example task** | Predicting house price (₹45,00,000) | Predicting email type (Spam / Not-Spam) |
| **Output** | Numerical value | Class label or probability |
| **Loss function** | MSE, RMSE, MAE | Cross-Entropy, Hinge Loss |
| **Evaluation** | $R^{2}$, RMSE | Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1 |
| **Algorithms** | Linear, Polynomial, Ridge, Lasso | Logistic Regression, SVM, KNN, Naive Bayes |

**Example**: Predicting tomorrow's temperature (32.5 °C) is regression; classifying a tumor as malignant or benign is classification.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 2]`

**Q: Explain the role of the sigmoid function in logistic regression. Why cannot MSE be used as the cost function for logistic regression?**

**Model Answer**:

**Role of Sigmoid Function**:
- Maps any real-valued input $z = \boldsymbol{\theta}^{T}\mathbf{x}$ to a probability range $[0, 1]$.
- Defined as $g(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$.
- Converts the linear output into a probabilistic prediction suitable for binary classification.
- A threshold of $0.5$ partitions the feature space into two classes.

**Why MSE Fails for Logistic Regression**:
1. **Non-convexity**: When MSE is applied with sigmoid, the cost surface becomes **non-convex** with multiple local minima, making gradient descent unreliable.
2. **Gradient saturation**: For predictions close to 0 or 1, the gradient of MSE with respect to parameters becomes extremely small, causing the model to **stall during training**.
3. **Probabilistic inconsistency**: MSE assumes Gaussian-distributed residuals, which is incompatible with the **Bernoulli distribution** underlying binary outcomes.

**Correct Cost Function**: Binary Cross-Entropy (Log Loss) is used instead, derived from maximum likelihood estimation under a Bernoulli assumption. It produces a convex optimization landscape.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2 — Question A Choice]`

**Q: (a)** Derive the normal equation for linear regression. Under what conditions does it fail, and how can the failure be addressed? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For the dataset $\{(x, y)\} = \{(1, 3), (2, 5), (3, 7), (4, 9)\}$, compute the optimal parameters $\theta_{0}, \theta_{1}$ using the normal equation. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question A(a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1**: Define the cost function (MSE in matrix form):

$$J(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \frac{1}{2m} \left( \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\theta} - \mathbf{y} \right)^{T} \left( \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\theta} - \mathbf{y} \right)$$

**Step 2**: Expand using matrix identities:

$$J(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \frac{1}{2m} \left[ \boldsymbol{\theta}^{T} \mathbf{X}^{T} \mathbf{X} \boldsymbol{\theta} - 2 \, \mathbf{y}^{T} \mathbf{X} \boldsymbol{\theta} + \mathbf{y}^{T} \mathbf{y} \right]$$

**Step 3**: Differentiate with respect to $\boldsymbol{\theta}$:

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \boldsymbol{\theta}} = \frac{1}{m} \left( \mathbf{X}^{T} \mathbf{X} \boldsymbol{\theta} - \mathbf{X}^{T} \mathbf{y} \right)$$

**Step 4**: Set gradient to zero and solve:

$$\boldsymbol{\theta}^{*} = \left( \mathbf{X}^{T} \mathbf{X} \right)^{-1} \mathbf{X}^{T} \mathbf{y}$$

**Failure Conditions**:
1. $\mathbf{X}^{T} \mathbf{X}$ is **singular or non-invertible** (occurs when features are linearly dependent or $m < n$).
2. **Multicollinearity** between features causes numerical instability.
3. Very large $n$ (e.g., $n > 10^{5}$) makes inversion computationally expensive at $O(n^{3})$.

**Remedies**:
1. Use the **Moore–Penrose pseudo-inverse**: $\boldsymbol{\theta} = \left( \mathbf{X}^{T} \mathbf{X} + \lambda \mathbf{I} \right)^{-1} \mathbf{X}^{T} \mathbf{y}$ (Ridge regularization).
2. Apply **feature selection / dimensionality reduction** (PCA) to remove redundant features.
3. Use **gradient descent** for high-dimensional problems where matrix inversion is infeasible.

---

### Model Solution for Question A(b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1**: Construct the design matrix and target vector.

$$\mathbf{X} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 \\ 1 & 3 \\ 1 & 4 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{y} = \begin{bmatrix} 3 \\ 5 \\ 7 \\ 9 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2**: Compute $\mathbf{X}^{T} \mathbf{X}$.

$$\mathbf{X}^{T} \mathbf{X} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 & 3 & 4 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 \\ 1 & 3 \\ 1 & 4 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 10 \\ 10 & 30 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3**: Compute $\mathbf{X}^{T} \mathbf{y}$.

$$\mathbf{X}^{T} \mathbf{y} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 & 3 & 4 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 3 \\ 5 \\ 7 \\ 9 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 24 \\ 74 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4**: Compute the inverse of $\mathbf{X}^{T}\mathbf{X}$.

$$\det(\mathbf{X}^{T}\mathbf{X}) = (4)(30) - (10)(10) = 120 - 100 = 20$$

$$\left( \mathbf{X}^{T} \mathbf{X} \right)^{-1} = \frac{1}{20} \begin{bmatrix} 30 & -10 \\ -10 & 4 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1.5 & -0.5 \\ -0.5 & 0.2 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5**: Compute $\boldsymbol{\theta}^{*}$.

$$\boldsymbol{\theta}^{*} = \begin{bmatrix} 1.5 & -0.5 \\ -0.5 & 0.2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 24 \\ 74 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1.5(24) - 0.5(74) \\ -0.5(24) + 0.2(74) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 36 - 37 \\ -12 + 14.8 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -1 \\ 2.8 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Final Answer**: $\theta_{0} = -1$, $\theta_{1} = 2.8$. The regression line is $\hat{y} = -1 + 2.8 x$.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 2 — Question B Choice]`

**Q: (a)** Explain the logistic regression algorithm in detail. Derive its cost function from the maximum likelihood principle. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Given training data with $h_{\theta}(x) = 0.7$ and $y = 1$, compute the cost. Repeat with $h_{\theta}(x) = 0.2$ and $y = 0$. Comment on the results. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question B(a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1**: Introduce logistic regression as a classification algorithm.

Logistic regression predicts the probability $P(y = 1 \mid \mathbf{x}; \boldsymbol{\theta})$ by passing the linear combination $\boldsymbol{\theta}^{T}\mathbf{x}$ through the sigmoid function $g(z) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$.

**Step 2**: Write the Bernoulli likelihood.

$$P(y \mid \mathbf{x}; \boldsymbol{\theta}) = h^{y} (1-h)^{1-y}$$

**Step 3**: Form the joint likelihood over $m$ i.i.d. samples.

$$\mathcal{L}(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \prod_{i=1}^{m} \left( h^{(i)} \right)^{y^{(i)}} \left( 1 - h^{(i)} \right)^{1-y^{(i)}}$$

**Step 4**: Take the log-likelihood.

$$\ell(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left[ y^{(i)} \log h^{(i)} + (1-y^{(i)}) \log(1-h^{(i)}) \right]$$

**Step 5**: Negate and scale to obtain the cost function.

$$J(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = -\frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left[ y^{(i)} \log h^{(i)} + (1-y^{(i)}) \log(1-h^{(i)}) \right]$$

**Step 6**: Derive the gradient (same form as linear regression).

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_{j}} = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( h^{(i)} - y^{(i)} \right) x_{j}^{(i)}$$

**Explanation**: Despite the nonlinear hypothesis, the gradient retains a remarkably clean form — a key reason logistic regression is computationally tractable.

---

### Model Solution for Question B(b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1**: Recall the per-example cost formula.

$$\text{Cost}(h, y) = -y \log(h) - (1-y)\log(1-h)$$

**Step 2**: Case 1 — $h = 0.7$, $y = 1$.

$$\text{Cost} = -(1)\log(0.7) - (0)\log(0.3) = -\log(0.7)$$

Using $\log(0.7) \approx -0.3567$:

$$\text{Cost} = -(-0.3567) = 0.3567$$

**Step 3**: Case 2 — $h = 0.2$, $y = 0$.

$$\text{Cost} = -(0)\log(0.2) - (1)\log(0.8) = -\log(0.8)$$

Using $\log(0.8) \approx -0.2231$:

$$\text{Cost} = -(-0.2231) = 0.2231$$

**Step 4**: Interpretation.

| Case | Prediction | Actual | Cost | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0.7 | 1 | 0.3567 | Correct class, moderate confidence |
| 2 | 0.2 | 0 | 0.2231 | Correct class, high confidence (low cost) |

**Comment**: When the prediction matches the true label and is **far from the decision boundary** ($0.5$), the cost is small. As $h \to y$, cost $\to 0$. Conversely, if $h = 0.99$ and $y = 0$, the cost blows up to $\approx 4.6$, strongly penalizing confident wrong predictions. This asymmetric penalization is the strength of cross-entropy over MSE for classification.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark-Deduction Zones**:
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the bias term**: When writing the design matrix, students often omit the column of 1s for $\theta_{0}$, leading to an under-parameterized model. **Always include $x_{0} = 1$.**
> 
> 2. **Confusing linear and logistic regression gradients**: Many students write $\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_{j}} = \frac{1}{m} \sum h x$ (forgetting to subtract $y$). The correct form is $\frac{1}{m} \sum (h - y) x$.
> 
> 3. **Sigmoid output misinterpretation**: Students sometimes confuse $g(z) \geq 0.5$ with $z \geq 0$. Both are equivalent, but examiners expect the $z \geq 0$ justification (i.e., $\boldsymbol{\theta}^{T}\mathbf{x} \geq 0$ implies $y = 1$).
> 
> 4. **Cost function sign errors**: Cross-entropy cost is **negative** log-likelihood. Dropping the negative sign gives a maximization problem — a critical mistake.
> 
> 5. **Reporting parameters without units or interpretation**: Always state what $\theta_{1}$ represents (e.g., "for every unit increase in $x$, $y$ increases by 2.8 units").
> 
> 6. **Missing threshold justification in classification**: Logistic regression outputs a probability; you **must** explicitly state the threshold (typically $0.5$) to convert it into a class label.
> 
> 7. **Matrix inversion errors**: When computing $\left( \mathbf{X}^{T}\mathbf{X} \right)^{-1}$, students often make arithmetic mistakes in the determinant or adjugate. Show every intermediate step.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quick-Fire Revision — KTU 2024 Module 2: Regression and Classification**

- [x] **Regression** predicts **continuous** values; **Classification** predicts **discrete** class labels.
- [x] The **linear regression hypothesis** is $h_{\theta}(\mathbf{x}) = \boldsymbol{\theta}^{T}\mathbf{x}$; the **logistic hypothesis** is $h_{\theta}(\mathbf{x}) = g(\boldsymbol{\theta}^{T}\mathbf{x})$ where $g$ is the sigmoid.
- [x] **MSE** (half-mean-squared-error) is the standard regression cost; **Binary Cross-Entropy** is the standard logistic regression cost.
- [x] The **Normal Equation** $\boldsymbol{\theta} = \left( \mathbf{X}^{T}\mathbf{X} \right)^{-1} \mathbf{X}^{T}\mathbf{y}$ gives a closed-form solution for linear regression, but fails when $\mathbf{X}^{T}\mathbf{X}$ is singular.
- [x] **Gradient Descent** is an iterative optimization that scales to large feature spaces; the learning rate $\alpha$ critically affects convergence.
- [x] The gradient formula $\frac{1}{m} \sum (h - y) x$ is **identical** for both linear and logistic regression — only $h$ differs.
- [x] The **sigmoid function** $g(z) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$ has range $(0,1)$, satisfies $g'(z) = g(z)(1-g(z))$, and $g(0) = 0.5$.
- [x] The **decision boundary** is the locus where $h_{\theta}(\mathbf{x}) = 0.5$, equivalently $\boldsymbol{\theta}^{T}\mathbf{x} = 0$.
- [x] **Classification metrics**: Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1 Score. **Regression metrics**: MSE, RMSE, MAE, $R^{2}$.
- [x] Logistic regression is solved via **Maximum Likelihood Estimation** assuming a **Bernoulli likelihood**.
- [x] **Feature scaling** (standardization or min-max normalization) is essential for fast gradient descent convergence.
- [x] **Multicollinearity** can be mitigated using **Ridge** ($L_{2}$) or **Lasso** ($L_{1}$) regularization.
- [x] **Logistic Regression** is a classification algorithm despite its name — a high-frequency conceptual KTU trap.
- [x] For $k > 2$ classes, use **Softmax Regression** with $P(c \mid \mathbf{x}) = \frac{e^{\mathbf{w}_{c}^{T}\mathbf{x}}}{\sum_{j} e^{\mathbf{w}_{j}^{T}\mathbf{x}}}$.
- [x] **Bias-Variance Tradeoff**: too simple $\Rightarrow$ underfitting (high bias); too complex $\Rightarrow$ overfitting (high variance). Regularization helps balance this.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Linear Discriminant Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEOT604 - MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Linear Discriminant Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Linear Discriminant Analysis (LDA)** is a supervised, linear, generative-discriminative hybrid technique used simultaneously for **dimensionality reduction** and **class separability maximization**. Originally proposed by **Sir R. A. Fisher (1936)** as a two-class linear classifier, it was later generalized by **C. R. Rao (1948)** to the multi-class setting. 

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, LDA is defined as a projection-based statistical method that seeks a linear combination of features which best separates two or more classes by simultaneously **maximizing the between-class variance (separability)** and **minimizing the within-class variance (compactness)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PBEOT604 – Module 2):**
> LDA is treated as the foundational supervised linear projection technique, contrasted against Principal Component Analysis (PCA). KTU examiners specifically test the **scatter matrix formulations, the Rayleigh Quotient derivation, the eigenvalue solution**, and the practical **limit of (C − 1) discriminant axes** for a C-class problem.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The School Assembly Line"

Imagine a school with three houses — *Red, Blue, and Green* — and students from each house are standing in a crowded playground. The Principal wants to take a single photograph that, when seen, immediately tells which house a student belongs to. A bad angle (e.g., looking from above) makes all students look mixed up. A good angle (e.g., the diagonal view from the podium) keeps each house tightly bunched and far from the others.

**LDA is the camera-man** that finds this **"best viewing angle"** in feature space. It looks at the data and asks: *"From which direction should I look so that the projections of the same-class students are as close together as possible, and the projections of different classes are as far apart as possible?"*

### 1.3 Geometric vs. Probabilistic View

| Viewpoint | What LDA Does |
|---|---|
| **Geometric (Fisher's view)** | Finds projection direction $w$ that maximizes the ratio of between-class to within-class scatter. |
| **Probabilistic (Bayes view)** | Models $P(x \mid y)$ as multivariate Gaussian with a **shared covariance matrix** $\Sigma$ across classes; the decision boundary is linear. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Assumptions of LDA (Examiners Love This!)**
> 1. Each class is **multivariate Gaussian distributed**: $P(x \mid y = k) \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_k, \Sigma)$.
> 2. All classes share a **common covariance matrix** $\Sigma$ (homoscedasticity).
> 3. Features are **statistically independent of class labels** except through the mean.
> 4. Training data is **approximately normally distributed** for each class.
> 5. No severe **multicollinearity** in the feature space.

### 1.4 LDA vs. PCA — The Critical Distinction

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Mnemonic — "PCA = Unsupervised Spread, LDA = Supervised Separation"**
> - **PCA** maximizes **total variance** (ignores class labels).
> - **LDA** maximizes **class separability** (uses class labels).

| Parameter | PCA | LDA |
|---|---|---|
| Supervision | Unsupervised | Supervised |
| Objective | Maximize variance | Maximize class separation |
| Direction | Eigenvectors of $S_T$ (total scatter) | Generalized eigenvectors of $(S_W^{-1} S_B)$ |
| Max Components | $\min(n-1, p)$ | $C - 1$ |
| Assumption | Linearity | Multivariate Gaussian + Equal Covariance |

where $p$ = number of features, $n$ = samples, $C$ = number of classes.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Operational Pipeline of LDA

LDA, as mandated in the KTU Module 2 syllabus, executes in **seven rigorous sequential steps**. Each step has a clear mathematical purpose, and the KTU valuation key expects students to articulate the "why" behind every transition.

1. **Compute the Global Mean Vector $\mu$**: This is the centroid of the *entire* dataset, irrespective of class membership. It acts as the *reference origin* against which class means will be compared.
2. **Compute the Per-Class Mean Vector $\mu_i$**: For every class $i \in \{1, 2, \dots, C\}$, compute the mean of all samples belonging to that class. These represent *class prototypes*.
3. **Construct the Within-Class Scatter Matrix $S_W$**: This matrix quantifies the *spread of samples within each class*. It is the sum of covariance matrices of all classes.
4. **Construct the Between-Class Scatter Matrix $S_B$**: This matrix quantifies the *separation between class centroids*, weighted by the prior probability (or sample count) of each class.
5. **Formulate the Fisher Criterion (Rayleigh Quotient)**: The objective function $J(w)$ measures the ratio of between-class separation to within-class scatter along projection direction $w$.
6. **Solve the Generalized Eigenvalue Problem**: Differentiating $J(w)$ yields $S_B w = \lambda S_W w$, which when $S_W$ is invertible reduces to the standard eigenvalue problem $S_W^{-1} S_B w = \lambda w$.
7. **Project and Classify**: Sort eigenvectors by descending eigenvalue, pick the top $(C-1)$ for projection, and use Bayes' rule on the projected space.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master every formula below — these are direct exam-fetch formulas in the KTU 2024 scheme.**

| # | Formula / Expression | Meaning / Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\mu = \dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i$ | Global mean of all $N$ training samples. |
| 2 | $\mu_i = \dfrac{1}{N_i} \sum_{x \in D_i} x$ | Mean vector of class $i$ with $N_i$ samples. |
| 3 | $S_i = \sum_{x \in D_i} (x - \mu_i)(x - \mu_i)^{T}$ | Scatter matrix of a single class $i$. |
| 4 | $S_W = \sum_{i=1}^{C} S_i = \sum_{i=1}^{C} \sum_{x \in D_i} (x - \mu_i)(x - \mu_i)^{T}$ | **Within-class scatter matrix** (sum of all $S_i$). |
| 5 | $S_B = \sum_{i=1}^{C} N_i (\mu_i - \mu)(\mu_i - \mu)^{T}$ | **Between-class scatter matrix**. |
| 6 | $S_T = S_W + S_B = \sum_{i=1}^{N} (x_i - \mu)(x_i - \mu)^{T}$ | **Total scatter matrix** (sum of 1 and 2). |
| 7 | $J(w) = \dfrac{w^{T} S_B w}{w^{T} S_W w}$ | **Fisher's Linear Discriminant** criterion. |
| 8 | $S_B w = \lambda S_W w$ | **Generalized Eigenvalue Problem** (EVP). |
| 9 | $S_W^{-1} S_B w = \lambda w$ | Reduced standard EVP (assumes $S_W$ is non-singular). |
| 10 | $w^* = \arg\max_{w} J(w)$ | Optimal projection direction (top eigenvector). |
| 11 | Number of discriminant axes = $C - 1$ | Max rank of $S_B$ is $(C - 1)$. |
| 12 | $\delta_k(x) = x^{T} \Sigma^{-1} \mu_k - \tfrac{1}{2} \mu_k^{T} \Sigma^{-1} \mu_k + \ln P(y=k)$ | **LDA Discriminant Function** (Bayes view). |

**Engineering Utility of LDA:**
- **Face Recognition (Fisherfaces)**: Belhumeur et al. (1997) used LDA to outperform Eigenfaces (PCA) under lighting variation.
- **Medical Diagnosis**: Tumor classification in gene-expression microarrays where $p \gg n$.
- **Speech Recognition**: Speaker identification in MFCC feature space.
- **Pre-processing for SVMs / Neural Networks**: Reducing dimensionality before feeding to non-linear classifiers.

### 2.3 Why the Rayleigh Quotient is the Right Objective

The Fisher criterion $J(w) = \dfrac{w^T S_B w}{w^T S_W w}$ is structurally a **Rayleigh Quotient**, which has a beautiful mathematical property: its extrema correspond to the eigenvectors of the matrix pencil $(S_B, S_W)$. Specifically:
- The **maximum** of $J(w)$ equals the **largest generalized eigenvalue** $\lambda_{\max}$.
- The **minimizer** $w^*$ is the corresponding eigenvector.

> [!TIP]
> **Invariance Property of $J(w)$:** 
> If you scale $w \to \alpha w$ (for any $\alpha \neq 0$), the ratio $J(w)$ remains unchanged. Therefore, only the **direction** of $w$ matters, not its magnitude. This is why $J(w)$ is often called the *Generalized Rayleigh Quotient*.

### 2.4 The $C-1$ Theorem — Why Not More Dimensions?

The rank of $S_B$ cannot exceed $C - 1$. This is because $S_B$ is a weighted sum of $C$ outer products of the form $(\mu_i - \mu)(\mu_i - \mu)^T$, but the deviations $\mu_i - \mu$ all live in a subspace where they sum to zero: $\sum_i N_i (\mu_i - \mu) = 0$. Hence the **effective rank is $(C-1)$**, meaning LDA gives you at most $C-1$ useful projection directions.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Many KTU students write "LDA reduces to 1D" or "LDA reduces to 2D" without justification. The correct statement is: **"LDA can produce at most $C-1$ discriminant components, where $C$ is the number of classes."**
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: From the Fisher Criterion to the Eigenvalue Problem

We begin with the Fisher criterion for a **two-class** problem and show step-by-step how it yields the generalized eigenvalue problem.

**Step 1 — Define the Projected Means.**

When data $x \in \mathbb{R}^{p}$ is projected onto a 1-D line via direction $w \in \mathbb{R}^{p}$, the scalar projection is $y = w^{T} x$. The projected mean for class $i$ is:

$$
\tilde{\mu}_i = \frac{1}{N_i} \sum_{x \in D_i} w^{T} x = w^{T} \mu_i
$$

This is a scalar that lives on the 1-D projection line.

**Step 2 — Express the Projected Scatters in Quadratic Form.**

The projected within-class scatter is the variance of projected samples of class $i$:

$$
\tilde{s}_i^2 = \sum_{x \in D_i} (w^{T} x - w^{T} \mu_i)^{2} = \sum_{x \in D_i} w^{T} (x - \mu_i)(x - \mu_i)^{T} w = w^{T} S_i w
$$

Summing over both classes for the two-class case gives the total within-class scatter on the line:

$$
\tilde{S}_W = \tilde{s}_1^2 + \tilde{s}_2^2 = w^{T} S_W w
$$

The projected between-class scatter is the squared distance between the two projected class means:

$$
\tilde{S}_B = (\tilde{\mu}_1 - \tilde{\mu}_2)^{2} = (w^{T} \mu_1 - w^{T} \mu_2)^{2} = w^{T} (\mu_1 - \mu_2)(\mu_1 - \mu_2)^{T} w = w^{T} S_B w
$$

**Step 3 — Define the Fisher Criterion.**

The objective is to maximize class separation while minimizing class spread:

$$
J(w) = \frac{\tilde{S}_B}{\tilde{S}_W} = \frac{w^{T} S_B w}{w^{T} S_W w}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Constraint and Form the Lagrangian.**

Since $J(w)$ is invariant to scaling, we can fix the denominator and maximize the numerator. We impose the constraint $w^{T} S_W w = 1$ and form the Lagrangian:

$$
\mathcal{L}(w, \lambda) = w^{T} S_B w - \lambda (w^{T} S_W w - 1)
$$

**Step 5 — Differentiate with Respect to $w$.**

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial w} = 2 S_B w - 2 \lambda S_W w = 0
$$

This simplifies to:

$$
S_B w = \lambda S_W w
$$

**Step 6 — Interpret the Result.**

This is the **generalized eigenvalue problem**. The optimal projection $w^*$ is the eigenvector of $(S_B, S_W)$ corresponding to the largest eigenvalue $\lambda_{\max}$. If $S_W$ is invertible, premultiplying by $S_W^{-1}$ gives the standard eigenvalue problem:

$$
S_W^{-1} S_B w = \lambda w
$$

Thus, $w^*$ is the **dominant eigenvector** of the matrix $S_W^{-1} S_B$.

> [!TIP]
> **Maximum value of $J(w)$:** It can be shown that $\max_w J(w) = \lambda_{\max}$, the largest generalized eigenvalue. This is because:
> $$J(w) = \frac{w^{T} S_B w}{w^{T} S_W w} = \frac{w^{T} (\lambda S_W w)}{w^{T} S_W w} = \lambda$$
> Substituting $S_B w = \lambda S_W w$ from the optimality condition.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Bayes' Discriminant Function for LDA

**Step 1 — Bayesian Setup.** Given a test point $x$, the predicted class is:

$$
\hat{y} = \arg\max_{k} P(y = k \mid x) = \arg\max_{k} P(x \mid y = k) \cdot P(y = k)
$$

**Step 2 — Gaussian Likelihood Assumption.** LDA assumes:

$$
P(x \mid y = k) = \frac{1}{(2\pi)^{p/2} \vert \Sigma \vert^{1/2}} \exp\left( -\frac{1}{2} (x - \mu_k)^{T} \Sigma^{-1} (x - \mu_k) \right)
$$

The key simplification: **all classes share the same $\Sigma$**.

**Step 3 — Take the Logarithm and Drop Constants.** The log-posterior is:

$$
\ln P(y = k \mid x) \propto \ln P(x \mid y = k) + \ln P(y = k)
$$

Expanding the quadratic form and simplifying:

$$
\delta_k(x) = x^{T} \Sigma^{-1} \mu_k - \frac{1}{2} \mu_k^{T} \Sigma^{-1} \mu_k + \ln P(y = k)
$$

**Step 4 — Why the Decision Boundary is Linear.**

The decision boundary between classes $k$ and $l$ is the set of $x$ where $\delta_k(x) = \delta_l(x)$. Setting them equal:

$$
x^{T} \Sigma^{-1} (\mu_k - \mu_l) = \frac{1}{2} (\mu_k^{T} \Sigma^{-1} \mu_k - \mu_l^{T} \Sigma^{-1} \mu_l) - \ln \frac{P(y = k)}{P(y = l)}
$$

The left-hand side is **linear in $x$**, confirming the linear decision boundary.

> [!NOTE]
> **Estimation of $\Sigma$:** In practice, $\Sigma$ is estimated as the pooled within-class covariance:
> $$\hat{\Sigma} = \frac{1}{N - C} \sum_{i=1}^{C} \sum_{x \in D_i} (x - \mu_i)(x - \mu_i)^{T} = \frac{S_W}{N - C}$$
> This is a maximum-likelihood estimator under the homoscedasticity assumption.

### 3.3 Analytical Worked Example

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern Question):**
> Consider a 2-class, 2-feature dataset. Compute $S_W$, $S_B$, and the optimal projection direction $w^*$.
>
> | Sample | $x_1$ | $x_2$ | Class |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | $x_1$ | 1 | 2 | $C_1$ |
> | $x_2$ | 2 | 3 | $C_1$ |
> | $x_3$ | 3 | 4 | $C_1$ |
> | $x_4$ | 6 | 8 | $C_2$ |
> | $x_5$ | 7 | 9 | $C_2$ |
> | $x_6$ | 8 | 10 | $C_2$ |
>
> **Step 1 — Class Means:**
> $$\mu_1 = \begin{pmatrix} 2 \\ 3 \end{pmatrix}, \quad \mu_2 = \begin{pmatrix} 7 \\ 9 \end{pmatrix}, \quad \mu = \begin{pmatrix} 4.5 \\ 6 \end{pmatrix}$$
>
> **Step 2 — Within-Class Scatter Matrices:**
> For $C_1$: deviations are $(-1,-1), (0,0), (1,1)$. Hence $S_1 = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 2 \\ 2 & 2 \end{pmatrix}$.
> For $C_2$: deviations are $(-1,-1), (0,0), (1,1)$. Hence $S_2 = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 2 \\ 2 & 2 \end{pmatrix}$.
> Therefore $S_W = S_1 + S_2 = \begin{pmatrix} 4 & 4 \\ 4 & 4 \end{pmatrix}$.
>
> **Step 3 — Between-Class Scatter Matrix:**
> $$S_B = 3 \begin{pmatrix} 2.5 \\ 3 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 2.5 & 3 \end{pmatrix} + 3 \begin{pmatrix} -2.5 \\ -3 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} -2.5 & -3 \end{pmatrix} = 6 \begin{pmatrix} 6.25 & 7.5 \\ 7.5 & 9 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 37.5 & 45 \\ 45 & 54 \end{pmatrix}$$
>
> **Step 4 — Solve Eigenvalue Problem:** $S_W^{-1} = \frac{1}{4 \cdot 4 - 4 \cdot 4}$ ... but $S_W$ here is singular! In practice, we add a regularization term $\epsilon I$ (a "shrinkage" trick), giving $S_W^{\text{reg}} = S_W + \epsilon I$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: LDA Algorithm Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Input: Labeled Dataset D] --> stepB[Step 1: Compute Global Mean mu]
    stepB --> stepC[Step 2: Compute Class Means mu_i for i=1..C]
    stepC --> stepD[Step 3: Build Within-Class Scatter S_W]
    stepD --> stepE[Step 4: Build Between-Class Scatter S_B]
    stepE --> stepF[Step 5: Formulate Fisher Criterion J w]
    stepF --> stepG[Step 6: Solve S_B w = lambda S_W w]
    stepG --> stepH[Step 7: Sort Eigenvectors by Descending Eigenvalue]
    stepH --> stepI[Step 8: Pick Top C-1 Components]
    stepI --> stepJ[Step 9: Project Data onto W matrix]
    stepJ --> stepK[Step 10: Bayes Classifier on Reduced Space]
    stepK --> endL[Output: Predicted Class Labels]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Geometric Intuition of LDA vs PCA

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    raw[High-Dim Feature Space] --> pca[PCA Projection]
    raw --> lda[LDA Projection]
    pca --> pcaOut[Direction of Maximum Total Variance]
    lda --> ldaOut[Direction of Maximum Class Separation]
    pcaOut --> pcaNote[Ignores class labels]
    ldaOut --> ldaNote[Maximizes between class scatter / within class scatter]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Block-Level Functional Architecture of LDA Classifier

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph INPUT["INPUT BLOCK"]
        xvec[Feature Vector x in R^p]
    end
    subgraph SCATTER["SCATTER COMPUTATION BLOCK"]
        sw[Within-Class Scatter S_W]
        sb[Between-Class Scatter S_B]
    end
    subgraph EIGEN["EIGEN-SOLVER BLOCK"]
        evp[Generalized EVP: S_B w = lambda S_W w]
        eigvec[Eigenvectors w_1, w_2, ..., w_C-1]
    end
    subgraph PROJ["PROJECTION BLOCK"]
        wmat[Projection Matrix W = w_1 ... w_C-1]
        yproj[Projected Vector y = W^T x in R to the C-1]
    end
    subgraph DECISION["DECISION BLOCK"]
        bayes[Bayes Discriminant Function delta_k y]
        argmax[argmax over k]
    end
    xvec --> sw
    xvec --> sb
    sw --> evp
    sb --> evp
    evp --> eigvec
    eigvec --> wmat
    xvec --> wmat
    wmat --> yproj
    yproj --> bayes
    bayes --> argmax
    argmax --> outclass[Predicted Class Label]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Decision Boundary Behavior

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    cond1{Is data approximately Gaussian?}
    cond1 -- Yes --> cond2{Are covariances equal across classes?}
    cond1 -- No --> out1[LDA may underperform, use QDA]
    cond2 -- Yes --> out2[LDA is OPTIMAL Bayes classifier]
    cond2 -- No --> out3[Use QDA or regularized LDA]
    out2 --> note2[Linear decision boundary]
    out3 --> note3[Quadratic decision boundary]
    out1 --> note1[Non-Gaussian assumption violated]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
*Define Linear Discriminant Analysis. State any two assumptions made in LDA.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Linear Discriminant Analysis (LDA) is a supervised statistical method used for classification and dimensionality reduction. It projects high-dimensional data onto a lower-dimensional space by finding linear combinations of features that best separate two or more classes by maximizing the ratio of between-class variance to within-class variance.

**Two key assumptions:**
1. Each class follows a **multivariate Gaussian distribution** $P(x \mid y=k) \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_k, \Sigma)$.
2. All classes share a **common covariance matrix** $\Sigma$ (equal covariance across classes / homoscedasticity).

*(Allocate ~1 mark for definition, ~1.5 marks for each assumption with brief justification. Total = 3 marks.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*Why does LDA produce at most $(C-1)$ discriminant components, where $C$ is the number of classes?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The between-class scatter matrix $S_B = \sum_{i=1}^{C} N_i (\mu_i - \mu)(\mu_i - \mu)^T$ is a sum of $C$ rank-1 matrices. However, the deviations $(\mu_i - \mu)$ satisfy the constraint $\sum_{i=1}^{C} N_i (\mu_i - \mu) = 0$, which imposes one linear dependency among them. Consequently, the rank of $S_B$ is at most $C-1$. Since the discriminant directions are eigenvectors of $S_W^{-1} S_B$, there are at most $C-1$ non-zero eigenvalues, giving at most $C-1$ useful projection directions.

*(Allocate ~2 marks for the rank argument, ~1 mark for the eigenvector conclusion. Total = 3 marks.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]`

**(a)** Derive the Fisher Linear Discriminant criterion $J(w) = \dfrac{w^{T} S_B w}{w^{T} S_W w}$ and show that maximizing it leads to the generalized eigenvalue problem $S_B w = \lambda S_W w$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a 3-class classification problem, explain how LDA generalizes the two-class formulation. Discuss the role of $S_B$, $S_W$, and the constraint that the number of discriminant axes is at most $C-1$. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Detailed Derivation (7 Marks):**

We consider a two-class problem with classes $C_1$ and $C_2$, having means $\mu_1$ and $\mu_2$ and within-class scatter matrices $S_1$ and $S_2$.

*Step 1 — Project Data onto Line $w$ (1 mark):*

The projection of any data point $x$ onto direction $w$ is the scalar $y = w^T x$. The projection of the class mean $\mu_i$ is $\tilde{\mu}_i = w^T \mu_i$.

*Step 2 — Quantify Projected Within-Class Scatter (1.5 marks):*

The within-class scatter of class $i$ after projection is:

$$
\tilde{s}_i^{2} = \sum_{x \in D_i} (w^{T} x - w^{T} \mu_i)^{2} = \sum_{x \in D_i} w^{T} (x - \mu_i)(x - \mu_i)^{T} w = w^{T} S_i w
$$

Total within-class scatter is $\tilde{S}_W = \tilde{s}_1^{2} + \tilde{s}_2^{2} = w^{T} S_W w$, where $S_W = S_1 + S_2$.

*Step 3 — Quantify Projected Between-Class Scatter (1.5 marks):*

$$
\tilde{S}_B = (\tilde{\mu}_1 - \tilde{\mu}_2)^{2} = w^{T} (\mu_1 - \mu_2)(\mu_1 - \mu_2)^{T} w = w^{T} S_B w
$$

*Step 4 — Formulate the Fisher Criterion and Solve (3 marks):*

$$
J(w) = \frac{w^{T} S_B w}{w^{T} S_W w}
$$

Since scaling $w \to \alpha w$ leaves $J(w)$ unchanged, we impose the constraint $w^{T} S_W w = 1$ and maximize $w^{T} S_B w$ using a Lagrangian:

$$
\mathcal{L}(w, \lambda) = w^{T} S_B w - \lambda (w^{T} S_W w - 1)
$$

Setting $\partial \mathcal{L} / \partial w = 0$:

$$
2 S_B w - 2 \lambda S_W w = 0 \implies S_B w = \lambda S_W w
$$

This is the generalized eigenvalue problem. If $S_W$ is non-singular, this becomes $S_W^{-1} S_B w = \lambda w$. The optimal $w^*$ is the eigenvector of $S_W^{-1} S_B$ corresponding to the largest eigenvalue $\lambda_{\max}$, and the maximum of $J(w)$ equals $\lambda_{\max}$.

---

**Part (b) — Multi-Class LDA Explanation (7 Marks):**

*Step 1 — Generalize the Scatter Matrices (2 marks):*

For $C$ classes, the **within-class scatter matrix** becomes:

$$
S_W = \sum_{i=1}^{C} S_i = \sum_{i=1}^{C} \sum_{x \in D_i} (x - \mu_i)(x - \mu_i)^{T}
$$

This is a $p \times p$ matrix (where $p$ = number of features) and is shared across all classes. It captures the **compactness** of each class.

The **between-class scatter matrix** is:

$$
S_B = \sum_{i=1}^{C} N_i (\mu_i - \mu)(\mu_i - \mu)^{T}
$$

where $\mu = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i$ is the global mean. It captures the **separation** of class centroids from the global centroid, weighted by class size $N_i$.

*Step 2 — Multi-Class Fisher Criterion (2 marks):*

For projection onto a $(C-1)$-dimensional subspace using a matrix $W \in \mathbb{R}^{p \times (C-1)}$, the criterion becomes:

$$
J(W) = \frac{\det(W^{T} S_B W)}{\det(W^{T} S_W W)}
$$

The columns of $W^*$ are the $(C-1)$ generalized eigenvectors of $(S_B, S_W)$ corresponding to the largest eigenvalues.

*Step 3 — Why $C-1$ Discriminant Axes? (3 marks):*

The matrix $S_B$ is built from $C$ rank-1 matrices of the form $(\mu_i - \mu)(\mu_i - \mu)^T$. However, the deviations satisfy the linear constraint:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{C} N_i (\mu_i - \mu) = 0
$$

This is easily verified by expanding the global mean. The constraint reduces the rank by one, so $\text{rank}(S_B) \le C - 1$. Since $S_B$ is symmetric positive semi-definite, it has at most $C-1$ positive eigenvalues. The product $S_W^{-1} S_B$ therefore has at most $C-1$ non-zero eigenvalues, and each non-zero eigenvalue contributes one useful projection direction. Hence LDA yields at most $C-1$ discriminant components, regardless of the original feature dimensionality $p$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the constraint derivation** when explaining $C-1$: Students often just *state* the result without showing $\sum N_i (\mu_i - \mu) = 0$. Always prove the linear dependency.
> 2. **Confusing $S_T$ with $S_B$**: $S_T = S_W + S_B$ is the *total* scatter (used in PCA), not the between-class scatter.
> 3. **Not mentioning the invertibility of $S_W$**: If $S_W$ is singular (e.g., when $p > n$), one must use the pseudo-inverse $S_W^{+}$ or apply regularization (shrinkage LDA).
> 4. **Skipping the Lagrangian step**: KTU examiners specifically look for the constrained optimization step. Writing only the final EVP without derivation costs 2-3 marks.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`

**(a)** Explain the **Bayesian (Probabilistic) derivation of LDA**. Starting from Bayes' theorem, derive the discriminant function $\delta_k(x) = x^{T} \Sigma^{-1} \mu_k - \frac{1}{2} \mu_k^{T} \Sigma^{-1} \mu_k + \ln P(y=k)$ and justify why the resulting decision boundary is linear. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare and contrast **LDA and PCA** as dimensionality reduction techniques. Use a tabular comparison and a real-world example (e.g., face recognition) to illustrate when LDA outperforms PCA and vice versa. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Bayesian Derivation (7 Marks):**

*Step 1 — Bayes' Theorem Setup (1 mark):*

$$
P(y = k \mid x) = \frac{P(x \mid y = k) P(y = k)}{P(x)}
$$

The decision rule is $\hat{y} = \arg\max_k P(y = k \mid x)$, which is equivalent to maximizing $P(x \mid y = k) P(y = k)$ since $P(x)$ is a constant w.r.t. $k$.

*Step 2 — Gaussian Likelihood (1.5 marks):*

LDA assumes each class-conditional density is multivariate Gaussian with **class-specific mean** $\mu_k$ but **shared covariance matrix** $\Sigma$:

$$
P(x \mid y = k) = \frac{1}{(2\pi)^{p/2} \vert \Sigma \vert^{1/2}} \exp\left( -\frac{1}{2} (x - \mu_k)^{T} \Sigma^{-1} (x - \mu_k) \right)
$$

*Step 3 — Logarithm and Algebraic Simplification (2.5 marks):*

Taking $\ln$ of $P(x \mid y=k) P(y=k)$ and dropping constants independent of $k$:

$$
\ln P(x \mid y = k) + \ln P(y = k) = -\frac{1}{2} (x - \mu_k)^{T} \Sigma^{-1} (x - \mu_k) + \ln P(y = k) + C
$$

Expanding the quadratic:

$$
(x - \mu_k)^{T} \Sigma^{-1} (x - \mu_k) = x^{T} \Sigma^{-1} x - 2 x^{T} \Sigma^{-1} \mu_k + \mu_k^{T} \Sigma^{-1} \mu_k
$$

Dropping the $x^{T} \Sigma^{-1} x$ term (independent of $k$) and multiplying by $-1/2$:

$$
\delta_k(x) = x^{T} \Sigma^{-1} \mu_k - \frac{1}{2} \mu_k^{T} \Sigma^{-1} \mu_k + \ln P(y = k)
$$

*Step 4 — Linearity of Decision Boundary (2 marks):*

The decision boundary between classes $k$ and $l$ satisfies $\delta_k(x) = \delta_l(x)$:

$$
x^{T} \Sigma^{-1} (\mu_k - \mu_l) = \frac{1}{2} (\mu_k^{T} \Sigma^{-1} \mu_k - \mu_l^{T} \Sigma^{-1} \mu_l) - \ln \frac{P(y = k)}{P(y = l)}
$$

The left side is **linear in $x$**, the right side is constant. Hence the boundary is a **hyperplane** in $\mathbb{R}^{p}$, i.e., a **linear decision boundary**.

---

**Part (b) — LDA vs PCA Comparison (7 Marks):**

*Step 1 — Conceptual Distinction (2 marks):*

- **PCA** is *unsupervised*: it finds the directions of **maximum total variance** in the data, ignoring class labels. It captures the global geometry of the feature space.
- **LDA** is *supervised*: it finds the directions of **maximum class separability** by leveraging label information. It captures the geometry relevant to discrimination.

*Step 2 — Mathematical Distinction (2 marks):*

- PCA diagonalizes the **total scatter matrix** $S_T$: principal components are eigenvectors of $S_T$.
- LDA diagonalizes the **ratio** $S_W^{-1} S_B$: discriminant components are generalized eigenvectors of $(S_B, S_W)$.

*Step 3 — Real-World Example — Face Recognition (3 marks):*

The **Eigenfaces (PCA)** method of Turk & Pentland (1991) projects face images onto principal components. PCA chooses directions that maximize the variance of all face images combined. If lighting conditions vary more than identity, PCA will keep the lighting direction and **discard identity** — leading to misclassification.

The **Fisherfaces (LDA)** method of Belhumeur et al. (1997) uses class labels (each person is a class). Since all face images of the same person have similar lighting responses, the within-class scatter $S_W$ becomes large under varying lighting. By minimizing $S_W$, LDA *suppresses* lighting variation and emphasizes identity-relevant features. **Fisherfaces outperform Eigenfaces** under varying illumination.

However, when training data per class is scarce ($N_i$ small), $S_W$ is poorly estimated, and **LDA can underperform PCA**. In such cases, PCA is used as a pre-processing step, followed by LDA on the reduced space.

| Aspect | PCA | LDA |
|---|---|---|
| Supervision | Unsupervised | Supervised |
| Objective | Maximize variance | Maximize class separation |
| Max Components | $p$ | $C - 1$ |
| Computation | Eigendecomposition of $S_T$ | Generalized EVP of $(S_W^{-1} S_B)$ |
| Distribution | None assumed | Multivariate Gaussian |
| Best Use | Feature extraction, denoising | Classification, when labeled data exists |
| Failure Case | Discards discriminative info | Small sample, non-Gaussian data |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall Callout (Question B):**
> 1. **Confusing $S_T$ (PCA) with $S_B$ (LDA)**: This is the #1 valuation killer. Write the correct matrix for each method.
> 2. **Saying "LDA assumes data is linear"**: It does not. LDA produces a *linear* decision boundary, but the data itself can be non-linearly distributed.
> 3. **Ignoring the pooled covariance $\Sigma$**: The "shared covariance" is what makes LDA a *linear* classifier. Different covariances per class → QDA (quadratic).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Must-Know for KTU Exam Day**

- **Definition**: LDA is a *supervised* dimensionality reduction + classification technique that maximizes the **Fisher criterion** $J(w) = \frac{w^{T} S_B w}{w^{T} S_W w}$.
- **Key Matrices**:
  - $S_W = \sum_{i=1}^{C} \sum_{x \in D_i} (x - \mu_i)(x - \mu_i)^{T}$ — *within-class scatter*.
  - $S_B = \sum_{i=1}^{C} N_i (\mu_i - \mu)(\mu_i - \mu)^{T}$ — *between-class scatter*.
  - $S_T = S_W + S_B$ — *total scatter*.
- **Solution**: $w^* = $ dominant eigenvector of $S_W^{-1} S_B$. Solve $S_B w = \lambda S_W w$.
- **Limit on components**: At most $C - 1$ discriminant axes, where $C$ is the number of classes.
- **Assumptions**: Multivariate Gaussian, shared covariance, no severe multicollinearity.
- **Bayes' Discriminant**: $\delta_k(x) = x^{T} \Sigma^{-1} \mu_k - \frac{1}{2} \mu_k^{T} \Sigma^{-1} \mu_k + \ln P(y = k)$.
- **Decision Boundary**: Linear (hyperplane) because $\Sigma$ is shared.
- **LDA vs PCA**: LDA = supervised, uses labels, class separation; PCA = unsupervised, ignores labels, total variance.
- **Regularization**: When $S_W$ is singular, use shrinkage: $S_W^{\text{reg}} = S_W + \epsilon I$.
- **Real-world**: Fisherfaces (face recognition) outperforms Eigenfaces under lighting variation.
- **Generalization**: When covariances differ per class, use **QDA** (Quadratic Discriminant Analysis).
- **When LDA fails**: Non-Gaussian data, severe class imbalance, very small training set per class, or when $p \gg n$ (high-dimensional small sample).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Decision Trees
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBEOT604 - MACHINE LEARNING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Decision Trees # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Decision Tree** is a non-parametric, supervised machine learning algorithm that recursively partitions the feature space into homogeneous sub-regions using a tree-structured model of decisions and their possible consequences. It is a hierarchical, flowchart-like structure composed of three fundamental node types: - **Root Node (RN):** Represents the entire dataset and the initial splitting attribute. - **Internal / Decision Node (IN):** Represents a test on a single feature (attribute). - **Leaf / Terminal Node (LN):** Represents the final class label (classification) or a continuous value (regression). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A Decision Tree is a predictive modeling approach used in statistics, data mining, and machine learning, in which a model is built in the form of a tree structure that breaks down a dataset into smaller and smaller subsets while at the same time an associated decision tree is incrementally developed. The final result is a tree with **decision nodes** and **leaf nodes**. Formally, given a training dataset $D = \{(x_1, y_1), (x_2, y_2), \ldots, (x_n, y_n)\}$ where $x_i \in \mathbb{R}^d$ and $y_i \in \{C_1, C_2, \ldots, C_k\}$, the goal is to learn a function $f: \mathbb{R}^d \rightarrow \{C_1, C_2, \ldots, C_k\}$ that recursively partitions the input space into axis-aligned rectangular regions. ## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy Think of a **Decision Tree as a 20-Questions game** or a **medical diagnostic flowchart**. > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Analogy — The Doctor's Diagnosis:** Imagine a doctor diagnosing a patient. She first asks: *"Do you have a fever?"* — If **YES**, she asks another question: *"Is it above 101°F?"* — If **YES**, she concludes "Possible Infection" and prescribes tests. Each question splits the possibilities further until a final diagnosis is reached. A decision tree learns *exactly* the most informative question to ask at each step, in the optimal order, purely from data. **Why this works:** A decision tree mimics human decision-making by asking the *most informative* feature-based question first (the root), and progressively narrowing down to a conclusion through binary or multi-way splits. No math is required at the prediction step — just trace the path from root to leaf. ## 1.3 Types of Decision Trees There are two fundamental variants based on the target variable type: | Variant | Target Type | Output | Typical Algorithm | |---|---|---|---| | **Classification Tree** | Categorical (Discrete) | Class Label | **ID3**, **C4.5**, **CART** | | **Regression Tree** | Continuous (Numerical) | Real-valued Number | **CART** (with MSE criterion) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Favorite:** In the PBEOT604 syllabus, the primary focus is the **ID3 (Iterative Dichotomiser 3)** algorithm by Quinlan (1986) and its **Information Gain** criterion, along with **C4.5** (Gain Ratio extension) and the **CART** algorithm (Gini Index). Be prepared to compute splits manually in the exam. ## 1.4 High-Level Algorithmic Intuition At a high level, a decision tree is built by **Top-Down Induction**: 1. **Select the best feature** at the current node using a purity measure (Information Gain, Gain Ratio, or Gini Index). 2. **Split** the dataset into subsets based on the chosen feature's values. 3. **Recurse** on each subset — repeat steps 1-2 on every child node. 4. **Stop** when a node is *pure* (all samples belong to one class), or a stopping criterion is met (e.g., max depth, min samples). 5. **Prune** (optional) to reduce overfitting. This is captured in the famous **Hunt's Algorithm**, the conceptual ancestor of all modern decision tree learners (ID3, C4.5, CART). # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Central Question: "What is the Best Split?" At every internal node, the algorithm must decide **which feature** to split on. This is the heart of decision tree learning. The chosen feature is the one that produces the *purest* (most homogeneous) child subsets. To quantify "purity," we use **impurity measures**. The two most important impurity criteria (KTU syllabus mandated) are: ### 2.1.1 Entropy (Information-Theoretic Measure) **Entropy** measures the average amount of *information* (or *disorder/uncertainty*) in a dataset. It is rooted in **Claude Shannon's Information Theory (1948)**. For a dataset $S$ with $c$ classes and probability $p_i$ of class $i$: $$H(S) = -\sum_{i=1}^{c} p_i \log_2(p_i)$$ where $p_i = \frac{\vert S_i \vert}{\vert S \vert}$ is the proportion of samples in class $i$. > [!NOTE] > **Boundary Conditions of Entropy:** > - $H(S) = 0$ when the dataset is **perfectly pure** (only one class present). $\log_2(1) = 0$, so $-1 \cdot 0 = 0$. > - $H(S) = \log_2(c)$ when the dataset is **maximally impure** (uniform distribution across all $c$ classes). For binary: max is $1$ bit. ### 2.1.2 Conditional Entropy $H(S \vert A)$ The entropy of $S$ **conditional** on splitting by attribute $A$ with values $\{a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_v\}$: $$H(S \vert A) = \sum_{j=1}^{v} \frac{\vert S_j \vert}{\vert S \vert} H(S_j)$$ This is a **weighted average** of the entropies of the partitioned subsets $S_j$. ### 2.1.3 Information Gain (IG) — The ID3 Criterion Information Gain is the **reduction in entropy** achieved by partitioning $S$ on attribute $A$: $$IG(S, A) = H(S) - H(S \vert A)$$ The attribute that **maximizes** $IG(S, A)$ is selected for splitting at that node. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Pitfall:** Information Gain is **biased toward attributes with many distinct values** (e.g., a unique "ID" column would have $IG \approx 1$ but is useless for generalization). This is why **C4.5** introduced the **Gain Ratio** to normalize this bias. ### 2.1.4 Gain Ratio — The C4.5 Criterion $$GainRatio(S, A) = \frac{IG(S, A)}{SplitInfo(S, A)}$$ where the **Split Information** (intrinsic value) is: $$SplitInfo(S, A) = -\sum_{j=1}^{v} \frac{\vert S_j \vert}{\vert S \vert} \log_2\!\left(\frac{\vert S_j \vert}{\vert S \vert}\right)$$ ### 2.1.5 Gini Index — The CART Criterion **Gini Impurity** measures the probability of misclassifying a randomly chosen element if it were labeled randomly according to the class distribution: $$Gini(S) = 1 - \sum_{i=1}^{c} p_i^{\,2}$$ For a split on attribute $A$: $$Gini_{split}(S, A) = \sum_{j=1}^{v} \frac{\vert S_j \vert}{\vert S \vert} Gini(S_j)$$ The attribute with the **minimum** $Gini_{split}$ (equivalently, maximum **Gini Gain** $Gini(S) - Gini_{split}$) is selected. > [!NOTE] > **Entropy vs. Gini — KTU Comparison:** > - Both range in $[0, 1]$ for binary classification. > - **Entropy** involves $\log$ (computationally expensive). > - **Gini** is faster (no $\log$), and is the default in scikit-learn's `DecisionTreeClassifier`. > - They produce **very similar trees** in practice. ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > Memorize this table for the 3-mark derivations and 14-mark numerical questions. | # | Concept | Formula | Range | Used By | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Entropy** | $H(S) = -\sum_{i=1}^{c} p_i \log_2 p_i$ | $[0, \log_2 c]$ | ID3 | | 2 | **Conditional Entropy** | $H(S \vert A) = \sum_{j=1}^{v} \frac{\vert S_j \vert}{\vert S \vert} H(S_j)$ | $[0, \log_2 c]$ | ID3 | | 3 | **Information Gain** | $IG(S, A) = H(S) - H(S \vert A)$ | $[0, \log_2 c]$ | ID3 | | 4 | **Split Information** | $SplitInfo(S,A) = -\sum_{j=1}^{v} \frac{\vert S_j \vert}{\vert S \vert} \log_2\!\left(\frac{\vert S_j \vert}{\vert S \vert}\right)$ | $[0, \log_2 v]$ | C4.5 | | 5 | **Gain Ratio** | $GainRatio(S, A) = \dfrac{IG(S, A)}{SplitInfo(S, A)}$ | $[0, 1]$ | C4.5 | | 6 | **Gini Impurity** | $Gini(S) = 1 - \sum_{i=1}^{c} p_i^{\,2}$ | $[0, 1 - 1/c]$ | CART | | 7 | **Weighted Gini after Split** | $Gini_{split}(S, A) = \sum_{j=1}^{v} \frac{\vert S_j \vert}{\vert S \vert} Gini(S_j)$ | $[0, 1 - 1/c]$ | CART | | 8 | **Gini Gain** | $\Delta Gini = Gini(S) - Gini_{split}(S, A)$ | $[0, 0.5]$ | CART | | 9 | **Classification Error** | $1 - \max_i(p_i)$ | $[0, 1 - 1/c]$ | CART (alt.) | ## 2.3 The Major Decision Tree Algorithms — A Comparative Study | Feature | **ID3** (Quinlan, 1986) | **C4.5** (Quinlan, 1993) | **CART** (Breiman, 1984) | |---|---|---|---| | **Task** | Classification | Classification | Classification + Regression | | **Split Criterion** | Information Gain | Gain Ratio | Gini Index (clf) / MSE (reg) | | **Splitting Type** | Multi-way | Multi-way | **Binary only** | | **Handles Missing Values?** | No | Yes | Yes (surrogate splits) | | **Pruning** | None (pre-prune only) | Error-based post-pruning | Cost-complexity pruning | | **Continuous Attributes** | No | Yes (threshold split) | Yes | | **Output** | Multi-branch tree | Multi-branch tree | Binary tree | ## 2.4 Engineering Real-World Utility Decision Trees are foundational to **production-grade ML systems**: - **Medical Diagnosis:** Predicting disease presence from symptoms and lab tests (interpretable — required for FDA approval). - **Credit Scoring / Banking:** Loan default prediction — regulators *demand* interpretability that DTs provide. - **Ensemble Methods:** The building block of **Random Forests** and **XGBoost / Gradient Boosting** — the most successful algorithms in Kaggle competitions and industry. - **Embedded Systems / Edge ML:** Tiny DTs can be hand-coded in C for microcontrollers — no GPU needed. - **Remote Sensing & Agriculture:** Crop disease classification from satellite/leaf imagery (e.g., PlantVillage dataset). # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Worked Example ## 3.1 Worked Example: Building a Decision Tree Using ID3 (Information Gain) This is the **exact type of numerical question** that appears in KTU 14-mark questions. We work through it exhaustively, with **no skipped steps**. ### 3.1.1 The Dataset: "Should I Play Tennis?" Consider the following classic training dataset with 4 features and 14 instances: | Day | Outlook | Temperature | Humidity | Wind | PlayTennis | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Sunny | Hot | High | Weak | **No** | | 2 | Sunny | Hot | High | Strong | **No** | | 3 | Overcast | Hot | High | Weak | **Yes** | | 4 | Rain | Mild | High | Weak | **Yes** | | 5 | Rain | Cool | Normal | Weak | **Yes** | | 6 | Rain | Cool | Normal | Strong | **No** | | 7 | Overcast | Cool | Normal | Strong | **Yes** | | 8 | Sunny | Mild | High | Weak | **No** | | 9 | Sunny | Cool | Normal | Weak | **Yes** | | 10 | Rain | Mild | Normal | Weak | **Yes** | | 11 | Sunny | Mild | Normal | Strong | **Yes** | | 12 | Overcast | Mild | High | Strong | **Yes** | | 13 | Overcast | Hot | Normal | Weak | **Yes** | | 14 | Rain | Mild | High | Strong | **No** | **Class distribution:** 9 "Yes", 5 "No". Total $\vert S \vert = 14$. ### 3.1.2 Step 1: Compute the Entropy of the Full Dataset $S$ We need the prior probabilities of each class: $$p_{Yes} = \frac{9}{14}, \quad p_{No} = \frac{5}{14}$$ Apply the entropy formula: $$H(S) = -\sum_{i=1}^{c} p_i \log_2 p_i = -p_{Yes}\log_2 p_{Yes} - p_{No}\log_2 p_{No}$$ Substituting numerical values: $$H(S) = -\frac{9}{14} \log_2\!\left(\frac{9}{14}\right) - \frac{5}{14} \log_2\!\left(\frac{5}{14}\right)$$ Compute each term using $\log_2(9/14) \approx -0.6365$ and $\log_2(5/14) \approx -1.4854$: $$H(S) = -\frac{9}{14} \times (-0.6365) - \frac{5}{14} \times (-1.4854)$$ $$H(S) = 0.4092 + 0.5305 = 0.940 \; \text{bits}$$ > [!NOTE] > This is the **total uncertainty** in the dataset before any split. The maximum possible entropy for 2 classes is $\log_2(2) = 1$ bit, so our dataset is moderately impure. ### 3.1.3 Step 2: Compute Information Gain for Each Attribute We test all 4 candidate attributes and pick the one with the **highest IG**. #### Attribute A: Outlook — Values: {Sunny, Overcast, Rain} **Partition sizes:** Sunny = 5, Overcast = 4, Rain = 5. **Sub-entropy $H(S_{Sunny})$:** Sunny days have Yes=2, No=3 (from rows 1, 2, 8, 9, 11). $$H(S_{Sunny}) = -\frac{2}{5}\log_2\!\left(\frac{2}{5}\right) - \frac{3}{5}\log_2\!\left(\frac{3}{5}\right)$$ Using $\log_2(2/5) = -1.3219$ and $\log_2(3/5) = -0.7370$: $$H(S_{Sunny}) = -\frac{2}{5} \times (-1.3219) - \frac{3}{5} \times (-0.7370) = 0.5288 + 0.4422 = 0.971 \; \text{bits}$$ **Sub-entropy $H(S_{Overcast})$:** Overcast days have Yes=4, No=0 (rows 3, 7, 12, 13). **Pure node!** $$H(S_{Overcast}) = -\frac{4}{4}\log_2\!\left(\frac{4}{4}\right) - 0 = 0 \; \text{bits}$$ **Sub-entropy $H(S_{Rain})$:** Rain days have Yes=3, No=2 (rows 4, 5, 6, 10, 14). $$H(S_{Rain}) = -\frac{3}{5}\log_2\!\left(\frac{3}{5}\right) - \frac{2}{5}\log_2\!\left(\frac{2}{5}\right) = 0.4422 + 0.5288 = 0.971 \; \text{bits}$$ **Conditional Entropy:** $$H(S \vert Outlook) = \frac{5}{14}(0.971) + \frac{4}{14}(0) + \frac{5}{14}(0.971)$$ $$H(S \vert Outlook) = 0.3468 + 0 + 0.3468 = 0.694 \; \text{bits}$$ **Information Gain:** $$IG(S, Outlook) = H(S) - H(S \vert Outlook) = 0.940 - 0.694 = 0.246 \; \text{bits}$$ --- #### Attribute B: Humidity — Values: {High, Normal} **Partition sizes:** High = 7, Normal = 7. **Sub-entropy $H(S_{High})$:** High humidity has Yes=3, No=4 (rows 1, 2, 3, 4, 8, 12, 14). $$H(S_{High}) = -\frac{3}{7}\log_2\!\left(\frac{3}{7}\right) - \frac{4}{7}\log_2\!\left(\frac{4}{7}\right)$$ Using $\log_2(3/7) \approx -1.2224$ and $\log_2(4/7) \approx -0.8074$: $$H(S_{High}) = 0.5239 + 0.4613 = 0.985 \; \text{bits}$$ **Sub-entropy $H(S_{Normal})$:** Normal humidity has Yes=6, No=1 (rows 5, 6, 7, 9, 10, 11, 13). $$H(S_{Normal}) = -\frac{6}{7}\log_2\!\left(\frac{6}{7}\right) - \frac{1}{7}\log_2\!\left(\frac{1}{7}\right)$$ Using $\log_2(6/7) \approx -0.2224$ and $\log_2(1/7) \approx -2.8074$: $$H(S_{Normal}) = 0.1906 + 0.4011 = 0.592 \; \text{bits}$$ **Conditional Entropy:** $$H(S \vert Humidity) = \frac{7}{14}(0.985) + \frac{7}{14}(0.592) = 0.5(0.985) + 0.5(0.592) = 0.788 \; \text{bits}$$ **Information Gain:** $$IG(S, Humidity) = 0.940 - 0.788 = 0.152 \; \text{bits}$$ --- #### Attribute C: Wind — Values: {Weak, Strong} **Partition sizes:** Weak = 8, Strong = 6. **Sub-entropy $H(S_{Weak})$:** Weak wind has Yes=6, No=2 (rows 1, 3, 4, 5, 8, 9, 10, 13). $$H(S_{Weak}) = -\frac{6}{8}\log_2\!\left(\frac{6}{8}\right) - \frac{2}{8}\log_2\!\left(\frac{2}{8}\right)$$ Using $\log_2(6/8) = -0.4150$ and $\log_2(2/8) = -2.0000$: $$H(S_{Weak}) = 0.3113 + 0.5000 = 0.811 \; \text{bits}$$ **Sub-entropy $H(S_{Strong})$:** Strong wind has Yes=3, No=3 (rows 2, 6, 7, 11, 12, 14). $$H(S_{Strong}) = -\frac{3}{6}\log_2\!\left(\frac{3}{6}\right) - \frac{3}{6}\log_2\!\left(\frac{3}{6}\right) = 0.5 + 0.5 = 1.000 \; \text{bits}$$ **Conditional Entropy:** $$H(S \vert Wind) = \frac{8}{14}(0.811) + \frac{6}{14}(1.000) = 0.4637 + 0.4286 = 0.892 \; \text{bits}$$ **Information Gain:** $$IG(S, Wind) = 0.940 - 0.892 = 0.048 \; \text{bits}$$ --- #### Attribute D: Temperature — Values: {Hot, Mild, Cool} **Partition sizes:** Hot = 4, Mild = 6, Cool = 4. **Sub-entropy $H(S_{Hot})$:** Hot has Yes=2, No=2 (rows 1, 2, 3, 13). $$H(S_{Hot}) = -\frac{2}{4}\log_2\!\left(\frac{2}{4}\right) - \frac{2}{4}\log_2\!\left(\frac{2}{4}\right) = 0.5 + 0.5 = 1.000 \; \text{bits}$$ **Sub-entropy $H(S_{Mild})$:** Mild has Yes=4, No=2 (rows 4, 8, 10, 11, 12, 14). $$H(S_{Mild}) = -\frac{4}{6}\log_2\!\left(\frac{4}{6}\right) - \frac{2}{6}\log_2\!\left(\frac{2}{6}\right) = 0.3899 + 0.5283 = 0.918 \; \text{bits}$$ **Sub-entropy $H(S_{Cool})$:** Cool has Yes=3, No=1 (rows 5, 6, 7, 9). $$H(S_{Cool}) = -\frac{3}{4}\log_2\!\left(\frac{3}{4}\right) - \frac{1}{4}\log_2\!\left(\frac{1}{4}\right) = 0.3113 + 0.5000 = 0.811 \; \text{bits}$$ **Conditional Entropy:** $$H(S \vert Temperature) = \frac{4}{14}(1.000) + \frac{6}{14}(0.918) + \frac{4}{14}(0.811)$$ $$H(S \vert Temperature) = 0.2857 + 0.3934 + 0.2317 = 0.911 \; \text{bits}$$ **Information Gain:** $$IG(S, Temperature) = 0.940 - 0.911 = 0.029 \; \text{bits}$$ ### 3.1.4 Step 3: Comparison and Root Selection | Attribute | Information Gain (bits) | Rank | |---|---|---| | **Outlook** | **0.246** | 🥇 **1st (Root)** | | Humidity | 0.152 | 2nd | | Wind | 0.048 | 3rd | | Temperature | 0.029 | 4th | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Root Node Selection:** **Outlook** is chosen as the root because it has the **highest Information Gain** (0.246 bits). This is the ID3 decision rule. ### 3.1.5 Step 4: Recurse on Each Branch - **Outlook = Overcast** → All 4 samples are "Yes" → **Leaf Node: Play = Yes** (Pure, stop). - **Outlook = Sunny** → 5 samples, still impure. Recurse using only the Sunny subset with remaining attributes. - **Outlook = Rain** → 5 samples, still impure. Recurse using only the Rain subset with remaining attributes. Repeating the IG computation on the *Sunny subset* (Yes=2, No=3) typically yields **Humidity** as the next best splitter, and on the *Rain subset* (Yes=3, No=2) typically yields **Wind** as the next best splitter. ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Decision Criteria Comparison | Property | Information Gain | Gain Ratio | Gini Index | |---|---|---|---| | Mathematical Basis | Shannon Entropy | Normalized IG | Variance Impurity | | Computationally Fast? | No (uses $\log$) | No | **Yes** (no $\log$) | | Bias Toward Multi-valued? | **Yes — heavily** | **No (normalized)** | **Yes (mild)** | | Range of Values | $[0, \log_2 c]$ | $[0, 1]$ | $[0, 1 - 1/c]$ | | Preferred for | Small datasets, ID3 | Mixed-type data, C4.5 | Large data, CART, sklearn default | | Multi-way Splits? | Yes | Yes | **No (binary)** | ## 3.3 Analytical Derivation: Why is Entropy at Most $\log_2 c$? Using Jensen's inequality on the convex function $-\log_2 x$: $$H(S) = -\sum_{i=1}^{c} p_i \log_2 p_i = \sum_{i=1}^{c} p_i \log_2\!\left(\frac{1}{p_i}\right)$$ Since $-\log_2 x$ is convex, by Jensen's inequality: $$H(S) \leq \log_2\!\left(\sum_{i=1}^{c} p_i \cdot \frac{1}{p_i}\right) = \log_2(c)$$ Equality holds iff all $p_i$ are equal ($p_i = 1/c$), confirming maximum entropy occurs at uniform distribution. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — The Decision Tree Built on the "Play Tennis" Dataset > [!NOTE] > The following Mermaid block renders the **final decision tree** obtained from the ID3 algorithm in Section 3. Entropy (using $\log$) is slightly more curved; Gini is a parabola. For tree-building, the resulting trees are usually nearly identical — this is why CART's Gini is a popular fast alternative to ID3's Information Gain. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions > **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, 3 Marks Each]** ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model] **Q: Define a Decision Tree. Differentiate between Classification Trees and Regression Trees.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **Decision Tree** is a supervised, non-parametric machine learning algorithm that builds a tree-structured model by recursively partitioning the feature space based on attribute tests, with leaf nodes representing the final prediction. The differences are summarized in the table below: | Aspect | Classification Tree | Regression Tree | |---|---|---| | **Target Variable** | Discrete / Categorical | Continuous / Numerical | | **Leaf Node Output** | Class label (mode) | Real number (mean) | | **Split Criterion** | Information Gain, Gini | Mean Squared Error (MSE), MAE | | **Example** | "Spam / Not Spam" | "House Price Prediction" | *(Award 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for tabular differentiation with examples.)* --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model] **Q: State and explain the Information Gain splitting criterion used in the ID3 algorithm.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Information Gain (IG)** is the reduction in entropy achieved by partitioning a dataset $S$ using attribute $A$: $$IG(S, A) = H(S) - H(S \vert A)$$ where $H(S) = -\sum p_i \log_2 p_i$ is the entropy before the split, and $H(S \vert A)$ is the weighted average entropy of the partitions created by $A$. **ID3 Rule:** The attribute with the **maximum Information Gain** is selected as the splitting attribute at each node. The intuition is that a good split maximally *reduces uncertainty* (disorder) in the resulting subsets. A higher IG means the resulting child nodes are purer and more predictable. *(Award 1 mark for the formula, 1 mark for the selection rule, 1 mark for the intuitive explanation.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (With Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam — 14-Mark ESE Pattern, Module 2]** --- ### ❖ Question A — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model] **Q: (a)** Explain the ID3 algorithm for Decision Tree construction. Describe its key splitting criterion (Information Gain) and discuss the role of Entropy. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Consider the following training data for a binary classification problem. Build a decision tree using the ID3 (Information Gain) algorithm. Show all entropy and IG calculations. **(7 marks)** | Instance | Feature X1 | Feature X2 | Class | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | A | P | Yes | | 2 | A | Q | Yes | | 3 | B | P | No | | 4 | B | Q | No | | 5 | A | P | No | | 6 | B | P | No | | 7 | A | Q | Yes | | 8 | A | P | Yes | --- #### Model Solution (a) — 7 marks **ID3 Algorithm (Quinlan, 1986):** A greedy, top-down, recursive partitioning algorithm. Steps: 1. Compute the entropy $H(S)$ of the entire dataset. 2. For each unused attribute $A$, compute the conditional entropy $H(S \vert A)$ and the Information Gain $IG(S, A) = H(S) - H(S \vert A)$. 3. Select the attribute $A^*$ with the **maximum IG** as the splitting attribute. 4. Partition $S$ into subsets based on $A^*$'s values; create a child node for each. 5. If a child node is **pure** (single class) → make it a **leaf node**. Else, recurse on that child node with the remaining attributes. 6. Stop when all nodes are pure, or no more attributes exist (assign majority class). **Entropy** quantifies the disorder or uncertainty in a dataset. $H(S) = 0$ means pure (single class). $H(S) = \log_2 c$ is maximum disorder (uniform distribution). The information-theoretic interpretation: entropy equals the **expected number of bits** needed to encode the class label of a randomly drawn sample. #### Model Solution (b) — 7 marks **Step 1: Compute $H(S)$.** Out of 8 samples: Yes = 4, No = 4. $$H(S) = -\frac{4}{8}\log_2\!\left(\frac{4}{8}\right) - \frac{4}{8}\log_2\!\left(\frac{4}{8}\right) = -0.5 \times (-1) - 0.5 \times (-1) = 1.000 \; \text{bit}$$ **Step 2: Compute $IG$ for attribute X1** with values {A, B}. - X1 = A: rows {1, 2, 5, 7, 8}. Yes = 4, No = 1. $$H(S_A) = -\frac{4}{5}\log_2\!\left(\frac{4}{5}\right) - \frac{1}{5}\log_2\!\left(\frac{1}{5}\right) = 0.7219$$ - X1 = B: rows {3, 4, 6}. Yes = 0, No = 3. **Pure leaf.** $$H(S_B) = 0$$ - Conditional entropy: $$H(S \vert X1) = \frac{5}{8}(0.7219) + \frac{3}{8}(0) = 0.4512$$ - Information Gain: $$IG(S, X1) = 1.000 - 0.4512 = 0.549 \; \text{bits}$$ **Step 3: Compute $IG$ for attribute X2** with values {P, Q}. - X2 = P: rows {1, 3, 5, 6, 8}. Yes = 3, No = 2. $$H(S_P) = -\frac{3}{5}\log_2\!\left(\frac{3}{5}\right) - \frac{2}{5}\log_2\!\left(\frac{2}{5}\right) = 0.9710$$ - X2 = Q: rows {2, 4, 7}. Yes = 2, No = 1. $$H(S_Q) = -\frac{2}{3}\log_2\!\left(\frac{2}{3}\right) - \frac{1}{3}\log_2\!\left(\frac{1}{3}\right) = 0.9183$$ - Conditional entropy: $$H(S \vert X2) = \frac{5}{8}(0.9710) + \frac{3}{8}(0.9183) = 0.6069 + 0.3444 = 0.9513$$ - Information Gain: $$IG(S, X2) = 1.000 - 0.9513 = 0.049 \; \text{bits}$$ **Step 4: Comparison.** | Attribute | IG (bits) | |---|---| | **X1** | **0.549** ✅ | | X2 | 0.049 | **X1 is the root.** Branch X1 = B → pure leaf (Class = No). **Recurse on subset where X1 = A** (5 samples: Yes=4, No=1). The only remaining attribute is X2. - X2 = P: {1, 5, 8}. Yes = 2, No = 1. $$H = 0.9183$$ - X2 = Q: {2, 7}. Yes = 2, No = 0. **Pure leaf.** $$H = 0$$ - IG = 0.7219 - weighted ≈ 0.722 bits → X2 splits further. **Final Tree:** Root = X1 → If B then No; If A then test X2 → If Q then Yes; If P then mostly Yes (2 Yes vs 1 No → Yes). --- ### ❖ Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model] **Q: (a)** Define the Gini Index. Explain how CART uses it for splitting. Why is it preferred over Entropy in some implementations? **(7 marks)** **(b)** A dataset $S$ contains 10 samples: 6 of class C1 and 4 of class C2. After splitting on attribute A, the partitions are: $S_1$ = 4 C1, 1 C2 and $S_2$ = 2 C1, 3 C2. Compute the Gini Index of $S$, the Gini Index of each partition, and the weighted Gini of the split. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution (a) — 7 marks **Gini Index Definition:** The Gini Index (or Gini Impurity) of a dataset $S$ is the probability of incorrectly classifying a randomly chosen sample if its label were assigned according to the class distribution in $S$: $$Gini(S) = 1 - \sum_{i=1}^{c} p_i^{\,2}$$ where $p_i$ is the proportion of class $i$ in $S$. $Gini(S) = 0$ for a pure node and is maximized at $1 - 1/c$ for uniform class distribution. **CART (Classification and Regression Trees) by Breiman et al. (1984)** uses the Gini Index as its primary splitting criterion. At each node, CART: 1. Computes $Gini(S)$ for the current node. 2. For each candidate attribute $A$, computes the **weighted Gini of the split** $Gini_{split}(S, A) = \sum_j \frac{\vert S_j \vert}{\vert S \vert} Gini(S_j)$. 3. Selects the attribute $A^*$ that **minimizes** $Gini_{split}$ (equivalently, maximizes Gini Gain). **Why Gini over Entropy?** - **Computational speed:** Gini involves only squaring and summing — no logarithms. It is significantly faster on large datasets. - **Very similar results:** The trees produced are nearly identical in practice because Gini and Entropy are mathematically close (Gini ≈ $H/2$ near 0.5). - **Default choice:** Most production libraries (e.g., scikit-learn) default to Gini. - **CART extension:** Gini works seamlessly with CART's binary-only splitting, which is computationally simpler than multi-way splits. #### Model Solution (b) — 7 marks **Step 1: Gini Index of original dataset $S$.** 6 of C1, 4 of C2, total = 10. $$p_{C1} = 0.6, \quad p_{C2} = 0.4$$ $$Gini(S) = 1 - (0.6)^2 - (0.4)^2 = 1 - 0.36 - 0.16 = 0.48$$ **Step 2: Gini Index of $S_1$ (4 C1, 1 C2; total 5).** $$p_{C1} = 0.8, \quad p_{C2} = 0.2$$ $$Gini(S_1) = 1 - (0.8)^2 - (0.2)^2 = 1 - 0.64 - 0.04 = 0.32$$ **Step 3: Gini Index of $S_2$ (2 C1, 3 C2; total 5).** $$p_{C1} = 0.4, \quad p_{C2} = 0.6$$ $$Gini(S_2) = 1 - (0.4)^2 - (0.6)^2 = 1 - 0.16 - 0.36 = 0.48$$ **Step 4: Weighted Gini of the split.** $$Gini_{split}(S, A) = \frac{5}{10} \cdot Gini(S_1) + \frac{5}{10} \cdot Gini(S_2)$$ $$Gini_{split}(S, A) = 0.5 \times 0.32 + 0.5 \times 0.48 = 0.16 + 0.24 = 0.40$$ **Step 5: Gini Gain.** $$\Delta Gini = Gini(S) - Gini_{split}(S, A) = 0.48 - 0.40 = 0.08$$ **Interpretation:** The split on attribute $A$ reduces the Gini impurity from **0.48 to 0.40**, a reduction of **0.08**. CART will compare this $\Delta Gini$ against alternative splits and pick the one with the **largest reduction** (i.e., the lowest weighted Gini). --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls That Cost Marks:** > > 1. **Forgetting the $\log$ base:** Entropy must be computed using $\log_2$ (base 2), giving a result in **bits**. Using natural log ($\ln$) or base-10 is a **0.5 mark deduction**. > 2. **Skipping the conditional entropy step:** Examiners want to see the full breakdown: per-subset entropy, then the weighted sum, then the IG. Do not jump directly to the IG number. > 3. **Conflating $H(S \vert A)$ with the maximum of $H(S_j)$:** It is a **weighted sum**, not a max or simple average. Forgetting the weight $\frac{\vert S_j \vert}{\vert S \vert}$ loses 2 marks. > 4. **Gini vs. Gini Gain confusion:** The split is chosen to **minimize weighted Gini**, NOT to minimize Gini Gain. The gain is only for interpretation. > 5. **Rounding errors:** Carry at least 4 decimal places. Final answers within $\pm 0.01$ of the model are accepted. > 6. **Not stating the tie-breaker rule:** When two attributes have equal IG, the standard convention is to choose the one with **lower SplitInfo** (or alphabetically — state it explicitly). --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — Decision Trees (Module 2)** - ✅ **Decision Tree** is a hierarchical, flowchart-like model with **Root**, **Internal**, and **Leaf** nodes. It is non-parametric and handles both classification and regression. - ✅ The three most important **splitting criteria** are **Information Gain (ID3)**, **Gain Ratio (C4.5)**, and **Gini Index (CART)**. - ✅ **Entropy** is rooted in Shannon's information theory. $H(S) = -\sum p_i \log_2 p_i$. Range: $[0, \log_2 c]$. - ✅ **Information Gain** = $H(S) - H(S \vert A)$ — choose the attribute that **maximizes** IG. This is the ID3 rule. - ✅ **Gini Index** = $1 - \sum p_i^2$ — choose the attribute that **minimizes** the weighted Gini of the split. This is the CART rule. - ✅ **Gini is faster** than Entropy (no $\log$), and produces nearly identical trees. - ✅ **Gain Ratio** = $IG / SplitInfo$ — normalizes IG to handle the multi-valued attribute bias.
