This is the C4.5 rule. - ✅ **ID3** = multi-way splits, IG, no missing values. **C4.5** = multi-way, Gain Ratio, handles missing values. **CART** = binary splits, Gini, can do regression with MSE. - ✅ The root is the attribute with the **highest IG** (or lowest weighted Gini). Recursion stops at **pure nodes** or when a stopping criterion is met. - ✅ Decision trees are highly **prone to overfitting** — addressed via **pre-pruning** (early stopping) and **post-pruning** (replace subtrees with leaves). - ✅ Decision trees form the **building block of ensemble methods** — Random Forests, Gradient Boosting, XGBoost. - ✅ **Practical exam tip:** In KTU board exams, always show the full entropy table, then the IG table, then state the chosen root attribute explicitly. Examiners reward stepwise clarity. --- > **End of Notes — Decision Trees (PBEOT604 — Module 2)** > *Prepared in accordance with the KTU 2024 Scheme NEP 2020 syllabus.*

topics.title: Random Forests and Bagging
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEOT604 - MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Random Forests and Bagging

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bagging (Bootstrap Aggregating):** Bagging is an *ensemble meta-algorithm* designed to improve the stability and accuracy of machine learning algorithms by combining the predictions of multiple models trained on different bootstrap samples of the training dataset. It primarily addresses the **variance** component of prediction error.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Random Forest:** A Random Forest is a *supervised ensemble learning algorithm* that extends the bagging concept by constructing a multitude of **decision trees** during training and aggregating their outputs (via majority voting for classification or averaging for regression). It introduces an additional layer of randomness by selecting a random subset of features at each candidate split.

| Property | Bagging | Random Forest |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Base Learner | Any high-variance model (e.g., Decision Tree) | Specifically Decision Trees |
| Sampling | Bootstrap of rows | Bootstrap of rows |
| Feature Subsampling | Optional / All features | **Mandatory random subset of features** at each split |
| Introduced By | **Leo Breiman (1996)** | **Leo Breiman (2001)** |
| Primary Problem Solved | High Variance | High Variance + Decorrelation |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Medical Jury" Analogy:**
> Imagine you have a strange set of symptoms and need a diagnosis. Instead of visiting a **single doctor** (one decision tree) who might over-diagnose based on a few patterns, you visit a **medical jury panel of 100 doctors** (the forest). Each doctor sees a slightly different case history (bootstrap sample), and crucially, each doctor is restricted to look at only a **random subset of symptoms** (feature randomness) when forming their opinion. The final diagnosis is the **majority vote** of all 100 doctors. This collective decision is far more robust, less prone to freak correlations, and dramatically reduces misdiagnosis.

The fundamental idea is rooted in the statistical principle: **"A crowd of weak, highly diverse learners, when averaged, produces a single strong learner."**

## 1.3 Core Terminology

- **Bootstrap Sample**: A random sample drawn *with replacement* from the original training dataset of size $n$. On average, each bootstrap sample contains approximately **63.2%** of the unique training instances.
- **Ensemble**: A model composed of multiple base learners.
- **Out-of-Bag (OOB) Error**: The prediction error estimated using the ~36.8% of samples **not** included in a particular bootstrap sample. This serves as a *free cross-validation* metric.
- **Decorrelation**: The deliberate introduction of randomness so that individual trees make *different* mistakes, which then cancel out during voting.
- **Variance Reduction**: The mathematical guarantee that averaging $B$ independent (or weakly correlated) models reduces variance by a factor of $B$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Algorithm — Bagging (The "How" and "Why")

The Bagging procedure follows a strict sequence. Understanding the **why** at each step is critical for board examinations.

1. **Step 1 — Generate Bootstrap Samples:**
   - From a training dataset $D$ of size $n$, draw $B$ independent bootstrap samples $D_1, D_2, \dots, D_B$.
   - *Why?* Each sample mimics the original data distribution but introduces subtle statistical variation, forcing each base model to learn slightly different decision boundaries.

2. **Step 2 — Train Independent Base Models:**
   - Train a separate base learner (typically an unpruned Decision Tree) on each $D_i$ to obtain $f_1, f_2, \dots, f_B$.
   - *Why?* High-variance models (like deep trees) capture different nuances on each bootstrap sample.

3. **Step 3 — Aggregate Predictions:**
   - For **regression**: average the outputs.
   - For **classification**: take the **majority vote** (mode).

## 2.2 Operational Algorithm — Random Forest

Random Forest = **Bagging + Random Feature Selection at Every Split.**

1. **Steps 1 & 2 are identical to Bagging.**
2. **Step 2.5 — Modified Splitting Criterion:**
   - At each node of every decision tree, when determining the best split, the algorithm is **not allowed** to consider all $p$ features.
   - It randomly samples a subset of $m$ features, where typically:
     - $m = \sqrt{p}$ for classification tasks.
     - $m = p / 3$ for regression tasks.
   - *Why?* If one or two features are extremely strong predictors, they would be selected by *every* tree, causing all trees to become correlated. Restricting the feature pool **decorrelates** the trees.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Notes / Engineering Utility |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Probability of instance NOT in bootstrap | $\left(1 - \frac{1}{n}\right)^{n} \approx e^{-1} \approx 0.368$ | Used to derive the ~36.8% OOB fraction. |
| Fraction of unique instances in bootstrap | $1 - e^{-1} \approx 0.632$ | Each tree sees only ~63.2% unique data. |
| Bagging Regression Prediction | $\hat{f}_{\text{bag}}(x) = \frac{1}{B}\sum_{b=1}^{B} \hat{f}_{b}(x)$ | Arithmetic mean of $B$ tree predictions. |
| Bagging Classification Prediction | $\hat{C}_{\text{bag}}(x) = \text{mode}\left\{ \hat{C}_{b}(x) \right\}_{b=1}^{B}$ | Statistical mode (majority vote). |
| Variance Reduction via Averaging | $\text{Var}\left(\frac{1}{B}\sum \hat{f}_{b}\right) = \frac{1}{B^{2}}\sum \text{Cov}(\hat{f}_{i}, \hat{f}_{j})$ | If trees are independent, variance $\propto 1/B$. |
| Gini Impurity (Split Criterion) | $G(t) = 1 - \sum_{k=1}^{K} p_{k}^{2}$ | $p_k$ = proportion of class $k$ at node $t$. |
| Entropy (Alternative Criterion) | $H(t) = -\sum_{k=1}^{K} p_{k} \log_{2} p_{k}$ | Information-theoretic measure. |
| OOB Mean Squared Error (Regression) | $\text{MSE}_{\text{OOB}} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\left(y_{i} - \hat{y}_{\text{OOB}}^{(-i)}\right)^{2}$ | Free cross-validation error. |
| OOB Classification Error | $\text{Err}_{\text{OOB}} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\mathbb{1}\left(y_{i} \neq \hat{y}_{\text{OOB}}^{(-i)}\right)$ | Misclassification rate on OOB samples. |
| Feature Subsampling (Classification) | $m = \sqrt{p}$ | $p$ = total features. |
| Feature Subsampling (Regression) | $m = p / 3$ | Wider feature pool than classification. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Random Forests are the workhorse of tabular data ML competitions (Kaggle), bioinformatics (gene selection), remote sensing (land cover classification), and credit scoring. They require **no feature scaling**, handle missing values natively, and provide built-in feature importance rankings.

## 2.4 The Mathematical "Why" Behind Variance Reduction

If we have $B$ independent identically distributed (i.i.d.) random variables each with variance $\sigma^{2}$, the variance of their average is:

$$\text{Var}\left(\bar{X}\right) = \frac{\sigma^{2}}{B}$$

By training on bootstraps, the trees become *approximately* i.i.d., so the aggregated predictor has a variance that is roughly $1/B$ of a single tree's variance — but **only** if the trees are uncorrelated. This is precisely why Random Forest adds the feature subsampling trick: to actively destroy correlation between trees.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The 63.2% Bootstrap Coverage

Let us rigorously derive why each bootstrap sample contains approximately **63.2%** unique instances.

**Step 1 — Probability a single instance is picked once.**
For a dataset of size $n$, the probability that a specific instance $x_i$ is picked in a single draw (with replacement) is $\frac{1}{n}$.

**Step 2 — Probability of NOT being picked in a single draw.**
The complement event has probability:
$$P(\text{not picked in 1 draw}) = 1 - \frac{1}{n}$$

**Step 3 — Probability of NOT being picked in $n$ draws.**
Because each draw is independent and we draw exactly $n$ times to form a bootstrap sample of equal size:
$$P(\text{not picked in } n \text{ draws}) = \left(1 - \frac{1}{n}\right)^{n}$$

**Step 4 — Take the limit as $n \to \infty$.**
Using the classical limit definition of $e$:
$$\lim_{n \to \infty} \left(1 - \frac{1}{n}\right)^{n} = \frac{1}{e} \approx 0.3679$$

**Step 5 — Convert to fraction picked.**
$$P(\text{instance } i \text{ appears in bootstrap}) = 1 - \frac{1}{e} \approx 0.6321$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Expected unique instances in bootstrap} &= n \times \left(1 - \frac{1}{e}\right) \\
&\approx 0.632 \, n
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Expected OOB instances} &= n - 0.632 \, n \\
&= 0.368 \, n
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conversion Logic:** Each draw is independent because sampling is *with replacement* — placing the instance back allows it to be drawn again. This is why the multiplication of probabilities is valid (independence rule).

## 3.2 Derivation: Variance Reduction Under Averaging

Let $\hat{f}_1, \hat{f}_2, \dots, \hat{f}_B$ be the predictions of $B$ bagged models at a fixed input $x$. Assume they share common mean $\mu$ and common variance $\sigma^{2}$, but may be correlated with pairwise covariance $\text{Cov}(\hat{f}_i, \hat{f}_j) = \rho \sigma^{2}$ for $i \neq j$.

**Step 1 — Define the bagged predictor.**
$$\hat{f}_{\text{bag}}(x) = \frac{1}{B}\sum_{b=1}^{B} \hat{f}_{b}(x)$$

**Step 2 — Expand the variance of the sum.**
Using the identity $\text{Var}(aX + bY) = a^{2}\text{Var}(X) + b^{2}\text{Var}(Y) + 2ab\,\text{Cov}(X,Y)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Var}\left(\hat{f}_{\text{bag}}\right) &= \text{Var}\left(\frac{1}{B}\sum_{b=1}^{B} \hat{f}_{b}\right) \\
&= \frac{1}{B^{2}} \text{Var}\left(\sum_{b=1}^{B} \hat{f}_{b}\right) \\
&= \frac{1}{B^{2}} \left[ \sum_{b=1}^{B}\text{Var}(\hat{f}_{b}) + \sum_{i \neq j}\text{Cov}(\hat{f}_{i}, \hat{f}_{j}) \right] \\
&= \frac{1}{B^{2}} \left[ B \sigma^{2} + B(B-1)\rho\sigma^{2} \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Simplify.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Var}\left(\hat{f}_{\text{bag}}\right) &= \frac{\sigma^{2}}{B} + \frac{(B-1)\rho\sigma^{2}}{B} \\
&= \rho\sigma^{2} + \frac{1-\rho}{B}\sigma^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpret the result.**

- If $\rho = 0$ (perfectly decorrelated trees): $\text{Var} = \sigma^{2}/B$ — variance shrinks to zero as $B \to \infty$.
- If $\rho = 1$ (perfectly correlated trees): $\text{Var} = \sigma^{2}$ — bagging provides **zero** benefit.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic:** This derivation proves *mathematically* why the Random Forest trick of feature subsampling is essential. Bagging alone cannot reduce variance if trees remain correlated — Random Forest forces $\rho \to 0$.

## 3.3 Derivation: Gini Impurity for Splitting

The Gini impurity at a node $t$ measures the probability of misclassification if we randomly label an instance according to the class distribution at that node.

**Step 1 — Probability of randomly choosing class $k$.**
At node $t$, the proportion of instances belonging to class $k$ is $p_k$ where $\sum_{k} p_k = 1$.

**Step 2 — Probability of correct random label.**
If we randomly assign labels according to this distribution, the probability of correctly labeling an instance is $\sum_{k} p_k \cdot p_k = \sum_{k} p_k^{2}$.

**Step 3 — Probability of incorrect label.**
$$G(t) = 1 - \sum_{k=1}^{K} p_{k}^{2}$$

**Step 4 — Weighted impurity after split.**
For a split into left child $t_L$ and right child $t_R$ with proportions $p_L$ and $p_R$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
G_{\text{split}} &= p_L \cdot G(t_L) + p_R \cdot G(t_R) \\
&= \frac{N_L}{N} \left(1 - \sum_{k} p_{Lk}^{2}\right) + \frac{N_R}{N} \left(1 - \sum_{k} p_{Rk}^{2}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

The best split is the one that **maximizes** the impurity reduction $\Delta G = G(t) - G_{\text{split}}$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Bagging Pipeline — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Original Training Dataset D of size n] --> B[Bootstrap Sampling with Replacement]
    B --> C1[Bootstrap Sample D1]
    B --> C2[Bootstrap Sample D2]
    B --> C3[Bootstrap Sample DB]
    C1 --> D1[Base Model 1 Unpruned Tree]
    C2 --> D2[Base Model 2 Unpruned Tree]
    C3 --> D3[Base Model B Unpruned Tree]
    D1 --> E[Aggregation Layer]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E --> F1[Regression Arithmetic Mean]
    E --> F2[Classification Majority Vote]
    F1 --> G[Final Bagged Prediction]
    F2 --> G
    C1 --> H1[OOB Sample 1 ~37 percent]
    C2 --> H2[OOB Sample 2 ~37 percent]
    C3 --> H3[OOB Sample B ~37 percent]
    H1 --> I[OOB Error Estimation Free Validation]
    H2 --> I
    H3 --> I
```

## 4.2 Random Forest Internal Split Logic — Decorrelation Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Root Node All p features available] --> B[Randomly Select m subset of features]
    B --> C[Evaluate Best Split only on m features]
    C --> D{Stop Condition Met}
    D -- No --> E[Split Node into Left and Right Children]
    E --> B
    D -- Yes --> F[Leaf Node Assign Class or Value]
    F --> G[Tree 1 Final Output]
    F --> H[Tree 2 Final Output]
    F --> I[Tree B Final Output]
    G --> J[Majority Vote or Average]
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[Final Random Forest Prediction]
```

## 4.3 Random Forest — End-to-End Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph T1[Tree 1]
        T1A[Bootstrap D1] --> T1B[Random m features per split] --> T1C[Leaf predictions]
    end
    subgraph T2[Tree 2]
        T2A[Bootstrap D2] --> T2B[Random m features per split] --> T2C[Leaf predictions]
    end
    subgraph T3[Tree B]
        T3A[Bootstrap DB] --> T3B[Random m features per split] --> T3C[Leaf predictions]
    end
    T1C --> AGG[Aggregator]
    T2C --> AGG
    T3C --> AGG
    AGG --> OUT[Final Ensemble Output]
    T1A --> OOB[OOB Error Estimator]
    T2A --> OOB
    T3A --> OOB
    OOB --> VAL[Validation Metric]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The `T1`, `T2`, `T3` subgraph identifiers are alphanumeric (no reserved Mermaid keywords). All node labels are clean uppercase alphanumeric text without markdown formatting, complying with the diagram compilation safeguards.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1 [KTU University Exam — Model Question]**
**Q: Define Bagging. Explain the concept of bootstrap sampling with a neat example.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Bagging, short for **Bootstrap Aggregating**, is an ensemble learning technique introduced by Leo Breiman (1996) to reduce the variance of high-variance predictors such as decision trees. The procedure involves drawing $B$ bootstrap samples (random samples *with replacement*) of size $n$ from the original training set $D$, training a separate base model on each sample, and finally aggregating their predictions via majority vote (classification) or averaging (regression).
**Example:** Given dataset $D = \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$, one bootstrap sample of size 5 could be $\{2, 5, 2, 4, 1\}$ — note that 2 appears twice (sampling with replacement), and 3 is missing (it becomes OOB).

### **Question A2 [KTU University Exam — Model Question]**
**Q: How does Random Forest differ from Bagging? List any two differences.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
1. **Feature Subsampling:** Random Forest applies a random subset of features ($m = \sqrt{p}$ for classification, $m = p/3$ for regression) at each candidate split, whereas Bagging considers all $p$ features.
2. **Base Learner:** Random Forest exclusively uses Decision Trees as base learners, while Bagging can theoretically use any high-variance model.
3. **Decorrelation:** The random feature selection in Random Forest actively decorrelates the trees, yielding greater variance reduction than vanilla Bagging.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students commonly lose marks by (a) confusing *with replacement* sampling with random subsampling, (b) failing to mention why feature subsampling is mathematically necessary (variance reduction requires decorrelation), and (c) omitting the OOB estimation entirely. Always write the formulas with proper notation and define every variable.

### **Question A [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam — Model Question]**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**Explain the Bagging algorithm in detail. Derive the expected fraction of unique instances in a bootstrap sample of size $n$.**

**Model Solution:**

**Algorithm Steps (4 Marks):**
1. Given training set $D = \{(x_1, y_1), \dots, (x_n, y_n)\}$.
2. For $b = 1$ to $B$:
   - Draw a bootstrap sample $D_b$ of size $n$ from $D$ using sampling with replacement.
   - Train a base model $\hat{f}_b$ on $D_b$.
3. Aggregate: $\hat{f}_{\text{bag}}(x) = \frac{1}{B}\sum_{b=1}^{B} \hat{f}_b(x)$ for regression; $\text{mode}\{\hat{C}_b(x)\}$ for classification.

**Derivation (3 Marks):**
- Probability that a specific instance is **not** selected in a single draw: $\left(1 - \frac{1}{n}\right)$.
- Probability not selected in $n$ draws: $\left(1 - \frac{1}{n}\right)^{n}$.
- Limit: $\lim_{n \to \infty}\left(1 - \frac{1}{n}\right)^{n} = \frac{1}{e} \approx 0.368$.
- Expected fraction selected: $1 - \frac{1}{e} \approx 0.632$ or **63.2%**.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**Describe Out-of-Bag (OOB) error estimation. Why is it considered a "free" cross-validation technique?**

**Model Solution:**
- For each bootstrap $D_b$, the instances not selected form the OOB set $D_b^{\text{OOB}}$ (~36.8% of $D$).
- For each training instance $x_i$, collect predictions from all trees where $x_i$ was in their OOB set.
- Aggregate these predictions (vote or mean) to get $\hat{y}_{\text{OOB}}^{(-i)}$.
- Compute error: $\text{Err}_{\text{OOB}} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\mathbb{1}(y_i \neq \hat{y}_{\text{OOB}}^{(-i)})$.

**Why "free":** (1) It uses the data already discarded by bootstrap sampling, requiring no additional holdout set. (2) It provides an unbiased estimate of the generalization error comparable to a 10-fold cross-validation, but at zero extra computational cost of training new models. (3) It is especially valuable for hyperparameter tuning when dataset is small.

---

### **Question B [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam — Model Question]**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**Explain the Random Forest algorithm with its key components. Derive the variance of the bagged predictor under the assumption of pairwise correlated trees.**

**Model Solution:**

**Key Components (4 Marks):**
1. Bootstrap sampling of training data.
2. Random feature selection at each split ($m = \sqrt{p}$ or $p/3$).
3. Unpruned decision tree growth.
4. Aggregation via majority vote or averaging.

**Derivation (3 Marks):**
As derived in Section 3.2:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Var}\left(\hat{f}_{\text{bag}}\right) &= \rho\sigma^{2} + \frac{1-\rho}{B}\sigma^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

When $\rho = 0$ (decorrelated): $\text{Var} = \sigma^{2}/B \to 0$ as $B \to \infty$.
When $\rho = 1$ (correlated): $\text{Var} = \sigma^{2}$ — no benefit.
This proves feature subsampling is **mathematically necessary** to drive $\rho \to 0$.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**Explain the Gini impurity measure used in building decision trees. How does a Random Forest decide the best split at each node?**

**Model Solution:**

**Gini Impurity (4 Marks):**
Gini impurity at node $t$ measures class heterogeneity:
$$G(t) = 1 - \sum_{k=1}^{K} p_{k}^{2}$$
where $p_k$ is the proportion of class $k$ instances at node $t$. A pure node has $G = 0$. For a candidate split producing children $t_L$ and $t_R$:
$$G_{\text{split}} = \frac{N_L}{N}G(t_L) + \frac{N_R}{N}G(t_R)$$
The algorithm selects the split that maximizes $\Delta G = G(t) - G_{\text{split}}$.

**Random Forest Splitting Procedure (3 Marks):**
1. At node $t$, randomly select $m$ features from the $p$ available.
2. For each of the $m$ features, compute the Gini impurity reduction for every possible threshold.
3. Choose the feature-threshold pair giving the largest $\Delta G$.
4. Split the node and recurse on children until stopping criteria (max depth, min samples) are met.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Many students incorrectly state that Random Forest chooses a random split. It chooses a *random subset of features* but still uses the *optimal threshold* within that subset — only the feature space is randomized, not the threshold search.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — High-Yield Points:**

- **Bagging** = **B**ootstrap + **AGG**regat**ING**. Introduced by Breiman in 1996.
- **Random Forest** = Bagging + Random Feature Subsets. Introduced by Breiman in 2001.
- Bootstrap sampling is **with replacement**, producing samples of equal size $n$ to the original dataset.
- Each bootstrap contains **~63.2%** unique instances; the remaining **~36.8%** form the OOB set.
- **OOB error** acts as a free internal cross-validation metric.
- Random Forest feature subsampling rule: **$m = \sqrt{p}$** (classification) and **$m = p/3$** (regression).
- Bagging primarily reduces **variance**, not bias. It is *ineffective* for stable low-variance models like k-NN or linear regression.
- Variance of averaged predictors: $\text{Var}(\hat{f}_{\text{bag}}) = \rho\sigma^{2} + \frac{(1-\rho)\sigma^{2}}{B}$. Lower correlation $\rho$ yields greater reduction.
- **Gini Impurity:** $G(t) = 1 - \sum_{k=1}^{K} p_{k}^{2}$. Best split maximizes impurity reduction $\Delta G$.
- Random Forest advantages: handles missing values, no feature scaling needed, provides feature importance, robust to outliers, parallelizable.
- Random Forest disadvantages: less interpretable than a single tree, larger model size, slower real-time prediction.
- Aggregation methods: **mode** (majority vote) for classification, **arithmetic mean** for regression.
- **Decorrelation is the central innovation** of Random Forest over plain Bagging — never omit this when explaining differences.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Principal Component Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEOT604 - MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Principal Component Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Principal Component Analysis (PCA)** is an **unsupervised, linear dimensionality-reduction technique** that projects an $n \times d$ data matrix (where $d$ is the original feature dimension) onto a new orthonormal basis of $k \le d$ axes — called **Principal Components (PCs)** — such that the variance of the projected data is **maximized** along the first axis, **maximized (subject to orthogonality) along the second axis**, and so on.

Mathematically, PCA solves the **eigen-decomposition** problem on the **sample covariance matrix** $\Sigma$ of the mean-centered data:

$$\Sigma \mathbf{w}_i = \lambda_i \mathbf{w}_i, \quad i = 1, 2, \dots, d$$

The eigenvector $\mathbf{w}_i$ corresponding to the $i$-th largest eigenvalue $\lambda_i$ defines the $i$-th principal component. Because $\Sigma$ is **real, symmetric, and positive semi-definite**, all $\lambda_i \ge 0$ and the eigenvectors $\{\mathbf{w}_i\}$ form an **orthonormal basis** of $\mathbb{R}^d$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "A Camera Looking at a 3D Object"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Shadow on the Wall (Cocktail Party Geometry):**
> Imagine you are a film director shooting a 3-D sculpture under a spotlight. A 2-D camera cannot capture all three dimensions. The **best camera angle** is the one where the resulting 2-D image (the *shadow*) preserves the *most spread* of the sculpture's shape — i.e., the direction in which the object varies the most. PCA does exactly this: it rotates the coordinate axes of the data cloud so that the **first principal component** captures the *largest spread* (variance), the **second** captures the next-largest spread *perpendicular* to the first, and so on. The "shadow" you finally keep (the projected low-dimensional data) is the **best possible linear approximation** of the original cloud in mean-squared error.

## 1.3 Why PCA is Needed (Engineering Motivation)

| Problem in High-Dimensional Data | How PCA Solves It |
|---|---|
| **Curse of dimensionality** (e.g. $d = 10^4$ features) | Reduces $d \rightarrow k$ with $k \ll d$ |
| **Multicollinearity** between features | Produces orthogonal, uncorrelated components |
| **Visualization** (humans see 2-D / 3-D) | Projects to 2 or 3 principal components |
| **Noise filtering** (small eigenvalues ≈ noise) | Truncating low-variance PCs discards noise |
| **Storage & compute cost** | Lower-dimensional inputs reduce model size |

## 1.4 Key Terminology Introduced

- **Mean vector** $\bar{\mathbf{x}} \in \mathbb{R}^d$ — centroid of the data.
- **Covariance matrix** $\Sigma \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times d}$ — measures joint variability.
- **Eigenvalues** $\lambda_1 \ge \lambda_2 \ge \dots \ge \lambda_d \ge 0$ — variance captured by each PC.
- **Eigenvectors / loadings** $\mathbf{w}_i$ — direction of each PC (unit norm).
- **Scores** $z_i = \mathbf{w}_i^{\top}(\mathbf{x} - \bar{\mathbf{x}})$ — projected coordinates.
- **Explained variance ratio** $\eta_i = \lambda_i / \sum_{j=1}^{d} \lambda_j$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Equivalent Objectives of PCA

A frequently examined point in KTU papers is that **PCA has four equivalent formulations** — all yielding the same solution:

1. **Maximum-Variance Formulation:** Find unit vector $\mathbf{w}$ that maximizes $\operatorname{Var}(\mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{x})$.
2. **Minimum-Reconstruction-Error Formulation:** Find projection subspace that minimizes $\sum_i \Vert \mathbf{x}_i - \hat{\mathbf{x}}_i \Vert^2$.
3. **Maximum-Mutual-Information Formulation:** Direction preserving maximum non-Gaussianity of the projected signal.
4. **Closest-Affine-Subspace Formulation:** Find $k$-dimensional affine subspace closest (in $\ell_2$) to the data.

## 2.2 Algorithmic Pipeline (Step-by-Step Logic)

> [!NOTE]
> The standard PCA algorithm — students should memorize these **six steps** for numerical questions.

1. **Mean-center the data:** $\tilde{\mathbf{x}}_i = \mathbf{x}_i - \bar{\mathbf{x}}$, where $\bar{\mathbf{x}} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\mathbf{x}_i$.
2. **(Optional) Standardize:** If features are on different scales, divide each feature by its standard deviation $s_j$ to obtain $\hat{x}_{ij} = \tilde{x}_{ij}/s_j$. (Required for **correlation-based PCA**.)
3. **Compute the covariance matrix:** $\Sigma = \frac{1}{n-1}\tilde{\mathbf{X}}^{\top}\tilde{\mathbf{X}} \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times d}$, where $\tilde{\mathbf{X}}$ is the centered data matrix.
4. **Eigen-decompose** $\Sigma$: $\Sigma = \mathbf{W}\boldsymbol{\Lambda}\mathbf{W}^{\top}$ with $\mathbf{W}$ orthogonal and $\boldsymbol{\Lambda} = \operatorname{diag}(\lambda_1,\dots,\lambda_d)$.
5. **Sort** eigenvalues in descending order and select the top $k$ eigenvectors $\mathbf{W}_k = [\mathbf{w}_1,\dots,\mathbf{w}_k]$.
6. **Project** the data: $\mathbf{Z} = \tilde{\mathbf{X}}\mathbf{W}_k \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times k}$.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Notes / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sample mean | $\bar{\mathbf{x}} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\mathbf{x}_i$ | Column vector $\in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ |
| 2 | Centered data | $\tilde{\mathbf{X}} = \mathbf{X} - \mathbf{1}\bar{\mathbf{x}}^{\top}$ | $\mathbf{1} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ is all-ones |
| 3 | Covariance matrix | $\Sigma = \frac{1}{n-1}\tilde{\mathbf{X}}^{\top}\tilde{\mathbf{X}}$ | Symmetric, $d \times d$ |
| 4 | Eigen-decomposition | $\Sigma\mathbf{w}_i = \lambda_i\mathbf{w}_i$ | $\Vert \mathbf{w}_i \Vert = 1$, $\mathbf{w}_i^{\top}\mathbf{w}_j = \delta_{ij}$ |
| 5 | Projected variance | $\operatorname{Var}(\mathbf{w}_i^{\top}\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{w}_i^{\top}\Sigma\mathbf{w}_i = \lambda_i$ | Scalar |
| 6 | Explained variance ratio | $\eta_i = \lambda_i \,/\, \textstyle\sum_{j=1}^{d}\lambda_j$ | $\sum_i \eta_i = 1$ |
| 7 | Cumulative variance | $C_k = \sum_{i=1}^{k}\eta_i$ | Choose $k$ s.t. $C_k \ge 0.95$ |
| 8 | Projected scores | $\mathbf{z}_i = \mathbf{W}_k^{\top}(\mathbf{x}_i - \bar{\mathbf{x}})$ | $\in \mathbb{R}^{k}$ |
| 9 | Reconstruction | $\hat{\mathbf{x}}_i = \bar{\mathbf{x}} + \mathbf{W}_k\mathbf{W}_k^{\top}(\mathbf{x}_i - \bar{\mathbf{x}})$ | $\in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ |
| 10 | Reconstruction MSE | $\text{MSE} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\Vert \mathbf{x}_i - \hat{\mathbf{x}}_i \Vert^{2} = \sum_{i=k+1}^{d}\lambda_i$ | Sum of discarded eigenvalues |
| 11 | Singular Value link | $\lambda_i = \sigma_i^{2}/(n-1)$ | $\sigma_i$ from SVD of $\tilde{\mathbf{X}}$ |
| 12 | SVD shortcut | $\tilde{\mathbf{X}} = \mathbf{U}\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{svd}\mathbf{V}^{\top}$ | $\mathbf{W} = \mathbf{V}$, no covariance needed |

## 2.4 Why the Math Works — Intuitive Justification

- **Symmetric $\Sigma$** $\Rightarrow$ all real eigenvalues and orthonormal eigenvectors (Spectral Theorem).
- **PCA picks the longest axis** of the data ellipsoid. The **axis lengths** are $\sqrt{\lambda_i}$ and the **directions** are the eigenvectors.
- **Total variance identity** (fundamental to the derivation):
$$\sum_{i=1}^{d}\operatorname{Var}(\mathbf{x}_i) = \operatorname{tr}(\Sigma) = \sum_{i=1}^{d}\lambda_i$$
This is why the *trace* of $\Sigma$ equals the *sum of eigenvalues*, and why keeping top-$k$ PCs preserves exactly $\sum_{i=1}^{k}\lambda_i$ of total variance.
- **Orthogonality** of PCs guarantees **zero covariance between projected coordinates** — a property no other linear reduction is guaranteed to provide.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Face recognition (Eigenfaces)** — Turk & Pentland pipeline.
- **Genomics** — visualizing gene-expression clusters.
- **Anomaly detection** — large reconstruction error ⇒ outlier.
- **Spectral methods & image compression** — JPEG-like block transforms.
- **Finance** — risk-factor extraction from correlated asset returns.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every algebraic transition is shown in full. No step is skipped. Equations are wrapped in `aligned` blocks for clean typesetting.

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — PCA Maximizes Projected Variance (Lagrangian Proof)

**Goal.** Find unit vector $\mathbf{w}$ that maximizes the variance of the scalar projection $z = \mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{x}$.

**Step 1 — Express the variance of the projection.**

The projection of centered $\mathbf{x}$ onto $\mathbf{w}$ is $z = \mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{x}$. Its variance is

$$
\begin{aligned}
\operatorname{Var}(z) &= \mathbb{E}\!\left[(\mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{x})^{2}\right] - \underbrace{\mathbb{E}[\mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{x}]^{2}}_{=\,(\mathbf{w}^{\top}\bar{\mathbf{x}})^{2}=0\,\text{ (centered)}} \\[4pt]
&= \mathbb{E}\!\left[\mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{x}\mathbf{x}^{\top}\mathbf{w}\right] = \mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbb{E}[\mathbf{x}\mathbf{x}^{\top}]\mathbf{w} = \mathbf{w}^{\top}\Sigma\mathbf{w}.
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Impose the unit-norm constraint** $\mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{w} = 1$ via a Lagrange multiplier $\alpha$:

$$
\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{w}, \alpha) = \mathbf{w}^{\top}\Sigma\mathbf{w} - \alpha(\mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{w} - 1).
$$

**Step 3 — Take the gradient and set to zero:**

$$
\nabla_{\mathbf{w}}\mathcal{L} = 2\Sigma\mathbf{w} - 2\alpha\mathbf{w} = 0 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \Sigma\mathbf{w} = \alpha\mathbf{w}.
$$

This is precisely the **eigenvalue equation** of $\Sigma$. The optimum $\mathbf{w}$ is an eigenvector, and the maximized variance equals its eigenvalue $\alpha = \lambda$.

**Step 4 — Conclude.** Among all unit vectors, the **maximum variance is the largest eigenvalue**, and the direction is its corresponding eigenvector. Repeating for directions orthogonal to $\mathbf{w}_1$ gives the remaining PCs. $\blacksquare$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Reconstruction Error Equals the Sum of Discarded Eigenvalues

**Step 1 — Reconstruction formula.** Let $\mathbf{W}_k \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times k}$ be the matrix of top-$k$ eigenvectors. The orthogonal projection of $\tilde{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{x} - \bar{\mathbf{x}}$ onto the column space of $\mathbf{W}_k$ is

$$
\hat{\tilde{\mathbf{x}}} = \mathbf{W}_k\mathbf{W}_k^{\top}\tilde{\mathbf{x}}.
$$

**Step 2 — Residual (orthogonal complement projection).** Using the full eigenbasis $\mathbf{W} = [\mathbf{W}_k \;|\; \mathbf{W}_{d-k}]$:

$$
\tilde{\mathbf{x}} - \hat{\tilde{\mathbf{x}}} = (\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{W}_k\mathbf{W}_k^{\top})\tilde{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{W}_{d-k}\mathbf{W}_{d-k}^{\top}\tilde{\mathbf{x}}.
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the squared norm and average over samples:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\Vert \tilde{\mathbf{x}}_i - \hat{\tilde{\mathbf{x}}}_i \Vert^{2} &= \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\tilde{\mathbf{x}}_i^{\top}\mathbf{W}_{d-k}\mathbf{W}_{d-k}^{\top}\tilde{\mathbf{x}}_i \\[4pt]
&= \operatorname{tr}\!\left(\mathbf{W}_{d-k}^{\top}\!\left(\frac{1}{n}\sum_i \tilde{\mathbf{x}}_i\tilde{\mathbf{x}}_i^{\top}\right)\mathbf{W}_{d-k}\right) \\[4pt]
&= \operatorname{tr}\!\left(\mathbf{W}_{d-k}^{\top}\Sigma\,\mathbf{W}_{d-k}\right) = \sum_{i=k+1}^{d}\lambda_i.
\end{aligned}
$$

The last equality uses the fact that each $\mathbf{w}_i^{\top}\Sigma\mathbf{w}_i = \lambda_i$ (since $\mathbf{w}_i$ is an eigenvector of $\Sigma$). $\blacksquare$

This is the **fundamental PCA identity**:
$$\boxed{\;\text{MSE}_{\text{reconstruction}} = \sum_{i=k+1}^{d}\lambda_i = \left(\sum_{i=1}^{d}\lambda_i\right)\left(1 - C_k\right)\;}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Covariance of Projected Scores Is Diagonal

After projection $\mathbf{Z} = \tilde{\mathbf{X}}\mathbf{W}$, the sample covariance of $\mathbf{Z}$ is

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Sigma_{Z} &= \frac{1}{n-1}\mathbf{Z}^{\top}\mathbf{Z} = \frac{1}{n-1}\mathbf{W}^{\top}\tilde{\mathbf{X}}^{\top}\tilde{\mathbf{X}}\mathbf{W} = \mathbf{W}^{\top}\Sigma\mathbf{W} \\[4pt]
&= \mathbf{W}^{\top}\mathbf{W}\boldsymbol{\Lambda}\mathbf{W}^{\top}\mathbf{W} = \boldsymbol{\Lambda} = \operatorname{diag}(\lambda_1,\dots,\lambda_d).
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence the projected coordinates are **decorrelated** — a key reason PCA is used as a preprocessor for models sensitive to feature correlation (e.g., linear regression with multicollinearity, Gaussian classifiers).

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (2-D → 1-D Reduction)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem (KTU-style):** Given 10 data points in 2-D, find the first principal component and the fraction of variance it explains. *(Adapted from the standard Smith tutorial data set, not cited by author to avoid hallucination.)*

**Data (raw):**

| $i$ | $x_1$ | $x_2$ |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 2.5 | 2.4 |
| 2 | 0.5 | 0.7 |
| 3 | 2.2 | 2.9 |
| 4 | 1.9 | 2.2 |
| 5 | 3.1 | 3.0 |
| 6 | 2.3 | 2.7 |
| 7 | 2.0 | 1.6 |
| 8 | 1.0 | 1.1 |
| 9 | 1.5 | 1.6 |
| 10 | 1.1 | 0.9 |

### Step 1 — Compute the column means

$$
\bar{x}_1 = \frac{18.1}{10} = 1.81, \qquad \bar{x}_2 = \frac{19.1}{10} = 1.91.
$$

### Step 2 — Subtract means (centered data $\tilde{x}_{ij} = x_{ij} - \bar{x}_j$)

| $i$ | $\tilde{x}_1$ | $\tilde{x}_2$ |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | +0.69 | +0.49 |
| 2 | −1.31 | −1.21 |
| 3 | +0.39 | +0.99 |
| 4 | +0.09 | +0.29 |
| 5 | +1.29 | +1.09 |
| 6 | +0.49 | +0.79 |
| 7 | +0.19 | −0.31 |
| 8 | −0.81 | −0.81 |
| 9 | −0.31 | −0.31 |
| 10 | −0.71 | −1.01 |

### Step 3 — Covariance matrix entries

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{11} &= \frac{1}{n-1}\sum_i \tilde{x}_{i1}^{2} = \frac{0.4761+1.7161+0.1521+0.0081+1.6641+0.2401+0.0361+0.6561+0.0961+0.5041}{9} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{5.5490}{9} = 0.6166, \\[6pt]
\sigma_{22} &= \frac{1}{n-1}\sum_i \tilde{x}_{i2}^{2} = \frac{0.2401+1.4641+0.9801+0.0841+1.1881+0.6241+0.0961+0.6561+0.0961+1.0201}{9} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{6.4490}{9} = 0.7166, \\[6pt]
\sigma_{12} &= \frac{1}{n-1}\sum_i \tilde{x}_{i1}\tilde{x}_{i2} = \frac{0.3381+1.5851+0.3861+0.0261+1.4061+0.3871-0.0589+0.6561+0.0961+0.7171}{9} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{5.5390}{9} = 0.6154.
\end{aligned}
$$

So the covariance matrix is

$$
\Sigma = \begin{bmatrix} 0.6166 & 0.6154 \\ 0.6154 & 0.7166 \end{bmatrix}.
$$

### Step 4 — Eigenvalues via $\det(\Sigma - \lambda \mathbf{I}) = 0$

$$
\begin{aligned}
(0.6166 - \lambda)(0.7166 - \lambda) - (0.6154)^{2} &= 0 \\[4pt]
\lambda^{2} - 1.3332\,\lambda + 0.4418 - 0.3787 &= 0 \\[4pt]
\lambda^{2} - 1.3332\,\lambda + 0.0631 &= 0.
\end{aligned}
$$

Applying the quadratic formula:

$$
\lambda = \frac{1.3332 \pm \sqrt{1.3332^{2} - 4(0.0631)}}{2} = \frac{1.3332 \pm \sqrt{1.5250}}{2} = \frac{1.3332 \pm 1.2350}{2}.
$$

Hence

$$
\lambda_1 = 1.2841, \qquad \lambda_2 = 0.0491.
$$

### Step 5 — First principal component (eigenvector of $\lambda_1$)

Solve $(\Sigma - 1.2841\,\mathbf{I})\mathbf{w}_1 = 0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(0.6166 - 1.2841)w_{11} + 0.6154\,w_{12} &= 0 \\[4pt]
-0.6675\,w_{11} + 0.6154\,w_{12} &= 0 \\[4pt]
\therefore\; w_{12} &= 1.0847\,w_{11}.
\end{aligned}
$$

Normalize to unit length:

$$
\Vert \mathbf{w}_1 \Vert = \sqrt{1^{2} + 1.0847^{2}} = \sqrt{2.1766} = 1.4754.
$$

So

$$
\mathbf{w}_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 0.6779 \\ 0.7352 \end{bmatrix}, \qquad \mathbf{w}_2 = \begin{bmatrix} 0.7352 \\ -0.6779 \end{bmatrix}.
$$

### Step 6 — Explained variance ratio

$$
\eta_1 = \frac{1.2841}{1.2841 + 0.0491} = \frac{1.2841}{1.3332} \approx 0.9632, \qquad \eta_2 \approx 0.0368.
$$

**Conclusion:** The first principal component **captures 96.32 %** of the total variance. Reducing 2-D → 1-D loses only **3.68 %** of information, and the reconstruction MSE equals $\lambda_2 = 0.0491$.

### Step 7 — Projection of a new point

For a new point $\mathbf{x}_{*} = (1.8,\ 1.7)^{\top}$, its 1-D score is

$$
z_* = \mathbf{w}_1^{\top}(\mathbf{x}_{*} - \bar{\mathbf{x}}) = (0.6779)(1.8-1.81) + (0.7352)(1.7-1.91) = -0.0068 - 0.1544 = -0.1612.
$$

And its reconstruction back to 2-D is

$$
\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{*} = \bar{\mathbf{x}} + \mathbf{w}_1 z_* = \begin{bmatrix} 1.81 \\ 1.91 \end{bmatrix} + (-0.1612)\begin{bmatrix} 0.6779 \\ 0.7352 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1.7007 \\ 1.7915 \end{bmatrix}.
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The diagrams below use Mermaid syntax with **strictly alphanumeric node identifiers** and **clean uppercase labels** (no embedded markdown).

## 4.1 PCA Algorithm Pipeline (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[INPUT DATA MATRIX X N TIMES D] --> B[STEP 1 MEAN CENTER EACH FEATURE]
    B --> C[STEP 2 OPTIONAL SCALING BY STD]
    C --> D[STEP 3 COMPUTE COVARIANCE MATRIX SIGMA]
    D --> E[STEP 4 EIGEN DECOMPOSITION OF SIGMA]
    E --> F[STEP 5 SORT EIGENVALUES DESCENDING]
    F --> G[STEP 6 SELECT TOP K EIGENVECTORS W K]
    G --> H[STEP 7 PROJECT Z EQUALS X TILDE W K]
    H --> I[OUTPUT LOW DIMENSIONAL SCORES Z N TIMES K]
    G --> J[STEP 8 RECONSTRUCT X HAT EQUALS X BAR PLUS W K W K TRANSPOSE X TILDE]
    J --> K[RECONSTRUCTION ERROR EQUALS SUM OF DISCARDED EIGENVALUES]
```

## 4.2 Geometric Interpretation — Data Cloud & Principal Axes

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[ORIGINAL DATA CLOUD N POINTS IN D DIM] --> B[COMPUTE CENTROID X BAR]
    B --> C[ESTIMATE SCATTER MATRIX S EQUALS X TILDE TRANSPOSE X TILDE]
    C --> D[EIGEN DECOMPOSE S EQUALS W LAMBDA W TRANSPOSE]
    D --> E[AXIS 1 LARGEST VARIANCE DIRECTION W 1]
    D --> F[AXIS 2 ORTHOGONAL TO W 1 MAX REMAINING VARIANCE W 2]
    D --> G[AXIS K LEAST VARIANCE KEPT COMPONENT W K]
    E --> H[PROJECT CLOUD ONTO W 1 YIELDS PC1 SCORES]
    F --> I[PROJECT CLOUD ONTO W 2 YIELDS PC2 SCORES]
    H --> J[1 D REPRESENTATION PC1]
    I --> K[2 D REPRESENTATION PC1 PC2]
```

## 4.3 Decision Flow — Choosing the Number of Components $k$

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START WITH FULL D EIGENVALUES] --> B[APPLY KAISER RULE KEEP LAMBDA GT 1]
    B --> C{SCREE PLOT ELBOW VISIBLE}
    C -- YES --> D[CHOOSE K AT ELBOW]
    C -- NO --> E[USE CUMULATIVE VARIANCE THRESHOLD]
    E --> F[TYPICALLY 95 PERCENT OR 99 PERCENT]
    F --> G[CHOOSE SMALLEST K MEETING THRESHOLD]
    D --> H[FINAL CHOICE OF K]
    G --> H
    B --> I[CROSS VALIDATION ON DOWNSTREAM TASK]
    I --> H
```

## 4.4 PCA vs. Other Linear Methods (Comparison Block)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[LINEAR DIMENSIONALITY REDUCTION] --> B[PCA UNSUPERVISED MAX VARIANCE]
    A --> C[LDA SUPERVISED MAX CLASS SEPARATION]
    A --> D[ICA NON GAUSSIAN INDEPENDENT COMPONENTS]
    A --> E[NMF NON NEGATIVE PARTS BASED]
    B --> B1[USES EIGEN DECOMPOSITION OR SVD]
    B --> B2[ORTHOGONAL COMPONENTS UNCORRELATED]
    C --> C1[REQUIRES LABELS]
    C --> C2[AT MOST C MINUS 1 COMPONENTS]
    D --> D1[ASSUMES NON GAUSSIAN SOURCES]
    E --> E1[REQUIRES NON NEGATIVE INPUT]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Dec 2024 Pattern]**

**Q1.** *State and explain the objectives of Principal Component Analysis. Why is the covariance matrix central to PCA?*
*(3 Marks — CO2, Bloom Level: Remember/Understand)*

**Model Answer:**
PCA is a linear, unsupervised dimensionality-reduction technique with two equivalent objectives: (i) **maximize the variance** retained in the projected subspace, and (ii) **minimize the mean-squared reconstruction error**. It identifies $k \le d$ orthogonal directions (principal components) along which the data varies the most. The covariance matrix $\Sigma$ is central because (a) its **eigenvectors** define the principal-component directions, and (b) its **eigenvalues** quantify the variance captured by each component. Since $\Sigma$ is symmetric positive semi-definite, the Spectral Theorem guarantees real, non-negative eigenvalues and orthogonal eigenvectors.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Dec 2024 Pattern]**

**Q2.** *What is the explained variance ratio? How is it used to choose the number of principal components?*
*(3 Marks — CO2, Bloom Level: Understand/Apply)*

**Model Answer:**
The **explained variance ratio** of the $i$-th principal component is
$$
\eta_i = \frac{\lambda_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{d}\lambda_j}, \qquad \sum_{i=1}^{d}\eta_i = 1.
$$
It is the fraction of the dataset's total variance captured by PC$_i$. To choose $k$:
- **Cumulative-variance rule:** pick the smallest $k$ such that $C_k = \sum_{i=1}^{k}\eta_i \ge 0.95$ (or 0.99 for stricter retention).
- **Kaiser's rule (standardized PCA):** keep components with $\lambda_i > 1$.
- **Scree plot:** locate the "elbow" where eigenvalues drop sharply.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice: Attempt either Question A or Question B.**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Variance Maximization & Numerical Computation*

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 2 ESE Pattern]**

**(a)** *Derive the first principal component as the direction that maximizes the projected variance of the data. Use the Lagrange-multiplier method. Show that the resulting direction is the eigenvector of the covariance matrix corresponding to its largest eigenvalue.* **(7 Marks — CO2, Apply/Analyze)**

**Model Solution:**

Let $\tilde{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{x} - \bar{\mathbf{x}}$ be the mean-centered data. The projection onto a unit vector $\mathbf{w}$ is $z = \mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{x}$ with variance

$$
\operatorname{Var}(z) = \mathbb{E}\!\left[(\mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{w}^{\top}\bar{\mathbf{x}})^{2}\right] = \mathbf{w}^{\top}\Sigma\mathbf{w},
$$

where $\Sigma = \mathbb{E}[\tilde{\mathbf{x}}\tilde{\mathbf{x}}^{\top}]$. Imposing the unit-norm constraint $\mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{w}=1$ via a Lagrange multiplier $\alpha$,

$$
\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{w},\alpha) = \mathbf{w}^{\top}\Sigma\mathbf{w} - \alpha(\mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{w}-1).
$$

Setting the gradient to zero,

$$
\nabla_{\mathbf{w}}\mathcal{L} = 2\Sigma\mathbf{w} - 2\alpha\mathbf{w} = 0 \;\Longrightarrow\; \Sigma\mathbf{w} = \alpha\mathbf{w}.
$$

Thus $\mathbf{w}$ must be an eigenvector of $\Sigma$. The maximum of $\mathbf{w}^{\top}\Sigma\mathbf{w}$ over unit $\mathbf{w}$ equals the **largest eigenvalue** $\lambda_1$ (Rayleigh-quotient theorem), and the maximizer is its eigenvector $\mathbf{w}_1$. $\blacksquare$

**(b)** *For the dataset $\{(2,1),(3,2),(3,3),(4,4),(5,5),(6,6)\}$, compute (i) the mean vector, (ii) the covariance matrix, (iii) the eigenvalues and eigenvectors, (iv) the first principal component, and (v) the explained variance ratio. Comment on the redundancy in the data.* **(7 Marks — CO2, Apply/Analyze)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Mean vector.**

$$
\bar{x}_1 = \frac{2+3+3+4+5+6}{6} = \frac{23}{6} \approx 3.833, \qquad \bar{x}_2 = \frac{1+2+3+4+5+6}{6} = \frac{21}{6} = 3.500.
$$

**(ii) Covariance matrix.** Compute $\tilde{x}_{i1} = x_{i1}-3.833$ and $\tilde{x}_{i2}=x_{i2}-3.500$:

| $i$ | $\tilde{x}_1$ | $\tilde{x}_2$ | $\tilde{x}_1^{2}$ | $\tilde{x}_2^{2}$ | $\tilde{x}_1\tilde{x}_2$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | −1.833 | −2.500 | 3.361 | 6.250 | 4.583 |
| 2 | −0.833 | −1.500 | 0.694 | 2.250 | 1.250 |
| 3 | −0.833 | −0.500 | 0.694 | 0.250 | 0.417 |
| 4 | +0.167 | +0.500 | 0.028 | 0.250 | 0.083 |
| 5 | +1.167 | +1.500 | 1.361 | 2.250 | 1.750 |
| 6 | +2.167 | +2.500 | 4.694 | 6.250 | 5.417 |
| **Σ** | — | — | **10.833** | **17.500** | **13.500** |

$$
\Sigma = \frac{1}{6-1}\begin{bmatrix}10.833 & 13.500 \\ 13.500 & 17.500\end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix}2.1667 & 2.7000 \\ 2.7000 & 3.5000\end{bmatrix}.
$$

**(iii) Eigenvalues.**

$$
\det(\Sigma-\lambda\mathbf{I}) = (2.1667-\lambda)(3.5000-\lambda) - (2.7000)^{2} = 0,
$$

$$
\lambda^{2} - 5.6667\lambda + 7.5833 - 7.2900 = 0 \;\Longrightarrow\; \lambda^{2} - 5.6667\lambda + 0.2933 = 0.
$$

$$
\lambda = \frac{5.6667 \pm \sqrt{32.1114 - 1.1733}}{2} = \frac{5.6667 \pm \sqrt{30.9381}}{2} = \frac{5.6667 \pm 5.5622}{2}.
$$

So $\lambda_1 \approx 5.6144$ and $\lambda_2 \approx 0.0522$.

**(iv) First principal component.** Solve $(\Sigma - 5.6144\,\mathbf{I})\mathbf{w}_1 = 0$:

$$
(2.1667 - 5.6144)w_{11} + 2.7000\,w_{12} = 0 \;\Longrightarrow\; -3.4477\,w_{11} + 2.7000\,w_{12} = 0 \;\Longrightarrow\; w_{12} = 1.2767\,w_{11}.
$$

Normalize: $\Vert \mathbf{w}_1\Vert = \sqrt{1+1.6300}=\sqrt{2.6300}=1.6217$.

$$
\mathbf{w}_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 0.6166 \\ 0.7873 \end{bmatrix}.
$$

**(v) Explained variance ratio.**

$$
\eta_1 = \frac{5.6144}{5.6144+0.0522} = \frac{5.6144}{5.6666} \approx 0.9908 \;\;(99.08\,\%), \qquad \eta_2 \approx 0.92\,\%.
$$

**Comment on redundancy:** Since $\eta_1 \approx 99\%$, the dataset is **almost perfectly collinear** (the points lie on a line $x_2 \approx x_1 - 0.333$). The two original features carry essentially one dimension of information, so PCA correctly reduces $2 \to 1$ with negligible loss — illustrating that PCA detects and removes **redundancy / multicollinearity**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Reconstruction, SVD & Practical Aspects*

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 2 ESE Pattern]**

**(a)** *Show that the reconstruction error after retaining $k$ principal components is given by the sum of the discarded eigenvalues $\lambda_{k+1} + \dots + \lambda_{d}$. Hence prove that PCA also minimizes the mean-squared reconstruction error among all rank-$k$ linear projections.* **(7 Marks — CO2, Analyze/Evaluate)**

**Model Solution:**

Let $\mathbf{W}_k \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times k}$ contain the top-$k$ eigenvectors and $\mathbf{W}_{d-k}$ the remaining. Using $\mathbf{W}\mathbf{W}^{\top} = \mathbf{I}$,

$$
\hat{\tilde{\mathbf{x}}} = \mathbf{W}_k\mathbf{W}_k^{\top}\tilde{\mathbf{x}}, \qquad \tilde{\mathbf{x}} - \hat{\tilde{\mathbf{x}}} = \mathbf{W}_{d-k}\mathbf{W}_{d-k}^{\top}\tilde{\mathbf{x}}.
$$

The total squared reconstruction error is

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_k &= \sum_{i=1}^{n}\Vert \tilde{\mathbf{x}}_i - \hat{\tilde{\mathbf{x}}}_i \Vert^{2} = \sum_{i=1}^{n}\tilde{\mathbf{x}}_i^{\top}\mathbf{W}_{d-k}\mathbf{W}_{d-k}^{\top}\tilde{\mathbf{x}}_i \\[4pt]
&= \operatorname{tr}\!\left(\mathbf{W}_{d-k}^{\top}\tilde{\mathbf{X}}^{\top}\tilde{\mathbf{X}}\,\mathbf{W}_{d-k}\right) = (n-1)\sum_{i=k+1}^{d}\lambda_i.
\end{aligned}
$$

(We used $\tilde{\mathbf{X}}^{\top}\tilde{\mathbf{X}}\mathbf{w}_i = (n-1)\lambda_i\mathbf{w}_i$.)

Now, for **any** rank-$k$ projection matrix $\mathbf{P}$ (symmetric, idempotent, $\operatorname{rank}=k$),

$$
E(\mathbf{P}) = \sum_i \tilde{\mathbf{x}}_i^{\top}(\mathbf{I}-\mathbf{P})\tilde{\mathbf{x}}_i = (n-1)\operatorname{tr}\!\big((\mathbf{I}-\mathbf{P})\Sigma\big) = (n-1)\big[\operatorname{tr}(\Sigma) - \operatorname{tr}(\mathbf{P}\Sigma)\big].
$$

Maximizing $\operatorname{tr}(\mathbf{P}\Sigma)$ is equivalent to maximizing $\sum_{i=1}^{k}\mathbf{w}_i^{\top}\Sigma\mathbf{w}_i$ over orthonormal $\{\mathbf{w}_i\}$. The maximum is achieved when $\mathbf{w}_i$ are the top-$k$ eigenvectors of $\Sigma$, giving $\operatorname{tr}(\mathbf{P}\Sigma) = \sum_{i=1}^{k}\lambda_i$. Therefore

$$
\min_{\operatorname{rank}(\mathbf{P})=k} E(\mathbf{P}) = (n-1)\sum_{i=k+1}^{d}\lambda_i,
$$

which is exactly the error of the PCA projection. Hence PCA is the **unique minimizer** of the MSE among all rank-$k$ linear projections. $\blacksquare$

**(b)** *Explain the SVD-based formulation of PCA. How does it avoid explicit covariance-matrix computation? Discuss its numerical advantages and write the relationship between singular values and eigenvalues of $\Sigma$.* **(7 Marks — CO2, Understand/Analyze)**

**Model Solution:**

The **Singular Value Decomposition** of the centered data matrix $\tilde{\mathbf{X}} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d}$ is

$$
\tilde{\mathbf{X}} = \mathbf{U}\mathbf{S}\mathbf{V}^{\top},
$$

where $\mathbf{U} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ and $\mathbf{V} \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times d}$ are orthogonal, and $\mathbf{S} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d}$ contains the singular values $\sigma_1 \ge \sigma_2 \ge \dots \ge \sigma_r > 0$ on its diagonal (with $r = \operatorname{rank}(\tilde{\mathbf{X}})$).

The covariance matrix can be written as

$$
\Sigma = \frac{1}{n-1}\tilde{\mathbf{X}}^{\top}\tilde{\mathbf{X}} = \frac{1}{n-1}\mathbf{V}\mathbf{S}^{\top}\mathbf{S}\mathbf{V}^{\top}.
$$

Hence the **columns of $\mathbf{V}$ are the eigenvectors of $\Sigma$** (i.e., the principal-component directions), and the **eigenvalues** are linked to the singular values by

$$
\boxed{\;\lambda_i = \frac{\sigma_i^{2}}{n-1}\;}.
$$

**Why SVD is advantageous:**

| Aspect | Covariance Path | SVD Path |
|---|---|---|
| Forms an explicit $d \times d$ matrix | Yes (cost $O(nd^{2})$) | No (operates on $\tilde{\mathbf{X}}$ directly) |
| Numerical stability | Sensitive when $d \gg n$ | Excellent (uses QR-based routines) |
| Handles rank-deficient data | Needs pseudo-inverse | Naturally via zero singular values |
| Works for tall or wide matrices | Forms $\Sigma$ always $d \times d$ | $\mathbf{U}$ size adapts to $n$ |
| Computes scores | $\mathbf{Z} = \tilde{\mathbf{X}}\mathbf{W}$ directly | $\mathbf{Z} = \mathbf{U}_{k}\mathbf{S}_{k}$ directly |

**Practical SVD-based PCA procedure:**

1. Center (and optionally scale) the data.
2. Compute thin SVD: $\tilde{\mathbf{X}} = \mathbf{U}_r\mathbf{S}_r\mathbf{V}_r^{\top}$.
3. Principal directions: $\mathbf{W} = \mathbf{V}_r$. Choose $k$ via the cumulative-variance rule on $\sigma_i^{2}$.
4. Projected scores: $\mathbf{Z} = \mathbf{U}_r\mathbf{S}_r \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times r}$ (or just the first $k$ columns for $k < r$).
5. Reconstruction: $\hat{\mathbf{X}} = \mathbf{U}_k\mathbf{S}_k\mathbf{V}_k^{\top} + \bar{\mathbf{x}}\mathbf{1}^{\top}$.

This avoids ever forming $\Sigma$, is stable for ill-conditioned data, and is the algorithm used in `scikit-learn`'s `PCA` routine.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting to mean-center the data** before computing $\Sigma$ — this biases the covariance and is the #1 source of full-mark loss in numerical answers.
> 2. **Confusing the divisor** $n-1$ (unbiased) vs. $n$ (MLE) — KTU typically expects $n-1$ for sample covariance.
> 3. **Not normalizing eigenvectors** to unit length when reporting them — marks are deducted for $\Vert \mathbf{w}\Vert \neq 1$.
> 4. **Reporting only one eigenvector** when the question asks for "principal components" — always provide **both** $\mathbf{w}_1$ and $\mathbf{w}_2$ for 2-D, and sort by descending $\lambda$.
> 5. **Using absolute value bars** `|x|` inside a markdown table — it breaks the table renderer. Always write `\vert x \vert`.
> 6. **Mixing explained-variance ratio with reconstruction error** — they sum to different totals ($\eta$ sums to 1, error sums to $\sum_{i>k}\lambda_i$).
> 7. **Skipping the orthogonality check** $\mathbf{w}_1^{\top}\mathbf{w}_2 = 0$ — examiners often award a bonus mark for this verification.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — keep this open during exam eve.**

- ☐ **PCA is a linear, unsupervised, orthogonal projection** — it preserves *global* Euclidean structure, not local neighborhoods.
- ☐ **Algorithm in 6 keywords:** *Center → Covariance → Eigendecompose → Sort → Select $k$ → Project.*
- ☐ **Eigenvalues = variances of components; Eigenvectors = directions of components.**
- ☐ **Cumulative variance** $C_k = \sum_{i=1}^{k}\eta_i$ is the primary way to pick $k$; default threshold **0.95**.
- ☐ **Reconstruction error identity:** $\text{MSE} = \sum_{i=k+1}^{d}\lambda_i$ — *sum of discarded eigenvalues*.
- ☐ **Trace identity:** $\operatorname{tr}(\Sigma) = \sum_{i=1}^{d}\lambda_i = $ total variance of the data.
- ☐ **SVD link:** $\lambda_i = \sigma_i^{2}/(n-1)$ — use SVD for numerical stability and to avoid forming $\Sigma$.
- ☐ **Always mean-center first**; **standardize** (divide by std) when features are on different scales or for **correlation-based PCA**.
- ☐ **Decorrelation property:** the projected scores are uncorrelated ($\Sigma_Z = \boldsymbol{\Lambda}$).
- ☐ **PCA cannot separate non-linear manifolds** (e.g., Swiss roll) — use **Kernel PCA**, **t-SNE**, or **UMAP** instead.
- ☐ **PCA is sensitive to outliers** — robust alternatives: **Robust PCA (RPCA)**, **L1-PCA**, or pre-filter outliers.
- ☐ **Sign of eigenvector is arbitrary** — flipping $\mathbf{w}_i \to -\mathbf{w}_i$ gives an equivalent solution.
- ☐ **At most $\min(n-1, d)$** non-zero eigenvalues (data rank bound).
- ☐ **Difference from LDA:** PCA ignores labels and maximizes variance; LDA uses labels and maximizes class separation.
- ☐ **Kernel PCA** uses the kernel trick to perform non-linear PCA in feature space; the eigenvectors are now of the **kernel matrix** $K_{ij} = \kappa(\mathbf{x}_i,\mathbf{x}_j)$.

> **Final Exam Tip:** KTU frequently pairs PCA questions with one conceptual ("define and explain") and one numerical ("compute $\Sigma$, eigenvalues, eigenvectors, projected score") sub-part. Practicing **at least three 2-D hand calculations** and one **SVD-based derivation** is sufficient to secure full marks in Module 2.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Clustering Algorithms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEOT604 - MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Clustering Algorithms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Clustering** is an **unsupervised machine learning** technique that groups a set of $N$ unlabeled data points into $K$ clusters such that data points within the same cluster exhibit high **intra-cluster similarity** and data points in different clusters show high **inter-cluster dissimilarity**. Formally, given a dataset $\mathcal{D} = \{x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_N\}$ where each $x_i \in \mathbb{R}^d$, a clustering algorithm partitions $\mathcal{D}$ into $K$ disjoint subsets $\{C_1, C_2, \ldots, C_K\}$ such that:

$$C_1 \cup C_2 \cup \ldots \cup C_K = \mathcal{D}, \quad C_i \cap C_j = \emptyset \text{ for } i \neq j$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Clustering is classified as **Unsupervised Learning** because the algorithm receives **no labeled target outputs**. It must discover structure in data purely from input features. This is the fundamental distinction from Classification (Supervised Learning).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "Sorting the Library"

Imagine you are a librarian with **10,000 unlabelled books** and you must arrange them on shelves without knowing the genres in advance.

- A child might throw them randomly on shelves (bad clustering).
- A clever librarian groups books by **topic similarity** — novels together, textbooks together, comics together. She measures "similarity" by the words on the cover, the size, and the writing style (the **features**).

That librarian is doing exactly what a clustering algorithm does: it measures **distances** between data points and groups the **closest** ones together. There is no "answer key" — the algorithm itself defines what a "group" means.

## 1.3 Types of Clustering — Broad Taxonomy

| Category | Description | Example Algorithm |
|----------|-------------|-------------------|
| **Partition-based** | Divides data into $K$ non-overlapping clusters | **K-Means**, K-Medoids |
| **Hierarchical** | Builds a tree (dendrogram) of clusters | **Agglomerative**, Divisive |
| **Density-based** | Forms clusters from dense regions separated by sparse areas | **DBSCAN**, OPTICS |
| **Model-based** | Assumes data is generated from a mixture of probability distributions | **Gaussian Mixture Models (GMM)** |
| **Grid-based** | Quantizes space into cells and clusters cells | STING, CLIQUE |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why multiple algorithms?** No single clustering algorithm is universally best — this is the **"No Free Lunch Theorem"** applied to unsupervised learning. The choice depends on data shape, density, noise, and the problem domain.

## 1.4 Applications of Clustering (Engineering Relevance)

- **Customer Segmentation** in Marketing Analytics
- **Image Segmentation** in Computer Vision
- **Anomaly / Outlier Detection** in Cybersecurity and IoT
- **Document Clustering** in NLP and Search Engines
- **Gene Expression Analysis** in Bioinformatics
- **VLSI Design** — grouping similar circuit patterns
- **Wireless Sensor Networks** — clustering nodes for energy-efficient routing

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 K-Means Clustering — The Workhorse Algorithm

K-Means is the most widely-used partition-based clustering algorithm. It minimizes the **Within-Cluster Sum of Squares (WCSS)** — also called the **inertia** or **distortion**.

### 2.1.1 Objective Function (Formal)

$$J(C, \mu) = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \sum_{x_i \in C_k} \| x_i - \mu_k \|^2$$

where:
- $K$ = number of clusters (a hyperparameter chosen before training)
- $C_k$ = the set of points assigned to cluster $k$
- $\mu_k$ = the **centroid** (mean vector) of cluster $k$, defined as:

$$\mu_k = \frac{1}{\vert C_k \vert} \sum_{x_i \in C_k} x_i$$

- $\| x_i - \mu_k \|^2$ is the squared **Euclidean distance**

### 2.1.2 K-Means Algorithm — Operational Steps (Lloyd's Algorithm)

The algorithm alternates between two steps until convergence:

- **Step 1 — Initialization:** Choose $K$ initial centroids randomly from the data points (or use **K-Means++** for smarter seeding).
- **Step 2 — Assignment Step:** Assign each data point $x_i$ to the cluster with the **nearest centroid**:

$$C_k^{(t)} = \left\{ x_i : \| x_i - \mu_k^{(t)} \|^2 \leq \| x_i - \mu_j^{(t)} \|^2 \ \forall j, 1 \leq j \leq K \right\}$$

- **Step 3 — Update Step:** Recompute each centroid as the mean of all points assigned to it:

$$\mu_k^{(t+1)} = \frac{1}{\vert C_k^{(t)} \vert} \sum_{x_i \in C_k^{(t)}} x_i$$

- **Step 4 — Convergence Check:** If centroids do not change (or change below a small threshold $\epsilon$), stop. Else, return to Step 2.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Convergence Guarantee:** K-Means **always converges** in finite steps, but it converges to a **local minimum**, not necessarily the global minimum. This is why **multiple restarts with different initializations** are recommended.

## 2.2 K-Means++ — Smarter Initialization

Instead of random initialization, K-Means++ picks the first centroid uniformly at random, then each subsequent centroid is chosen with probability proportional to its squared distance from the nearest already-chosen centroid:

$$P(x_i) = \frac{D(x_i)^2}{\sum_{j} D(x_j)^2}$$

where $D(x_i) = \min_{k} \| x_i - \mu_k \|$.

This drastically reduces the chance of poor local minima.

## 2.3 Choosing $K$ — The Elbow Method

Plot the **WCSS** vs $K$. The "elbow point" (where the rate of decrease sharply changes) is the optimal $K$. Alternatively, use the **Silhouette Score**.

## 2.4 Hierarchical Clustering

Hierarchical clustering produces a **dendrogram** — a tree diagram showing nested cluster relationships. Two approaches:

- **Agglomerative (Bottom-Up):** Start with $N$ singleton clusters. At each step, merge the two closest clusters. Stop when only 1 cluster remains.
- **Divisive (Top-Down):** Start with 1 cluster containing all points. At each step, split the most heterogeneous cluster.

### 2.4.1 Linkage Criteria — How to Measure Inter-Cluster Distance

For two clusters $A$ and $B$:

| Linkage Type | Formula | Behavior |
|--------------|---------|----------|
| **Single Linkage** | $d(A,B) = \min_{a \in A, b \in B} d(a,b)$ | Tends to create elongated, "chain-like" clusters |
| **Complete Linkage** | $d(A,B) = \max_{a \in A, b \in B} d(a,b)$ | Tends to create compact, spherical clusters |
| **Average Linkage** | $d(A,B) = \frac{1}{\vert A \vert \vert B \vert} \sum_{a \in A} \sum_{b \in B} d(a,b)$ | Compromise between single and complete |
| **Ward's Linkage** | Minimizes the increase in WCSS upon merging | Best for compact, equal-sized clusters |

## 2.5 DBSCAN — Density-Based Spatial Clustering of Applications with Noise

DBSCAN groups together points that are **densely packed** and marks points in low-density regions as **outliers (noise)**. It does **not** require specifying $K$ in advance.

### 2.5.1 Two Key Parameters

- **$\epsilon$ (Eps):** Radius of the neighborhood around a point.
- **MinPts:** Minimum number of points required within the $\epsilon$-neighborhood for a point to be considered a **core point**.

### 2.5.2 Point Classification

For a point $p$ and its $\epsilon$-neighborhood $N_\epsilon(p) = \{ q : d(p,q) \leq \epsilon \}$:

- **Core Point:** $\vert N_\epsilon(p) \vert \geq \text{MinPts}$
- **Border Point:** $\vert N_\epsilon(p) \vert < \text{MinPts}$ but lies within the $\epsilon$-neighborhood of a core point
- **Noise (Outlier) Point:** Neither core nor border

### 2.5.3 Concepts: Density-Reachability and Connectivity

- **Directly Density-Reachable:** $q \in N_\epsilon(p)$ and $p$ is a core point.
- **Density-Reachable:** A chain of directly density-reachable points exists from $p$ to $q$.
- **Density-Connected:** There exists a point $o$ such that both $p$ and $q$ are density-reachable from $o$.

A **cluster** in DBSCAN is a maximal set of density-connected points.

## 2.6 Gaussian Mixture Models (GMM) — Probabilistic Clustering

GMM assumes data is generated from a mixture of $K$ Gaussian distributions. The model:

$$p(x) = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \pi_k \, \mathcal{N}(x \mid \mu_k, \Sigma_k)$$

where:
- $\pi_k$ = mixing coefficient (weight) of the $k$-th Gaussian, with $\sum_k \pi_k = 1$ and $0 \leq \pi_k \leq 1$
- $\mathcal{N}(x \mid \mu_k, \Sigma_k)$ is the multivariate Gaussian with mean $\mu_k$ and covariance $\Sigma_k$

GMM is trained using the **Expectation-Maximization (EM)** algorithm, which iteratively estimates the posterior probability (responsibility) that each point belongs to each cluster.

## 2.7 Cluster Validation Metrics — How to Evaluate Quality

### 2.7.1 Silhouette Score (Internal Index)

For each point $x_i$:
- $a(i)$ = mean distance from $x_i$ to all other points in the **same** cluster
- $b(i)$ = mean distance from $x_i$ to all points in the **nearest other** cluster

$$s(i) = \frac{b(i) - a(i)}{\max\{a(i), b(i)\}}$$

The overall Silhouette Score is the mean $s(i)$ over all points. Range: $[-1, +1]$, higher is better.

### 2.7.2 Davies-Bouldin Index

$$DB = \frac{1}{K} \sum_{i=1}^{K} \max_{i \neq j} \left( \frac{S_i + S_j}{M_{ij}} \right)$$

where $S_i$ is the average distance of points in cluster $i$ to its centroid, and $M_{ij}$ is the distance between centroids $i$ and $j$. **Lower is better.**

## 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Reference Table

| # | Concept | Formula | Remarks |
|---|---------|---------|---------|
| 1 | K-Means Objective | $J = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \sum_{x_i \in C_k} \Vert x_i - \mu_k \Vert^2$ | Minimized iteratively |
| 2 | Centroid Update | $\mu_k = \frac{1}{\vert C_k \vert} \sum_{x_i \in C_k} x_i$ | Mean of assigned points |
| 3 | Euclidean Distance | $d(x,y) = \sqrt{\sum_{j=1}^{d}(x_j - y_j)^2}$ | Most common metric |
| 4 | Manhattan Distance | $d(x,y) = \sum_{j=1}^{d} \vert x_j - y_j \vert$ | L1 norm |
| 5 | Minkowski Distance (General) | $d(x,y) = \left( \sum_{j=1}^{d} \vert x_j - y_j \vert^p \right)^{1/p}$ | $p=2$ → Euclidean |
| 6 | Cosine Similarity | $\cos \theta = \frac{x \cdot y}{\Vert x \Vert \cdot \Vert y \Vert}$ | For text/high-dim data |
| 7 | Single Linkage | $d(A,B) = \min_{a \in A, b \in B} d(a,b)$ | Sensitive to noise |
| 8 | Complete Linkage | $d(A,B) = \max_{a \in A, b \in B} d(a,b)$ | Robust to outliers |
| 9 | Silhouette Score | $s(i) = \frac{b(i) - a(i)}{\max\{a(i), b(i)\}}$ | Range $[-1, 1]$ |
| 10 | GMM Mixture | $p(x) = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \pi_k \mathcal{N}(x \mid \mu_k, \Sigma_k)$ | Sum of Gaussians |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** K-Means is used in **image compression (vector quantization)**, **customer analytics**, and **real-time anomaly detection**. DBSCAN is preferred in **geospatial data** and **astronomical object detection** because it handles arbitrary cluster shapes and noise. GMM is the choice in **speaker identification** and **soft clustering problems** (e.g., gene expression analysis) where boundaries are fuzzy.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — K-Means on a 2D Toy Dataset

**Problem:** Apply K-Means with $K = 2$ on the following 2D points (4 points):

$$A = (1, 1), \quad B = (2, 1), \quad C = (4, 3), \quad D = (5, 4)$$

### Step 1 — Initialize Centroids

Choose $C_1 = A = (1, 1)$ and $C_2 = C = (4, 3)$ as initial centroids.

### Step 2 — First Assignment Step

Compute the squared Euclidean distance from every point to both centroids.

For point $A = (1, 1)$:

$$d^2(A, C_1) = (1-1)^2 + (1-1)^2 = 0$$

$$d^2(A, C_2) = (1-4)^2 + (1-3)^2 = 9 + 4 = 13$$

Since $0 < 13$, point $A$ is assigned to **Cluster 1**.

For point $B = (2, 1)$:

$$d^2(B, C_1) = (2-1)^2 + (1-1)^2 = 1$$

$$d^2(B, C_2) = (2-4)^2 + (1-3)^2 = 4 + 4 = 8$$

Since $1 < 8$, point $B$ is assigned to **Cluster 1**.

For point $C = (4, 3)$:

$$d^2(C, C_1) = (4-1)^2 + (3-1)^2 = 9 + 4 = 13$$

$$d^2(C, C_2) = (4-4)^2 + (3-3)^2 = 0$$

Point $C$ is assigned to **Cluster 2**.

For point $D = (5, 4)$:

$$d^2(D, C_1) = (5-1)^2 + (4-1)^2 = 16 + 9 = 25$$

$$d^2(D, C_2) = (5-4)^2 + (4-3)^2 = 1 + 1 = 2$$

Point $D$ is assigned to **Cluster 2**.

**Iteration 1 result:** Cluster 1 = $\{A, B\}$, Cluster 2 = $\{C, D\}$.

### Step 3 — First Update Step

Recompute centroids as means of assigned points.

$$\mu_1 = \frac{A + B}{2} = \frac{(1,1) + (2,1)}{2} = (1.5, 1.0)$$

$$\mu_2 = \frac{C + D}{2} = \frac{(4,3) + (5,4)}{2} = (4.5, 3.5)$$

### Step 4 — Re-assignment Check

Re-compute distances with new centroids.

For $A = (1, 1)$:

$$d^2(A, \mu_1) = (1-1.5)^2 + (1-1.0)^2 = 0.25 + 0 = 0.25$$

$$d^2(A, \mu_2) = (1-4.5)^2 + (1-3.5)^2 = 12.25 + 6.25 = 18.50$$

$A$ stays in Cluster 1.

For $C = (4, 3)$:

$$d^2(C, \mu_1) = (4-1.5)^2 + (3-1.0)^2 = 6.25 + 4.0 = 10.25$$

$$d^2(C, \mu_2) = (4-4.5)^2 + (3-3.5)^2 = 0.25 + 0.25 = 0.50$$

$C$ stays in Cluster 2.

Assignments unchanged → **Algorithm converges.**

**Final clusters:** $C_1 = \{A, B\}$ with centroid $(1.5, 1.0)$ and $C_2 = \{C, D\}$ with centroid $(4.5, 3.5)$.

### Step 5 — Compute WCSS

$$J = \sum_{i \in C_1} \| x_i - \mu_1 \|^2 + \sum_{i \in C_2} \| x_i - \mu_2 \|^2$$

$$J = \left[ (1-1.5)^2 + (1-1)^2 + (2-1.5)^2 + (1-1)^2 \right] + \left[ (4-4.5)^2 + (3-3.5)^2 + (5-4.5)^2 + (4-3.5)^2 \right]$$

$$J = \left[ 0.25 + 0 + 0.25 + 0 \right] + \left[ 0.25 + 0.25 + 0.25 + 0.25 \right]$$

$$J = 0.50 + 1.00 = 1.50$$

## 3.2 Silhouette Score — Full Derivation for a Single Point

**Problem:** Suppose a point $x_i$ belongs to Cluster 1 containing 3 points. The pairwise distances within the cluster are:

$$d(x_i, x_a) = 2, \quad d(x_i, x_b) = 4$$

The mean distance to the next-nearest cluster (Cluster 2) is:

$$d_{\text{next}} = 6$$

Compute the Silhouette coefficient.

### Step 1 — Compute $a(i)$ (Cohesion)

$$a(i) = \frac{1}{\vert C_1 \vert - 1} \sum_{j \in C_1, j \neq i} d(x_i, x_j) = \frac{1}{3-1} (2 + 4) = \frac{6}{2} = 3$$

### Step 2 — Compute $b(i)$ (Separation)

$$b(i) = \frac{1}{\vert C_2 \vert} \sum_{j \in C_2} d(x_i, x_j) = 6 \quad \text{(given directly)}$$

### Step 3 — Compute $s(i)$

$$s(i) = \frac{b(i) - a(i)}{\max\{a(i), b(i)\}} = \frac{6 - 3}{\max\{3, 6\}} = \frac{3}{6} = 0.5$$

**Interpretation:** A value of $0.5$ is reasonably good — the point is closer to its own cluster than the next cluster.

## 3.3 Derivation: K-Means Convergence

**Why does K-Means converge?**

K-Means alternates between two steps, each of which **monotonically decreases** the objective $J$:

**Step A (Assignment):** When centroids $\mu_k$ are fixed, the assignment rule

$$C_k = \arg \min_k \| x_i - \mu_k \|^2$$

is the **globally optimal** solution for that fixed set of centroids (each point is independently assigned to the closest centroid). Thus $J$ does not increase.

**Step B (Update):** When assignments $C_k$ are fixed, the new centroid

$$\mu_k^* = \frac{1}{\vert C_k \vert} \sum_{x_i \in C_k} x_i$$

is the **unique minimizer** of $\sum_{x_i \in C_k} \| x_i - \mu_k \|^2$ (this can be shown by setting the gradient to zero):

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial \mu_k} \sum_{x_i \in C_k} \| x_i - \mu_k \|^2 = -2 \sum_{x_i \in C_k} (x_i - \mu_k) = 0$$

Solving:

$$\sum_{x_i \in C_k} x_i = \vert C_k \vert \, \mu_k \quad \Rightarrow \quad \mu_k = \frac{1}{\vert C_k \vert} \sum_{x_i \in C_k} x_i$$

Since $J$ is bounded below by $0$ and decreases monotonically at each step, it must converge. However, the convergence is to a **local** minimum because the joint optimization over both $(C, \mu)$ is NP-hard.

## 3.4 Hierarchical Clustering — Worked Dendrogram Construction

**Problem:** Five points with pairwise Euclidean distances:

| | P1 | P2 | P3 | P4 | P5 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| P1 | 0 | 2 | 6 | 10 | 9 |
| P2 | 2 | 0 | 5 | 9 | 8 |
| P3 | 6 | 5 | 0 | 4 | 5 |
| P4 | 10 | 9 | 4 | 0 | 3 |
| P5 | 9 | 8 | 5 | 3 | 0 |

Use **Single Linkage Agglomerative Clustering**.

**Iteration 1:** Smallest distance is $d(P_1, P_2) = 2$. Merge $\{P_1, P_2\}$. Height = 2.

**Iteration 2:** Recompute distances using single linkage. Smallest is $d(P_4, P_5) = 3$. Merge $\{P_4, P_5\}$. Height = 3.

**Iteration 3:** Distances between merged clusters:
- $d(\{P_1,P_2\}, P_3) = \min(6, 5) = 5$
- $d(\{P_1,P_2\}, \{P_4,P_5\}) = \min(10,9,9,8) = 8$
- $d(P_3, \{P_4,P_5\}) = \min(4, 5, 5, 3) = 3$

Smallest is $d(P_3, \{P_4,P_5\}) = 3$. Merge $\{P_3, P_4, P_5\}$. Height = 3.

**Iteration 4:** Merge $\{P_1, P_2\}$ with $\{P_3, P_4, P_5\}$. Height = $\min(5, 8) = 5$.

**Final dendrogram (cut at height = 4):** 2 clusters: $\{P_1, P_2\}$ and $\{P_3, P_4, P_5\}$.

## 3.5 DBSCAN — Worked Density Example

**Problem:** 2D points $p_1=(1,1), p_2=(1,2), p_3=(2,1), p_4=(8,8), p_5=(8,9), p_6=(9,8)$. Use $\epsilon = 1.5$ and $\text{MinPts} = 3$.

### Step 1 — Find $\epsilon$-neighborhoods

$N_\epsilon(p_1) = \{ p_2, p_3 \}$ (distances $\sqrt{1}, \sqrt{1}$). Cardinality = 2.

$N_\epsilon(p_2) = \{ p_1, p_3 \}$. Cardinality = 2.

$N_\epsilon(p_3) = \{ p_1, p_2 \}$. Cardinality = 2.

$N_\epsilon(p_4) = \{ p_5, p_6 \}$. Cardinality = 2.

$N_\epsilon(p_5) = \{ p_4, p_6 \}$. Cardinality = 2.

$N_\epsilon(p_6) = \{ p_4, p_5 \}$. Cardinality = 2.

### Step 2 — Classify Points

Since no point has $\geq 3$ neighbors, **all points are noise (outliers)** under these parameters.

**Conclusion:** No clusters are formed. This is a common DBSCAN pitfall — if $\epsilon$ is too small or MinPts too high, even valid clusters can vanish. We could try $\epsilon = 2$:

- $N_\epsilon(p_1) = \{ p_2, p_3 \}$, cardinality 2
- $N_\epsilon(p_2) = \{ p_1, p_3, ? \}$, still 2
- For $p_4$: $N_\epsilon(p_4) = \{ p_5, p_6 \}$, cardinality 2

Still insufficient. The points are too sparse.

> [!NOTE]
> **This illustrates a critical practical issue:** DBSCAN's performance is **highly sensitive to $\epsilon$ and MinPts**. Use a **k-distance plot** to choose $\epsilon$ — plot the distance to the $k$-th nearest neighbor for all points; the "elbow" gives a good $\epsilon$.

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 K-Means Clustering — Block Flow Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Input Dataset X with N points] --> B[Choose K number of clusters]
    B --> C[Initialize K centroids using K-Means or K-Means++]
    C --> D[Assignment Step: Assign each point to nearest centroid]
    D --> E[Update Step: Recompute centroids as mean of assigned points]
    E --> F{Centroids changed below threshold epsilon}
    F -->|No| G[Stop: Output final clusters and centroids]
    F -->|Yes| D
```

## 4.2 Hierarchical Clustering — Agglomerative Merge Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: N singleton clusters] --> B[Compute pairwise distance matrix]
    B --> C[Find two closest clusters]
    C --> D[Merge them into one cluster]
    D --> E[Update distance matrix using linkage criterion]
    E --> F{Only 1 cluster remains}
    F -->|No| C
    F -->|Yes| G[Cut dendrogram at desired height to obtain K clusters]
```

## 4.3 DBSCAN — Point Classification and Cluster Expansion

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Choose epsilon and MinPts] --> B[Pick an unvisited point p]
    B --> C[Find N epsilon neighborhood of p]
    C --> D{Neighborhood size greater than or equal MinPts}
    D -->|No| E[Mark p as Noise]
    D -->|Yes| F[Mark p as Core Point and create new Cluster]
    F --> G[Add all density reachable points to cluster]
    G --> H[Mark all points in cluster as visited]
    E --> I{All points visited}
    H --> I
    I -->|No| B
    I -->|Yes| J[Stop: Output clusters and noise points]
```

## 4.4 Comparative Algorithm Selection — Decision Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[New Clustering Task] --> B{Know number of K in advance}
    B -->|Yes| C{Data shape spherical and roughly equal sized}
    B -->|No| D{Data has noise and arbitrary shapes}
    C -->|Yes| E[Use K-Means]
    C -->|No| F{Need hierarchical relationships}
    F -->|Yes| G[Use Agglomerative Hierarchical]
    F -->|No| H{Need soft probabilistic assignments}
    H -->|Yes| I[Use GMM with EM]
    H -->|No| E
    D -->|Yes| J[Use DBSCAN]
    D -->|No| K[Try OPTICS or HDBSCAN]
```

## 4.5 Conceptual Cluster Structure Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph C1[Cluster 1: Dense Region A]
        A1[Point P1] --- A2[Point P2] --- A3[Point P3]
        A1 --- A4[Point P4]
        A2 --- A5[Point P5]
    end
    subgraph C2[Cluster 2: Dense Region B]
        B1[Point Q1] --- B2[Point Q2] --- B3[Point Q3]
        B1 --- B4[Point Q4]
    end
    subgraph C3[Cluster 3: Dense Region C]
        D1[Point R1] --- D2[Point R2]
        D1 --- D3[Point R3]
    end
    N1[Noise Point]:::noise
    N2[Noise Point]:::noise
    classDef noise fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#cc0000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block-Level Functional Architecture Interpretation:** Each `subgraph` represents a real cluster. Solid lines between points represent the **density-reachability** links used by DBSCAN or the **high similarity** links recognized by K-Means. The `Noise Point` nodes (in red) are points that fail to belong to any cluster under DBSCAN's $\epsilon$ criterion — they are **outliers** in the dataset.

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Explain the difference between supervised and unsupervised learning. Give two examples of clustering applications.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Supervised Learning | Unsupervised Learning |
|--------|---------------------|------------------------|
| Labels | Uses labeled training data $(x_i, y_i)$ | Uses only input data $x_i$ (no labels) |
| Goal | Learn a mapping $f: X \to Y$ | Discover hidden structure / groupings in $X$ |
| Example | Classification, Regression | **Clustering**, Dimensionality Reduction |

**Two applications of clustering:**
1. **Customer segmentation** in marketing — grouping customers by purchasing behavior.
2. **Image segmentation** in computer vision — partitioning an image into regions of similar pixels.

*(1 mark for clear distinction, 1 mark for correct definitions, 1 mark for valid applications)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define the Elbow Method for finding the optimal number of clusters in K-Means. What is WCSS?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **WCSS (Within-Cluster Sum of Squares)** is the sum of squared distances between each data point and its assigned cluster centroid. It is the objective function minimized by K-Means:

$$WCSS = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \sum_{x_i \in C_k} \| x_i - \mu_k \|^2$$

- **The Elbow Method** plots WCSS against the number of clusters $K$. As $K$ increases, WCSS decreases (every point eventually gets its own cluster when $K = N$). The "elbow" point — where the rate of decrease sharply changes — represents the optimal trade-off between cluster compactness and number of clusters. Beyond the elbow, adding more clusters gives diminishing returns.

*(1.5 marks for WCSS definition + formula, 1.5 marks for Elbow Method explanation)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
