Imagine a **grocery shelf**:
- A **Python list** is like boxes of *mixed items* (rice, soap, milk) placed randomly. To find rice, you walk through every box.
- A **NumPy array** is like a shelf where *all items are rice*, packed tightly together. The shelf "knows" every grain's location instantly because they are uniform and contiguous.

This uniformity is what makes NumPy **10× to 100× faster** than pure Python for numerical work.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Standard Metric:** NumPy is implemented in **C** and uses **SIMD (Single Instruction Multiple Data)** CPU instructions. A single NumPy operation on an array of $10^6$ elements typically executes in milliseconds, while an equivalent Python `for` loop takes seconds.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The NumPy Array (ndarray) — Operational Breakdown

The `ndarray` is constructed on three structural concepts:

**Step 1 — Data Pointer:** A single pointer to a contiguous block of system memory holding the raw data.

**Step 2 — Shape Tuple:** A tuple describing how the flat memory block is interpreted as a multi-dimensional grid.
For a 1D array of 6 elements: $\text{shape} = (6,)$.
For a 2D array of 3 rows × 4 columns: $\text{shape} = (3, 4)$.

**Step 3 — Strides Tuple:** A tuple indicating how many bytes to skip in memory to move one step along each axis.
For a 2D array with shape $(R, C)$ and itemsize $s$ bytes:
$$\text{strides} = (C \cdot s,\; s)$$

### 2.2 Array Attributes — The "Identity Card"

| Attribute | Meaning | Example |
|---|---|---|
| `arr.ndim` | Number of axes (dimensions) | `2` for a matrix |
| `arr.shape` | Tuple of dimension sizes | `(3, 4)` |
| `arr.size` | Total element count | `12` |
| `arr.dtype` | Data type of elements | `int64`, `float32` |
| `arr.itemsize` | Bytes per element | `8` for `float64` |
| `arr.nbytes` | Total memory in bytes | `96` for 12 float64s |
| `arr.strides` | Byte-steps per axis | `(32, 8)` |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — NumPy Operations & Linear Algebra

> [!NOTE]
> The following formulae appear frequently in KTU end-semester questions for Module 3. Memorize the symbols, dimensions, and conditions.

| # | Operation | NumPy Syntax | Mathematical Form | Condition / Note |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Element-wise addition | `A + B` | $C_{ij} = A_{ij} + B_{ij}$ | Same shape or broadcastable |
| 2 | Element-wise product | `A * B` | $C_{ij} = A_{ij} \cdot B_{ij}$ | Hadamard product |
| 3 | Matrix dot product | `A @ B` | $C_{ij} = \sum_{k} A_{ik} B_{kj}$ | $\text{shape}(A) = (m,n),\; \text{shape}(B) = (n,p)$ |
| 4 | Transpose | `A.T` | $(A^T)_{ij} = A_{ji}$ | Swaps axes |
| 5 | Matrix inverse | `np.linalg.inv(A)` | $A^{-1}$ exists iff $\det(A) \neq 0$ | Square matrix |
| 6 | Determinant | `np.linalg.det(A)` | $\det(A)$ | Scalar output |
| 7 | Eigenvalues/Vectors | `np.linalg.eig(A)` | $A v = \lambda v$ | Square matrix |
| 8 | Mean (axis) | `A.mean(axis=k)` | $\bar{x} = \frac{1}{n}\sum x_i$ | Reduces dimension |
| 9 | Standard deviation | `A.std()` | $\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{1}{n}\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2}$ | Population std |
| 10 | L2 norm | `np.linalg.norm(v)` | $\Vert v \Vert_2 = \sqrt{\sum v_i^2}$ | Euclidean norm |
| 11 | Reshape | `A.reshape(m,n)` | Preserves total size | $m \cdot n = A.\text{size}$ |
| 12 | Broadcasting scalar | `A + c` | $C_{ij} = A_{ij} + c$ | Scalar stretches |

### 2.4 Broadcasting — The "Why" Behind NumPy's Power

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Broadcasting Rule (KTU High-Yield):**
> When operating on two arrays of different shapes, NumPy compares shapes from the **trailing (rightmost) dimension** backward:
> 1. Dimensions are *compatible* if they are **equal** OR one of them is **1**.
> 2. The array with dimension 1 is *stretched* (conceptually, no copy) to match.
> 3. If neither condition holds, broadcasting **fails** with a `ValueError`.

**Worked Mini-Example:**
- $A$ has shape $(3, 4)$, $B$ has shape $(4,)$. 
- Trailing dims: $4$ vs $4$ → equal ✓.
- Next dim: $3$ vs (none) → treated as 1, stretches ✓.
- Result shape: $(3, 4)$.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility of NumPy

- **Image Processing:** Images stored as 3D NumPy arrays of shape `(height, width, channels)`.
- **Signal Processing:** Convolution of audio signals via 1D arrays + `np.convolve`.
- **Control Systems:** State-space matrices $(A, B, C, D)$ are NumPy arrays.
- **Deep Learning:** A single training batch of 32 RGB images of size $224 \times 224$ is a tensor of shape $(32, 224, 224, 3)$ — exactly a 4D `ndarray`.
- **Optimization:** Gradients in gradient descent are computed as `theta = theta - lr * gradients` — pure NumPy vectorized arithmetic.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Problem Context

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Problem:** A 2D dataset is represented as:
> $$X = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 3 & 4 \\ 5 & 6 \end{bmatrix},\quad y = \begin{bmatrix} 10 \\ 20 \\ 30 \end{bmatrix}$$
> Compute (a) the matrix $X^T X$, (b) the inverse $(X^T X)^{-1}$, (c) the OLS coefficient vector $\beta = (X^T X)^{-1} X^T y$ using NumPy.

This mirrors the **Normal Equation** used in Linear Regression — a recurring KTU Module 3 question.

### 3.2 Manual Algebraic Derivation (Closed Form)

**Step 1 — Compute $X^T$ (transpose):**

Transpose swaps rows and columns.

$$X^T = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 3 & 5 \\ 2 & 4 & 6 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $X^T X$ (a $2 \times 2$ matrix):**

Each element is an inner product of one row of $X^T$ with one column of $X$.

$$X^T X = \begin{bmatrix} 1\cdot1 + 3\cdot3 + 5\cdot5 & 1\cdot2 + 3\cdot4 + 5\cdot6 \\ 2\cdot1 + 4\cdot3 + 6\cdot5 & 2\cdot2 + 4\cdot4 + 6\cdot6 \end{bmatrix}$$

Evaluating each entry:
- Top-left: $1 + 9 + 25 = 35$
- Top-right: $2 + 12 + 30 = 44$
- Bottom-left: $2 + 12 + 30 = 44$
- Bottom-right: $4 + 16 + 36 = 56$

$$X^T X = \begin{bmatrix} 35 & 44 \\ 44 & 56 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the determinant $\det(X^T X)$:**

For a $2 \times 2$ matrix $\begin{bmatrix} a & b \\ c & d \end{bmatrix}$, $\det = ad - bc$.

$$\det(X^T X) = (35)(56) - (44)(44)$$

$$= 1960 - 1936 = 24$$

Since $\det = 24 \neq 0$, the inverse exists.

**Step 4 — Compute the inverse using the adjugate formula:**

For a $2 \times 2$ matrix, the inverse is:

$$(X^T X)^{-1} = \frac{1}{\det(X^T X)} \begin{bmatrix} d & -b \\ -c & a \end{bmatrix}$$

Substituting $a=35,\; b=44,\; c=44,\; d=56$:

$$(X^T X)^{-1} = \frac{1}{24} \begin{bmatrix} 56 & -44 \\ -44 & 35 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$= \begin{bmatrix} 56/24 & -44/24 \\ -44/24 & 35/24 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 7/3 & -11/6 \\ -11/6 & 35/24 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5 — Compute $X^T y$:**

$$X^T y = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 3 & 5 \\ 2 & 4 & 6 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 10 \\ 20 \\ 30 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 10 + 60 + 150 \\ 20 + 80 + 180 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 220 \\ 280 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 6 — Compute the OLS coefficient vector $\beta$:**

$$\beta = (X^T X)^{-1} X^T y = \frac{1}{24} \begin{bmatrix} 56 & -44 \\ -44 & 35 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 220 \\ 280 \end{bmatrix}$$

Top entry:

$$\beta_1 = \frac{1}{24}(56 \cdot 220 + (-44) \cdot 280)$$

$$= \frac{1}{24}(12320 - 12320) = 0$$

Bottom entry:

$$\beta_2 = \frac{1}{24}((-44) \cdot 220 + 35 \cdot 280)$$

$$= \frac{1}{24}(-9680 + 9800) = \frac{120}{24} = 5$$

Therefore:

$$\beta = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 5 \end{bmatrix}$$

Interpretation: the regression line is $\hat{y} = 5 x_2$ (i.e., $y$ depends only on the second feature in this synthetic dataset).

### 3.3 NumPy Implementation (Step-by-Step)

The corresponding NumPy code that produces the same result:

```python
import numpy as np

# Step 1: Create the data
X = np.array([[1, 2],
              [3, 4],
              [5, 6]])
y = np.array([10, 20, 30])

print("X.shape =", X.shape)        # (3, 2)
print("y.shape =", y.shape)        # (3,)

# Step 2: Compute X^T X
XtX = X.T @ X
print("X^T X =\n", XtX)
# [[35 44]
#  [44 56]]

# Step 3: Compute determinant
det = np.linalg.det(XtX)
print("det(X^T X) =", det)          # 24.0

# Step 4: Compute inverse
XtX_inv = np.linalg.inv(XtX)
print("(X^T X)^-1 =\n", XtX_inv)

# Step 5: Compute X^T y
Xty = X.T @ y
print("X^T y =", Xty)               # [220 280]

# Step 6: Solve for beta
beta = XtX_inv @ Xty
print("beta =", beta)               # [0. 5.]

# Alternative one-liner using the built-in least-squares solver
beta_lstsq, residuals, rank, sv = np.linalg.lstsq(X, y, rcond=None)
print("beta (lstsq) =", beta_lstsq) # [0. 5.]
```

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Aggregation Along Axes

For a 2D array, the `axis` parameter controls *which* dimension is reduced.

| Input Shape | Operation | `axis` | Output Shape | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `(3, 4)` | `A.sum(axis=0)` | 0 | `(4,)` | Sum *down each column* |
| `(3, 4)` | `A.sum(axis=1)` | 1 | `(3,)` | Sum *across each row* |
| `(3, 4)` | `A.sum(axis=None)` | None | scalar | Sum of all elements |
| `(3, 4, 5)` | `A.mean(axis=2)` | 2 | `(3, 4)` | Mean along the last axis |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** "Axis $k$ is the one that **disappears**." Memorize this phrase — it appears verbatim in many past KTU papers.

### 3.5 Reshape & Flatten — Memory vs View

| Operation | Returns | Modifies Original? | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| `A.reshape(m,n)` | View (usually) | No (if compatible) | Re-arrange dimensions |
| `A.flatten()` | **Copy** | No | Always 1D, safe to modify |
| `A.ravel()` | View (usually) | No | Fast 1D, read-only intent |
| `A.resize(m,n)` | In-place modification | **Yes** | Permanent reshape |

The convention: `reshape` and `ravel` are **views** (no data copy) when the memory layout is contiguous; otherwise a copy is made. `flatten` *always* copies.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Python ML Stack — Layered Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph L5["LAYER 5: DEEP LEARNING"]
        L5A["TensorFlow"]
        L5B["PyTorch"]
        L5C["Keras"]
    end

    subgraph L4["LAYER 4: CLASSICAL ML"]
        L4A["Scikit-learn"]
        L4B["XGBoost"]
        L4C["LightGBM"]
    end

    subgraph L3["LAYER 3: VISUALIZATION"]
        L3A["Matplotlib"]
        L3B["Seaborn"]
        L3C["Plotly"]
    end

    subgraph L2["LAYER 2: DATA STRUCTURES"]
        L2A["Pandas DataFrame"]
        L2B["Pandas Series"]
    end

    subgraph L1["LAYER 1: NUMERICAL FOUNDATION"]
        L1A["NumPy ndarray"]
        L1B["SciPy"]
        L1C["C / Fortran Backend"]
    end

    L5 --> L4
    L5 --> L2
    L4 --> L2
    L4 --> L1
    L3 --> L1
    L2 --> L1
    L1A --> L1B
    L1B --> L1C
    L1A --> L1C
```

**Reading the Diagram:** Each upper layer depends on the layers below. NumPy is the bedrock — every higher library ultimately calls NumPy for its numerical core.

### 4.2 NumPy Array Internal Memory Layout

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph MD["METADATA (Python Object Header)"]
        D1["data: pointer to memory"]
        D2["shape: tuple of dims"]
        D3["strides: bytes per axis"]
        D4["dtype: element type"]
    end

    subgraph MEM["CONTIGUOUS MEMORY BLOCK (Raw Bytes)"]
        M1["e0"]
        M2["e1"]
        M3["e2"]
        M4["e3"]
        M5["e4"]
        M6["e5"]
        M7["e7"]
        M8["e8"]
        M9["e9"]
        M10["e10"]
        M11["e11"]
    end

    MD --> MEM
```

**Interpretation:** The `ndarray` object is *thin* Python metadata pointing to a *thick* contiguous C-level buffer. Operations reshape, transpose, or slice by changing the metadata (strides, shape) without moving any data — this is the source of NumPy's efficiency.

### 4.3 Broadcasting Visualization Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["Array A: shape (3, 1)"] --> C["Broadcasting Engine"]
    B["Array B: shape (1, 4)"] --> C
    C --> D["Conceptual Stretch: A -> (3, 4), B -> (3, 4)"]
    D --> E["Element-wise Operation"]
    E --> F["Result: shape (3, 4)"]
```

### 4.4 NumPy Operation Taxonomy (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ROOT["NumPy ndarray Operations"]

    ROOT --> CR["CREATION"]
    ROOT --> MA["MANIPULATION"]
    ROOT --> MA2["MATHEMATICS"]
    ROOT --> ALG["LINEAR ALGEBRA"]
    ROOT --> AGG["AGGREGATION"]
    ROOT --> RNG["RANDOM"]

    CR --> CR1["np.array, np.zeros, np.ones"]
    CR --> CR2["np.arange, np.linspace"]
    CR --> CR3["np.random.rand, np.random.randn"]

    MA --> MA1["reshape, flatten, ravel"]
    MA --> MA2["transpose, swapaxes"]
    MA --> MA3["concatenate, stack, split"]

    MA2X["MATHEMATICS"] --> MX1["element-wise: + - * /"]
    MA2X --> MX2["broadcasting"]
    MA2X --> MX3["ufuncs: sin, log, exp"]

    ALG --> AL1["np.dot, np.matmul, @"]
    ALG --> AL2["np.linalg.inv, det, eig"]
    ALG --> AL3["np.linalg.norm, svd"]

    AGG --> AG1["sum, mean, std, var"]
    AGG --> AG2["min, max, argmin, argmax"]
    AGG --> AG3["cumsum, prod"]

    RNG --> RN1["np.random.seed"]
    RNG --> RN2["np.random.choice"]
    RNG --> RN3["np.random.normal"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this taxonomy:** During exams, locate the *operation type* (e.g., "aggregation along axis") then trace to the *specific function* (e.g., `np.sum(axis=0)`). This mirrors how KTU board examiners expect you to answer "list and explain NumPy functions for X."

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]**
> **Define the term `ndarray` in NumPy. List any four attributes of a NumPy array.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> An **ndarray** (N-dimensional array) is the core data structure of NumPy. It represents a *homogeneous*, *fixed-size* grid of elements of the same data type, stored in a contiguous block of memory with metadata describing its shape, strides, and dtype.

**Four Attributes:**

1. **`ndim`** — Number of axes (dimensions) of the array.
2. **`shape`** — Tuple indicating the size along each axis (e.g., `(3, 4)`).
3. **`dtype`** — Data type of the array elements (e.g., `int64`, `float32`).
4. **`size`** — Total number of elements in the array (product of shape).

*(Any 4 from: ndim, shape, size, dtype, itemsize, nbytes, strides — full marks.)*

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
> **Explain the concept of *broadcasting* in NumPy with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Broadcasting** is NumPy's mechanism for performing element-wise operations on arrays of *different but compatible* shapes *without copying data*. Two dimensions are compatible if they are equal, or if one of them is 1.

**Example:** Adding a 1D array to each row of a 2D array.

```python
import numpy as np
A = np.array([[1, 2, 3],
              [4, 5, 6]])         # shape (2, 3)
b = np.array([10, 20, 30])       # shape (3,)
C = A + b
print(C)
# [[11 22 33]
#  [14 25 36]]
```

Here `b` is conceptually stretched along the first axis from `(3,)` to `(2, 3)` to match `A`, then added element-wise. This avoids an explicit Python loop and yields the result in a single vectorized call.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **Q3. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Main Question Paper]**
> **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the internal memory layout of a NumPy `ndarray`. Discuss how the `strides` attribute enables efficient reshaping and transposing without copying data. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Consider the matrices $A = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 1 & 3 \end{bmatrix}$ and $B = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & -1 \\ 2 & 5 \end{bmatrix}$. Using NumPy, write the code to compute (i) $A + B$, (ii) $A \cdot B$ (matrix product), (iii) the determinant and inverse of $A$, and (iv) the eigenvalues of $A$. Show the numerical outputs. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Internal Memory Layout (7 Marks):**

A NumPy `ndarray` consists of two parts:

1. **Python-level Metadata (the "thin" header):**
   - `data` — pointer to the start of the raw memory block.
   - `shape` — tuple of dimension sizes.
   - `strides` — tuple of byte-step sizes per axis.
   - `dtype` — element data type descriptor.

2. **Contiguous Memory Block (the "thick" data buffer):**
   - A flat, C-order sequence of homogeneous elements.

**Diagram of a $2 \times 3$ float64 array (total 48 bytes):**

```
Metadata:
   data  -> 0x1000
   shape -> (2, 3)
   strides -> (24, 8)   # row-step = 24 bytes, col-step = 8 bytes
   dtype -> float64

Memory (starting at 0x1000):
   0x1000: e00 e01 e02
   0x1018: e10 e11 e12
```

**Why strides matter:**
- To move from `A[i, j]` to `A[i, j+1]`, NumPy advances the pointer by `strides[1] = 8` bytes.
- To move from `A[i, j]` to `A[i+1, j]`, NumPy advances by `strides[0] = 24` bytes.
- For a **transpose**, NumPy simply swaps `shape` to `(3, 2)` and `strides` to `(8, 24)` — *no data is copied*. This is why `A.T` is instantaneous and memory-free.
- For a **reshape**, if the data is contiguous in the requested order, only the shape tuple changes. Otherwise, a contiguous copy is made.

**(b) NumPy Computations (7 Marks):**

```python
import numpy as np

A = np.array([[2, 1],
              [1, 3]])
B = np.array([[4, -1],
              [2, 5]])

# (i) Element-wise addition
add = A + B
print("A + B =\n", add)
# [[6 0]
#  [3 8]]

# (ii) Matrix product
prod = A @ B
print("A @ B =\n", prod)
# [[10  3]
#  [10 14]]

# (iii) Determinant and inverse of A
det_A = np.linalg.det(A)
inv_A = np.linalg.inv(A)
print("det(A) =", det_A)        # 5.0
print("inv(A) =\n", inv_A)
# [[ 0.6 -0.2]
#  [-0.2  0.4]]

# (iv) Eigenvalues of A
eigvals, eigvecs = np.linalg.eig(A)
print("eigenvalues =", eigvals)  # [3.618...  1.381...]
print("eigenvectors =\n", eigvecs)
```

---

> **Q4. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Alternative Choice for Q3]**
> **(a)** Explain the difference between a Python list and a NumPy `ndarray`. Discuss why NumPy is preferred for numerical computation in machine learning, with reference to *vectorization* and *memory efficiency*. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Given the dataset $X = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 2 & 1 \\ 3 & 4 \end{bmatrix}$ and target vector $y = \begin{bmatrix} 5 \\ 4 \\ 11 \end{bmatrix}$, compute the Ordinary Least Squares (OLS) coefficient vector $\beta = (X^T X)^{-1} X^T y$ using NumPy. Verify your answer using `np.linalg.lstsq`. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Python List vs NumPy ndarray (7 Marks):**

| Aspect | Python List | NumPy ndarray |
|---|---|---|
| Element types | Heterogeneous (mixed types) | Homogeneous (single dtype) |
| Memory layout | Non-contiguous; each element a separate Python object | Contiguous C-level buffer of raw values |
| Size overhead | High (each element is a full PyObject) | Low (only metadata + raw values) |
| Speed of math | Slow (Python-level `for` loops) | Fast (C-level vectorized loops) |
| Built-in math ops | No (`+` concatenates, `*` repeats) | Yes (`+`, `-`, `*`, `/` are element-wise) |
| Dimensions | 1D only natively | N-D (`ndim`, `shape`, `strides`) |
| Linear algebra | Not built-in | Full `np.linalg` module |
| Memory per `int` | ~28 bytes | ~8 bytes (`int64`) |

**Vectorization:** NumPy replaces explicit Python loops with a single C-level pass over the data. Example:
- Python loop: $10^6$ additions ≈ 0.5 seconds.
- NumPy vectorized: $10^6$ additions ≈ 1 millisecond.

**Memory Efficiency:** Because NumPy stores raw numeric values in a single contiguous buffer, memory access exploits CPU cache locality, dramatically improving throughput.

For ML, where datasets routinely contain millions of rows and thousands of features, these advantages are *essential*, not optional.

**(b) OLS via NumPy (7 Marks):**

```python
import numpy as np

X = np.array([[1, 2],
              [2, 1],
              [3, 4]])
y = np.array([5, 4, 11])

# Method 1: Closed-form Normal Equation
XtX = X.T @ X
Xty = X.T @ y
beta = np.linalg.inv(XtX) @ Xty
print("beta (closed-form) =", beta)
# [1. 2.]

# Method 2: Robust lstsq solver (numerically stable)
beta_lstsq, residuals, rank, sv = np.linalg.lstsq(X, y, rcond=None)
print("beta (lstsq)      =", beta_lstsq)
# [1. 2.]
```

**Manual verification (for partial credit if asked):**

$X^T X = \begin{bmatrix} 1+4+9 & 2+2+12 \\ 2+2+12 & 4+1+16 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 14 & 16 \\ 16 & 21 \end{bmatrix}$

$X^T y = \begin{bmatrix} 5+8+33 \\ 10+4+44 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 46 \\ 58 \end{bmatrix}$

$\det(X^T X) = 14 \cdot 21 - 16 \cdot 16 = 294 - 256 = 38$

$(X^T X)^{-1} = \frac{1}{38}\begin{bmatrix} 21 & -16 \\ -16 & 14 \end{bmatrix}$

$\beta = \frac{1}{38}\begin{bmatrix} 21\cdot 46 - 16 \cdot 58 \\ -16 \cdot 46 + 14 \cdot 58 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{38}\begin{bmatrix} 966 - 928 \\ -736 + 812 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{38}\begin{bmatrix} 38 \\ 76 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 2 \end{bmatrix}$

So the regression line is $\hat{y} = x_1 + 2 x_2$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Module 3):**
> 1. **Confusing `axis`:** Many students write `A.sum(axis=1)` when they mean "sum of each column." Remember: **`axis=k` is the dimension that disappears**.
> 2. **Using `inv()` blindly:** Computing $(X^T X)^{-1}$ directly is numerically unstable for ill-conditioned matrices. The KTU syllabus accepts the closed-form, but `np.linalg.lstsq` or `np.linalg.solve(XtX, Xty)` is preferred in practice.
> 3. **Forgetting dtype defaults:** `np.array([1, 2, 3])` creates an `int64` array, while `np.array([1.0, 2.0, 3.0])` creates `float64`. Mixing these in one expression causes silent upcasting — always declare `dtype` explicitly for ML pipelines.
> 4. **Using `A * B` instead of `A @ B`:** The `*` operator performs *element-wise* multiplication (Hadamard product), NOT matrix multiplication. For matrix product use `@` or `np.dot`.
> 5. **Ignoring broadcasting errors:** Operations between shapes `(3,)` and `(4,)` will raise `ValueError`. Always mentally check trailing-dim compatibility before broadcasting.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — NumPy Essentials for KTU Module 3:**

- **NumPy is the foundation** of the Python ML stack; every higher library (Pandas, Scikit-learn, TensorFlow) uses it internally.
- **`ndarray`** is a homogeneous, fixed-size, contiguous N-dimensional array — defined by `data`, `shape`, `strides`, `dtype`.
- **Vectorization** replaces Python `for` loops with single C-level operations — orders of magnitude faster.
- **Broadcasting** lets you operate on differently-shaped arrays provided trailing dimensions are equal or one is 1; the smaller array is *conceptually* stretched.
- **Creation functions:** `np.array()`, `np.zeros()`, `np.ones()`, `np.arange()`, `np.linspace()`, `np.random.rand()`.
- **Shape manipulation:** `reshape`, `flatten`, `ravel`, `transpose`/`T`, `swapaxes`. Remember reshape returns a *view* when possible.
- **Aggregation with `axis`:** `axis=k` reduces dimension `k`. Use `keepdims=True` to preserve shape.
- **Element-wise vs matrix product:** `*` is element-wise; `@` (or `np.dot`) is matrix product.
- **Linear algebra module (`np.linalg`):** `inv`, `det`, `eig`, `norm`, `solve`, `lstsq`, `svd`.
- **OLS Normal Equation:** $\beta = (X^T X)^{-1} X^T y$ — a **direct application** of NumPy's `inv` and `@` operators in Linear Regression.
- **Strides** make transpose and slicing essentially free (no data copy).
- **Default dtype:** Integer literals give `int64`; floating literals give `float64`. Use `np.float32` for GPU/deep-learning memory savings.
- **Famous ML pipeline using NumPy:** Every classical ML algorithm (Linear Regression, Logistic Regression, KNN) can be implemented in *pure NumPy* — it is the universal ML primitive.
- **Common exam trap:** `*` vs `@`. Mark deduction guaranteed if you mix them up.
- **Sanity check formula:** `A.size == np.prod(A.shape)`.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Pandas** is an open-source, BSD-licensed Python library providing high-performance, easy-to-use data structures and data analysis tools. It is built on top of the **NumPy** library and is the de-facto standard tool for data wrangling in any Machine Learning (ML) pipeline.

In the formal KTU 2024 Machine Learning syllabus, Pandas is positioned as the **Data Pre-processing & Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA)** engine that bridges raw heterogeneous data sources and the NumPy/SciPy mathematical back-end consumed by Scikit-learn models.

The two primary data structures of Pandas are:

1. **Series** — A **one-dimensional** labeled array capable of holding any data type (integers, strings, floats, Python objects, etc.). The *labels* are collectively called the **index**.
2. **DataFrame** — A **two-dimensional**, size-mutable, tabular data structure with potentially heterogeneously-typed columns. It is the *pandas* equivalent of a spreadsheet, an SQL table, or a dictionary of Series objects.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pandas Core Objects**
> | Object | Dimensionality | Mutability | Homogeneous? |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | `Series` | 1-D | Value-mutable, Size-immutable | No (single dtype typically) |
> | `DataFrame` | 2-D | Both value and size mutable | Column-wise |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have a **super-powered Excel spreadsheet** that lives inside Python:

- **Columns** can each hold a different type of data (numbers, text, dates, booleans), unlike NumPy arrays which force a single type.
- Every row and column has a **label (index)**, so you can ask "give me the row labelled `student_42`" instead of "give me row #41".
- It has built-in functions to **filter out missing grades, join two spreadsheets, group students by department, and compute average marks** — all in a single chained command.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Anchor:** Think of a Pandas **DataFrame** as a *labelled, indexed, dictionary-of-Columns*. Think of a **Series** as a *labelled, indexed, single Column*. Pandas = **Panel Data** + **Python Data Analysis**, coined by Wes McKinney in **2008**.

## 1.3 Why Pandas for Machine Learning?

In a standard KTU 2024 ML pipeline, Pandas handles approximately **70–80%** of the time spent on a project (the so-called *"data janitor"* phase). The reasons are:

1. **Vectorized Operations** — avoids Python `for` loops; uses optimized C code under the hood.
2. **I/O Flexibility** — reads CSV, Excel, JSON, SQL, Parquet, HTML tables directly.
3. **Missing-Data Handling** — NaN, NULL, None treated uniformly.
4. **Alignment** — automatic label-based alignment during arithmetic.
5. **Integration** — first-class citizen in the **Scikit-learn**, **Matplotlib**, **Seaborn** ecosystem.

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Internal Architecture of Pandas

A DataFrame is internally composed of three distinct components:

$$
\text{DataFrame} = \langle \text{Index},\ \text{Columns},\ \text{BlockManager} \rangle
$$

1. **Index** (`row_labels`) — Immutable sequence of labels (like a primary key).
2. **Columns** (`col_labels`) — The column names.
3. **BlockManager** — The internal C-level engine that groups columns of the same `dtype` into contiguous NumPy arrays called **blocks**. This is why column-wise operations are fast.

## 2.2 The Two Axes Convention

Pandas uses the `axis` parameter extensively:

| `axis` value | Meaning | Affects |
|---|---|---|
| `axis=0` (default) | Row-wise / Down the rows | `index` (rows) |
| `axis=1` | Column-wise / Across the columns | `columns` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory Trick:** `axis=0` ⇒ operates **vertically** (collapses rows). `axis=1` ⇒ operates **horizontally** (collapses columns). Equivalent to NumPy's `axis=0` (rows) and `axis=1` (columns).

## 2.3 Indexing Hierarchy — `loc` vs `iloc`

This is the **single most asked Pandas concept in KTU exams**.

| Method | Type | Selection Basis | Slicing Rule |
|---|---|---|---|
| `df.loc[]` | **Label-based** | Row/Column *names* | `start:stop` is **inclusive** of both ends |
| `df.iloc[]` | **Integer-position-based** | Row/Column *positions* (0-indexed) | `start:stop` is **exclusive** of `stop` (Python-style) |
| `df.iat[]` | Scalar, position | Single cell by position | — |
| `df.at[]` | Scalar, label | Single cell by label | — |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Pandas Cheat Sheet

### 2.4.1 Series & DataFrame Construction

| Operation | Syntax | Returns |
|---|---|---|
| Create Series from list | `pd.Series([1,2,3])` | Series with default RangeIndex |
| Series with custom index | `pd.Series(data, index=['a','b'])` | Labeled Series |
| Create DataFrame from dict | `pd.DataFrame({'A':[1,2], 'B':[3,4]})` | 2×2 DataFrame |
| From NumPy array | `pd.DataFrame(np.arange(6).reshape(2,3), columns=list('ABC'))` | 2×3 DataFrame |
| From list of dicts | `pd.DataFrame([{'a':1},{'a':2,'b':3}])` | Fills missing with NaN |

### 2.4.2 Inspection Methods

| Method | Purpose | Returns |
|---|---|---|
| `df.head(n)` | First n rows | DataFrame |
| `df.tail(n)` | Last n rows | DataFrame |
| `df.info()` | Schema, dtypes, non-null counts | Print to stdout |
| `df.describe()` | Summary stats for numeric cols | DataFrame |
| `df.shape` | (rows, cols) | Tuple |
| `df.dtypes` | Column dtypes | Series |
| `df.columns` | Column labels | Index object |
| `df.index` | Row labels | Index object |
| `df.values` / `df.to_numpy()` | Underlying NumPy array | ndarray |

### 2.4.3 Selection & Filtering

| Operation | Syntax Example |
|---|---|
| Select single column | `df['A']` → Series |
| Select multiple cols | `df[['A','B']]` → DataFrame |
| Boolean filter | `df[df['Age'] > 18]` |
| Multi-condition filter | `df[(df['Age']>18) & (df['Gender']=='F')]` |
| Query method | `df.query("Age > 18 and Gender == 'F'")` |
| Label-based | `df.loc[2:5, ['Name','Age']]` |
| Position-based | `df.iloc[0:3, 0:2]` |
| `isin` filter | `df[df['Dept'].isin(['CS','IT'])]` |
| `where` | `df.where(df['A']>0, 0)` — keeps shape, replaces False with 0 |

### 2.4.4 Missing Data Detection & Treatment

| Method | Action | Modifies Original? |
|---|---|---|
| `df.isnull()` / `df.isna()` | Boolean mask of NaN | No |
| `df.notnull()` / `df.notna()` | Boolean mask of non-NaN | No |
| `df.dropna()` | Drop rows/cols with NaN | No (use `inplace=True`) |
| `df.fillna(value)` | Replace NaN with `value` | No |
| `df.interpolate()` | Linear/polynomial interpolation | No |

> [!WARNING]
> `inplace=True` permanently mutates the original DataFrame. Avoid in production code; use `df = df.fillna(0)` instead.

### 2.4.5 Transformation & Aggregation

| Operation | Syntax | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Element-wise function | `df.apply(np.sqrt)` | Across cols by default |
| Row-wise function | `df.apply(lambda r: r.sum(), axis=1)` | — |
| Element-wise mapping | `df['col'].map({'M':1,'F':0})` | Series only |
| Element-wise on full df | `df.applymap(str)` | Deprecated in Pandas 2.1+ → use `df.map()` |
| Aggregation per column | `df.agg(['mean','sum'])` | — |
| `value_counts()` | `df['col'].value_counts()` | Frequency table |

### 2.4.6 Combining DataFrames

| Method | Use Case | Join Type |
|---|---|---|
| `pd.concat([df1, df2])` | Stack vertically or side-by-side | Union (default `outer`) |
| `pd.merge(df1, df2, on='key')` | SQL-style join | Inner/Outer/Left/Right |
| `df1.join(df2)` | Join on index | Default `left` |

> `concat` is **set-union** logic; `merge` is **set-intersection** logic by default.

### 2.4.7 GroupBy Mechanics (Split–Apply–Combine)

$$
\text{GroupBy} : \text{DataFrame} \xrightarrow{\text{split}} \{\text{Groups}\} \xrightarrow{\text{apply}} \{\text{Results}\} \xrightarrow{\text{combine}} \text{DataFrame}
$$

```python
df.groupby('Department')['Salary'].agg(['mean', 'max', 'count'])
```

### 2.4.8 Pivot & Crosstab

| Method | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `df.pivot_table(values, index, columns, aggfunc)` | Spreadsheet-style pivot |
| `pd.crosstab(df.A, df.B)` | Frequency cross-tabulation |
| `df.melt()` | Wide → Long format |
| `df.stack()` / `df.unstack()` | Reshape between long and wide |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Pandas Application |
|---|---|
| **Predictive Maintenance** | Merge IoT sensor logs with maintenance records; rolling-window means. |
| **Fraud Detection** | Group transactions by `user_id`; compute `time_diff` features. |
| **Healthcare Analytics** | Pivot patient lab results; resample time-series vitals. |
| **NLP** | Token frequency tables via `value_counts()`; `merge` corpus with labels. |
| **Finance** | Time-zone aware date alignment, OHLCV resampling. |

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations, Operations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> The following subsections provide **exhaustive, line-by-line** walkthroughs of every critical Pandas operation tested in the KTU 2024 syllabus. All code blocks are runnable in **Python 3.10+ with `pandas >= 2.0`**.

## 3.1 Series — Construction and Vectorized Arithmetic

**Step 1:** Import the library (the standard alias is `pd`).

```python
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
```

**Step 2:** Create a Series from a Python list with an explicit index.

```python
marks = pd.Series(
    data   = [85, 92, 78, 95],
    index  = ['Alice', 'Bob', 'Charlie', 'Diana'],
    name   = 'MidTermMarks',
    dtype  = 'int64'
)
print(marks)
```

**Output:**

```
Alice      85
Bob        92
Charlie    78
Diana      95
Name: MidTermMarks, dtype: int64
```

**Step 3:** Vectorized arithmetic — Pandas aligns by **label**, not by position.

```python
grace = pd.Series(data=[3, 0, 5, 0], index=['Alice','Bob','Charlie','Diana'])
adjusted = marks + grace
print(adjusted)
```

**Output:**

```
Alice      88
Bob        92
Charlie    83
Diana      95
dtype: int64
```

**Logic:** Pandas pairs the two Series on the **shared index labels** before adding. If a label is missing in one Series, the result is `NaN`.

**Step 4:** Boolean indexing on a Series.

```python
high_scorers = marks[marks > 85]
print(high_scorers)
```

**Output:**

```
Bob       92
Diana     95
Name: MidTermMarks, dtype: int64
```

## 3.2 DataFrame — The Workhorse

**Step 1:** Create from a dictionary of equal-length lists.

```python
data = {
    'Name'    : ['Alice', 'Bob', 'Charlie', 'Diana', 'Ethan'],
    'Age'     : [20, 22, 21, 23, 19],
    'GPA'     : [8.7, 7.9, 9.1, 8.4, 7.5],
    'Dept'    : ['CSE', 'ECE', 'CSE', 'MECH', 'CSE']
}
df = pd.DataFrame(data, index=['S1','S2','S3','S4','S5'])
print(df)
```

**Output:**

```
       Name  Age  GPA  Dept
S1    Alice   20  8.7   CSE
S2      Bob   22  7.9   ECE
S3  Charlie   21  9.1   CSE
S4    Diana   23  8.4  MECH
S5    Ethan   19  7.5   CSE
```

**Step 2:** Inspect schema with `info()` and `describe()`.

```python
df.info()
df.describe(include='all')   # include='all' forces non-numeric stats too
```

**Step 3:** Indexing comparison — `loc` vs `iloc`.

```python
# Label-based: inclusive of both ends
print(df.loc['S2':'S4', ['Name','GPA']])

# Position-based: exclusive of stop
print(df.iloc[1:4, [0,2]])
```

**Output for both:**

```
       Name  GPA
S2      Bob  7.9
S3  Charlie  9.1
S4    Diana  8.4
```

## 3.3 Handling Missing Data (Imputation Pipeline)

**Step 1:** Inject NaN.

```python
df.loc['S2', 'GPA'] = np.nan
df.loc['S4', 'Age'] = np.nan
print(df)
```

**Output:**

```
       Name   Age  GPA  Dept
S1    Alice  20.0  8.7   CSE
S2      Bob  22.0  NaN   ECE
S3  Charlie  21.0  9.1   CSE
S4    Diana   NaN  8.4  MECH
S5    Ethan  19.0  7.5   CSE
```

**Step 2:** Detect missing cells.

```python
mask = df.isnull()
print(mask.sum())         # count of NaN per column
```

**Output:**

```
Name    0
Age     1
GPA     1
Dept    0
dtype: int64
```

**Step 3:** Impute — mean for numeric, mode for categorical.

```python
df['Age'] = df['Age'].fillna(df['Age'].mean())
df['GPA'] = df['GPA'].fillna(df['GPA'].mean())
print(df)
```

**Mathematical Imputation Rule (Mean):**

$$
\hat{x}_{\text{imputed}} = \bar{x} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i \quad \text{(where } x_i \text{ is observed)}
$$

**Output after imputation:**

```
       Name    Age   GPA  Dept
S1    Alice  20.00  8.70   CSE
S2      Bob  22.00  8.425  ECE    # GPA now = (8.7+9.1+8.4+7.5)/4
S3  Charlie  21.00  9.10   CSE
S4    Diana  20.50  8.40  MECH   # Age now = (20+22+21+19)/4
S5    Ethan  19.00  7.50   CSE
```

## 3.4 GroupBy — Split, Apply, Combine

```python
result = df.groupby('Dept').agg(
    Count  = ('Name', 'count'),
    AvgGPA = ('GPA', 'mean'),
    MaxAge = ('Age', 'max')
).round(2)
print(result)
```

**Mathematical Interpretation:**

$$
\bar{x}_{\text{group}} = \frac{1}{\vert G \vert} \sum_{i \in G} x_i
$$

**Output:**

```
      Count  AvgGPA  MaxAge
Dept
CSE       3    8.51    21.0
ECE       1    8.43    22.0
MECH      1    8.40    20.5
```

## 3.5 Merge (SQL-Style Join)

```python
projects = pd.DataFrame({
    'Name'  : ['Alice','Bob','Charlie'],
    'Topic' : ['CNN','RNN','GAN']
})

merged = pd.merge(df, projects, on='Name', how='left')
print(merged[['Name','Dept','Topic']])
```

**Logic:** `how='left'` keeps every row of `df`; non-matches in `projects` become `NaN` in `Topic`. Diana and Ethan are preserved with `NaN`.

**Output:**

```
       Name  Dept  Topic
S1    Alice   CSE    CNN
S2      Bob   ECE    RNN
S3  Charlie   CSE    GAN
S4    Diana  MECH    NaN
S5    Ethan   CSE    NaN
```

## 3.6 Pivot Table

```python
pivot = df.pivot_table(
    values = 'GPA',
    index  = 'Dept',
    columns= 'Name',
    aggfunc= 'mean'
)
print(pivot)
```

**Output (truncated):**

```
Name   Alice  Charlie  Diana  Ethan
Dept
CSE     8.7     9.1     NaN    7.5
ECE     NaN     NaN     NaN    NaN
MECH    NaN     NaN     8.4    NaN
```

## 3.7 Time-Series Resampling

```python
rng = pd.date_range('2024-01-01', periods=10, freq='D')
ts = pd.Series(np.random.randint(10, 50, 10), index=rng)
weekly = ts.resample('W').sum()
print(weekly)
```

**Math:** Resampling from frequency $f_1$ to $f_2$ aggregates within each bin via the chosen function (e.g., sum, mean).

## 3.8 Analytical Comparison Matrix — When to Use What?

| Goal | Recommended Pandas Tool |
|---|---|
| Filter rows by condition | Boolean indexing / `query()` |
| Select specific cols/rows | `loc` / `iloc` |
| Rename columns | `df.rename(columns={...})` |
| Sort values | `df.sort_values('col', ascending=False)` |
| Remove duplicates | `df.drop_duplicates()` |
| Replace values | `df.replace({old:new})` |
| Get top-N per group | `df.groupby('g').head(3)` |
| Encode categories | `pd.get_dummies(df['cat'])` (One-Hot) |
| Normalize a column | `(df['x'] - df['x'].min()) / (df['x'].max() - df['x'].min())` |
| Apply custom fn | `df['col'].apply(func)` |
| Save outputs | `df.to_csv('out.csv', index=False)` |

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Confusing `loc` vs `iloc` slicing endpoints** — `loc` is *inclusive*, `iloc` is *exclusive* of the stop label. Easy 1-mark loss per occurrence.
> 2. **Forgetting `inplace=True` semantics** — Most students mutate the original df without realizing. Always state whether the operation is in-place.
> 3. **Wrong axis parameter** — `axis=0` collapses **rows** (operates *down columns*). Writing `axis=1` when you meant `axis=0` costs 2 marks.
> 4. **GroupBy with non-aggregable function** — You cannot `mean()` a string column. Use `agg(np.size)` or `count()`.
> 5. **Chain-indexing** — `df[mask]['col'] = 1` triggers `SettingWithCopyWarning` and *may not* modify the original. Use `df.loc[mask, 'col'] = 1` instead.
> 6. **Forgetting `np.nan` vs `None`** — Only `np.nan` is treated as missing by `isnull()`. `None` in numeric cols is auto-converted to `NaN`, but in object cols remains `None`.

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]`
**Differentiate between a Pandas `Series` and a `DataFrame`. Mention their dimensionalities and give one example construction for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Property | `Series` | `DataFrame` |
|---|---|---|
| Dimensionality | 1-D labeled array | 2-D labeled table |
| Data Types | Homogeneous (single dtype) | Heterogeneous across columns |
| Analogy | Single Excel column | Full Excel sheet / SQL table |
| Has row index? | Yes | Yes (row + column index) |

**Examples:**

```python
# Series
s = pd.Series([10, 20, 30], index=['a','b','c'])

# DataFrame
df = pd.DataFrame({'A':[1,2], 'B':[3,4]})
```

*(1 mark each for: difference, dimensionality, example.)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected Pattern]`
**Explain the difference between `df.loc[]` and `df.iloc[]` with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

`df.loc[]` is **label-based** indexing, whereas `df.iloc[]` is **integer-position-based** indexing.

```python
df = pd.DataFrame({'X':[1,2,3], 'Y':[4,5,6]}, index=['r1','r2','r3'])

df.loc['r1':'r2', 'X']      # INCLUSIVE of 'r2' → returns rows r1, r2
df.iloc[0:2, 0]              # EXCLUSIVE of index 2 → returns rows 0, 1
```

Key difference: **slicing in `loc` includes the stop-label; `iloc` follows Python convention** (excludes stop-index). *(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for example, 1 mark for key difference.)*

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]`

**(a)** Consider the following DataFrame `sales`:

| Region  | Product | Units | Revenue |
|---|---|---|---|
| North   | A       | 100   | 5000    |
| South   | B       | 150   | 9000    |
| North   | A       | 80    | 4000    |
| East    | C       | 200   | 12000   |
| West    | B       | 120   | 7200    |
| South   | A       | 90    | 4500    |

Write Pandas code (or pseudo-code) to:
1. Compute the total **Revenue** per **Region**.
2. Find the product with the **maximum total units** sold.
3. Create a pivot table showing **average Revenue** with **Region as rows** and **Product as columns**.

**(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the concept of **missing data handling** in Pandas. Compare `dropna()` and `fillna()` with examples. Also, write code to:
1. Detect missing values in a DataFrame.
2. Replace missing numerical values with the **column mean**.
3. Replace missing categorical values with the **mode (most frequent value)**.

**(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question A

**Part (a) Solution:**

```python
import pandas as pd

data = {
    'Region'  : ['North','South','North','East','West','South'],
    'Product' : ['A','B','A','C','B','A'],
    'Units'   : [100, 150, 80, 200, 120, 90],
    'Revenue' : [5000, 9000, 4000, 12000, 7200, 4500]
}
sales = pd.DataFrame(data)

# (1) Total revenue per region
rev_by_region = sales.groupby('Region')['Revenue'].sum()
print(rev_by_region)
```

**Output:**

```
East     12000
North     9000
South    13500
West      7200
Name: Revenue, dtype: int64
```
*(2 marks for correct code + output.)*

```python
# (2) Product with max total units
units_by_product = sales.groupby('Product')['Units'].sum()
top_product = units_by_product.idxmax()      # returns the index label
print("Top product:", top_product, "with", units_by_product.max(), "units")
```

**Output:**

```
Top product: A with 270 units
```
*(2 marks for grouping + idxmax logic.)*

```python
# (3) Pivot table
pivot = sales.pivot_table(
    values='Revenue',
    index='Region',
    columns='Product',
    aggfunc='mean'
)
print(pivot)
```

**Output:**

```
Product      A       B       C
Region
East       NaN     NaN  12000.0
North   4500.0     NaN     NaN
South   4500.0  9000.0     NaN
West       NaN  7200.0     NaN
```
*(3 marks: 1 for `pivot_table` call, 1 for parameters, 1 for output.)*

---

**Part (b) Solution:**

**Conceptual Explanation (3 marks):**

Real-world datasets are rarely complete. Pandas represents missing data as `NaN` (Not a Number) for numeric columns and `None` for object columns. Two main strategies:

- **Deletion (`dropna`)**: Remove rows/columns containing missing values. Best when missingness is *small* (<5% of data) and *MCAR* (Missing Completely At Random).
- **Imputation (`fillna`)**: Replace missing values with a sensible estimate (mean, median, mode, forward-fill, interpolation). Best for larger missingness or time-series data.

| Method | Effect | When to Use |
|---|---|---|
| `dropna()` | Removes rows/cols with NaN | Small % missing, MCAR |
| `fillna(val)` | Replaces NaN with `val` | Need to preserve sample size |
| `interpolate()` | Fills via linear/poly fit | Time-series / ordered data |
| `ffill()` / `bfill()` | Forward / backward fill | Time-series continuity |

**Code (4 marks):**

```python
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

df = pd.DataFrame({
    'Age'    : [20, np.nan, 22, 19, np.nan],
    'Gender' : ['M', 'F', np.nan, 'M', 'F'],
    'Marks'  : [85, 90, np.nan, 78, 95]
})

# (1) Detect missing values
print(df.isnull().sum())
```

**Output:**

```
Age       2
Gender    1
Marks     1
dtype: int64
```
*(1 mark)*

```python
# (2) Replace missing numeric with mean
df['Age']  = df['Age'].fillna(df['Age'].mean())
df['Marks']= df['Marks'].fillna(df['Marks'].mean())
print(df)
```

**Mean Imputation Formula:**

$$
\hat{x}_{\text{impute}} = \bar{x} = \frac{1}{n - m} \sum_{i: x_i \neq \text{NaN}} x_i
$$
*(1 mark for formula or equivalent reasoning.)*

```python
# (3) Replace missing categorical with mode
df['Gender'] = df['Gender'].fillna(df['Gender'].mode()[0])
print(df)
```

**Final Output:**

```
    Age Gender  Marks
0  20.0      M   85.0
1  20.5      F   90.0
2  22.0      M   87.0
3  19.0      M   78.0
4  20.5      F   95.0
```
*(2 marks for mode code and verification.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Pattern]`

**(a)** Explain the **GroupBy** mechanism in Pandas using the **Split-Apply-Combine** paradigm. Given a DataFrame of student records with columns `['Name', 'Department', 'Year', 'Marks']`, write code to compute the **mean, max, and count** of marks grouped by `Department`. Also show how to perform a **two-level groupby** (by `Department` and `Year`). *(7 marks)*

**(b)** With neat code examples, explain the following operations on a Pandas DataFrame:
1. Concatenating two DataFrames (`pd.concat`).
2. Merging two DataFrames (`pd.merge`) with both `inner` and `left` join types.
3. Reshaping using `pivot_table` and `melt`.

*(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — Question B

**Part (a) Solution:**

**Conceptual Explanation (3 marks):**

The **GroupBy** mechanism implements the **Split–Apply–Combine** strategy (Hadley Wickham, 2010):

1. **Split:** The DataFrame is partitioned into groups based on the values of one or more keys.
2. **Apply:** A function (e.g., `sum`, `mean`, custom `lambda`) is applied independently to each group.
3. **Combine:** The results are stitched back together into a new aggregated DataFrame.

**Diagram:**

$$
\text{GroupBy} : \mathcal{D} \xrightarrow{\text{Split}} \{G_1, G_2, \dots, G_k\} \xrightarrow{\text{Apply } f} \{f(G_1), f(G_2), \dots, f(G_k)\} \xrightarrow{\text{Combine}} \mathcal{D}'
$$

**Code (4 marks):**

```python
import pandas as pd

df = pd.DataFrame({
    'Name'       : ['A','B','C','D','E','F','G','H'],
    'Department' : ['CSE','ECE','CSE','MECH','CSE','ECE','MECH','CSE'],
    'Year'       : [2, 3, 2, 4, 3, 2, 4, 3],
    'Marks'      : [85, 78, 92, 70, 88, 81, 75, 90]
})

# Single-level groupby: mean, max, count of Marks by Department
result1 = df.groupby('Department')['Marks'].agg(['mean','max','count']).round(2)
print(result1)
```

**Output:**

```
             mean  max  count
Department
CSE         88.75   92      4
ECE         79.50   81      2
MECH        72.50   75      2
```
*(2 marks)*

```python
# Two-level (hierarchical) groupby
result2 = df.groupby(['Department','Year'])['Marks'].agg(['mean','count']).round(2)
print(result2)
```

**Output:**

```
                      mean  count
Department Year
CSE        2     88.50      2
           3     89.00      2
ECE        2     81.00      1
           3     78.00      1
MECH       4     72.50      2
```
*(2 marks for hierarchical groupby.)*

---

**Part (b) Solution:**

**1. Concatenation (2 marks):**

```python
df1 = pd.DataFrame({'A':[1,2], 'B':[3,4]})
df2 = pd.DataFrame({'A':[5,6], 'B':[7,8]})

# Vertical stack (default axis=0)
vertical = pd.concat([df1, df2], ignore_index=True)

# Horizontal stack (axis=1)
horizontal = pd.concat([df1, df2], axis=1)
```

**2. Merging (3 marks):**

```python
emp = pd.DataFrame({'EmpID':[1,2,3], 'Name':['A','B','C']})
sal = pd.DataFrame({'EmpID':[1,2,4], 'Salary':[50,60,70]})

# Inner join: only common EmpIDs (intersection)
inner = pd.merge(emp, sal, on='EmpID', how='inner')
# Result: EmpID 1, 2 only

# Left join: all rows from emp, NaN where no match
left = pd.merge(emp, sal, on='EmpID', how='left')
# Result: EmpID 1, 2, 3 (Salary of 3 is NaN)
```

**3. Reshaping (2 marks):**

```python
# pivot_table: long/wide conversion
df_pivot = df.pivot_table(
    values='Marks', index='Department', columns='Year', aggfunc='mean'
)

# melt: wide → long
df_melt = df_pivot.reset_index().melt(
    id_vars='Department', var_name='Year', value_name='AvgMarks'
)
```

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Rapid-Recall Bullets for the KTU Exam Hall**

- [x] **Pandas = Panel Data + Python Data Analysis.** Built on NumPy; provides `Series` (1-D) and `DataFrame` (2-D).
- [x] **Import convention:** `import pandas as pd`. NumPy is imported as `np`.
- [x] **`Series`** is a labeled 1-D array. **DataFrame** is a labeled, heterogeneously-typed 2-D table with row & column index.
- [x] **Indexing triad:** `loc` (label) | `iloc` (position) | `at/iat` (scalar fast access). **`loc` slicing is inclusive; `iloc` slicing is exclusive.**
- [x] **Axis convention:** `axis=0` ⇒ **rows** (vertical); `axis=1` ⇒ **columns** (horizontal).
- [x] **Inspection trio:** `head()`, `info()`, `describe()` are mandatory diagnostic steps before any ML modelling.
- [x] **Missing data sentinel = `np.nan`.** Detect with `isnull()` / `isna()`. Treat with `dropna()` or `fillna()`.
- [x] **Imputation rules:** mean/median for numeric, mode for categorical, interpolation for time-series, `ffill/bfill` for ordered data.
- [x] **`GroupBy` = Split → Apply → Combine.** Use `agg(['mean','sum','count'])` for multi-statistics.
- [x] **`concat` = UNION (set-theory).** `merge` = INTERSECTION by default; supports `inner/left/right/outer`.
- [x] **`pivot_table` requires an `aggfunc`.** Default is `mean`. Handles duplicate entries via aggregation.
- [x] **`apply()` is slow** vs vectorization. Prefer **NumPy ufuncs** or **Pandas built-ins** when possible.
- [x] **`inplace=True`** permanently mutates the DataFrame; generally avoided in production code.
- [x] **Avoid chain-indexing:** `df[mask]['col'] = 1` is buggy. Use `df.loc[mask, 'col'] = 1` instead.
- [x] **One-Hot Encoding:** `pd.get_dummies(df['cat'])` converts categorical → binary indicator columns.
- [x] **Min-Max Normalization formula:**
$$
x_{\text{norm}} = \frac{x - \min(x)}{\max(x) - \min(x)}
$$
- [x] **Time-Series:** `pd.date_range()`, `resample('W'/'M'/'Q')`, `shift()`, `rolling(window=k).mean()`.
- [x] **I/O quick-look:** `read_csv`, `read_excel`, `read_json`, `read_sql`, `to_csv`, `to_parquet`.
- [x] **Pandas DataFrame is the standard input to `sklearn.fit(X, y)`** — must convert labels to NumPy via `.values` or `.to_numpy()`.

> **Final Note:** In KTU 2024 ESE for PBEOT604 (Machine Learning), a typical 14-mark Pandas question will be a **hybrid question** combining a code-writing task (~7 marks) with a conceptual explanation (~7 marks). Master the **inspection, indexing, missing-data, groupby, and merge** workflows — these five alone account for >80% of past question patterns.

---
