## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**
*Define a dataset in the context of machine learning. Differentiate between feature matrix and label vector with suitable notation.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **dataset** is a structured collection of data records used to train, validate, and test a machine learning model. Mathematically, it is the pair $(\mathbf{X}, \mathbf{y})$ for supervised learning.

- **Feature Matrix** $\mathbf{X} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times m}$: an $n \times m$ matrix where $n$ is the number of instances (rows) and $m$ is the number of features (columns). Each row $\mathbf{x}_i \in \mathbb{R}^m$ is the feature vector of the $i$-th instance.
- **Label Vector** $\mathbf{y} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$: a column vector of length $n$ containing the target/output for each instance.

The model learns a function $f: \mathbb{R}^m \to \mathcal{Y}$ such that $f(\mathbf{x}_i) \approx y_i$ for all $i$. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
*List and briefly explain any three common strategies for handling missing values in a dataset.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Mean/Median/Mode Imputation (1 mark):** Replace missing entries with the central tendency of the column. Mean is sensitive to outliers; median is preferred for skewed numerical data; mode is used for categorical features.

2. **Dropping Rows/Columns (1 mark):** Discard instances or features with missing values. Acceptable when the missing proportion is small (<5%) and missingness is random, but reduces sample size.

3. **Model-Based Imputation (1 mark):** Use a predictive model (e.g., KNN, regression) trained on complete rows to predict and fill the missing values. More accurate but computationally expensive.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common student pitfall:** Students often say *"delete the missing rows"* without first checking the *percentage* of missingness or the *pattern* of missingness (MCAR, MAR, MNAR). KTU expects you to comment on the *trade-off* (information loss vs. data integrity).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

**Question A. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the standard pipeline for "working with datasets" in a machine learning project. Discuss the importance of stratified sampling during the train-test split. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider the dataset:

| Instance | $x_1$ | $x_2$ | $y$ |
|----------|-------|-------|-----|
| 1 | 10 | 100 | 0 |
| 2 | 20 | 200 | 1 |
| 3 | 30 | 300 | 1 |
| 4 | 40 | 400 | 0 |

Apply **Min-Max Normalization** to $x_1$ and **Z-Score Standardization** to $x_2$. Show all intermediate steps. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Pipeline (7 marks):**

The standard pipeline consists of six stages:

1. **Data Ingestion (1 mark):** Loading data from CSV/database/APIs into a feature matrix $\mathbf{X} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times m}$ and label vector $\mathbf{y}$.

2. **Exploratory Data Analysis (1 mark):** Inspecting shape, types, summary statistics, and class distribution. Generates histograms, box plots, and correlation heatmaps.

3. **Data Cleaning (1 mark):** Handling missing values (imputation/dropping) and outliers (IQR/Z-score methods).

4. **Feature Engineering & Encoding (1 mark):** Encoding categorical variables (one-hot for nominal, label for ordinal), creating polynomial/interaction features.

5. **Feature Scaling (1 mark):** Normalization to $[0,1]$ or standardization to $N(0,1)$ to make features comparable.

6. **Train-Validation-Test Split (1 mark):** Partitioning into 70/15/15 (or similar) subsets.

7. **Importance of Stratified Sampling (1 mark):** When the dataset is **imbalanced** (e.g., 95% class 0, 5% class 1), random sampling may yield subsets with very different class proportions. **Stratified sampling** preserves the original class ratio $P(y=c_k)$ in every subset, ensuring the model sees a representative distribution during training and is evaluated on a representative test set.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Min-Max Normalization on $x_1$ (3.5 marks):**

For $x_1$: $x_{\min} = 10$, $x_{\max} = 40$, range $= 30$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
x'_1 &= (10 - 10)/30 = 0.0000 \\
x'_2 &= (20 - 10)/30 = 0.3333 \\
x'_3 &= (30 - 10)/30 = 0.6667 \\
x'_4 &= (40 - 10)/30 = 1.0000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Z-Score Standardization on $x_2$ (3.5 marks):**

For $x_2$: $\mathbf{x}_2 = (100, 200, 300, 400)$, $n = 4$.

Compute mean:
$$\mu = \frac{100 + 200 + 300 + 400}{4} = 250$$

Compute variance:
$$\sigma^2 = \frac{(100-250)^2 + (200-250)^2 + (300-250)^2 + (400-250)^2}{4}$$

$$
\sigma^2 = \frac{22500 + 2500 + 2500 + 22500}{4} = \frac{50000}{4} = 12500
$$

So $\sigma = \sqrt{12500} \approx 111.803$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
z_1 &= (100 - 250)/111.803 \approx -1.342 \\
z_2 &= (200 - 250)/111.803 \approx -0.447 \\
z_3 &= (300 - 250)/111.803 \approx +0.447 \\
z_4 &= (400 - 250)/111.803 \approx +1.342
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Scaled Dataset (verifying):**

| Instance | $x_1$ (norm) | $x_2$ (std) | $y$ |
|----------|--------------|-------------|-----|
| 1 | 0.0000 | −1.342 | 0 |
| 2 | 0.3333 | −0.447 | 1 |
| 3 | 0.6667 | +0.447 | 1 |
| 4 | 1.0000 | +1.342 | 0 |

Both features now lie in compatible numeric ranges, ensuring that distance-based algorithms treat them equally. **(Full 7 marks)**

---

**Question B. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** Explain the difference between **Min-Max Normalization** and **Z-Score Standardization**. Under what conditions would you prefer one over the other? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A dataset has 1000 instances with the following class distribution: 800 instances of class A and 200 of class B. Perform a **stratified 70-30 split** and compute the number of instances of each class in the training and test sets. Discuss why this split is preferred over a simple random split for this dataset. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Min-Max vs. Z-Score (7 marks):**

| Aspect | Min-Max Normalization | Z-Score Standardization |
|--------|----------------------|------------------------|
| **Formula** | $x' = (x - x_{\min})/(x_{\max} - x_{\min})$ | $x' = (x - \mu)/\sigma$ |
| **Range** | $[0, 1]$ (bounded) | Unbounded, typically $[-3, +3]$ |
| **Sensitivity to outliers** | **High** — outliers compress the rest of the data | **Low** — uses mean and std (more robust) |
| **Distribution assumption** | None (non-parametric) | Assumes roughly Gaussian for interpretability |
| **Output statistics** | Bounded range, original shape preserved | Mean = 0, Variance = 1 |
| **Use case** | Neural networks, image pixels, bounded inputs | Linear/logistic regression, SVM, PCA, k-NN, K-Means |

**When to prefer (1 mark each):**

1. **Prefer Min-Max when** the data has a known bounded range, no severe outliers, and the algorithm requires inputs in $[0, 1]$ (e.g., sigmoid-activated networks, image processing).

2. **Prefer Z-Score when** the data contains outliers, the distribution is approximately Gaussian, or the algorithm assumes zero-centered inputs (e.g., logistic regression, PCA, SVM with RBF kernel).

3. **Prefer Robust Scaling when** outliers dominate and one cannot remove them.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students simply state "Min-Max is for neural networks" without justifying the *mathematical reason* (bounded activation outputs). Always explain the *why* — KTU 2024 Scheme awards marks for reasoning, not rote statements.

---

**(b) Stratified 70-30 Split (7 marks):**

**Given:** $n = 1000$, $n_A = 800$, $n_B = 200$, split ratio $= 70/30$.

**Step 1 — Compute training set size:** $n_{\text{train}} = 0.70 \times 1000 = 700$ instances.

**Step 2 — Compute test set size:** $n_{\text{test}} = 0.30 \times 1000 = 300$ instances.

**Step 3 — Stratified allocation for class A (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{A,\text{train}} &= 0.70 \times 800 = 560 \\
n_{A,\text{test}} &= 0.30 \times 800 = 240
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Stratified allocation for class B (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{B,\text{train}} &= 0.70 \times 200 = 140 \\
n_{B,\text{test}} &= 0.30 \times 200 = 60
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Verify totals (1 mark):**

- Training: $560 + 140 = 700$ ✓
- Test: $240 + 60 = 300$ ✓
- Total: $1000$ ✓
- Class ratio preserved in both: $560/140 = 240/60 = 4:1$ ✓

**Step 6 — Why Stratified > Random (1 mark):**

The dataset is **imbalanced** (4:1 ratio). A simple random split *could* produce a training set with, e.g., 580 A's and 120 B's, and a test set with 220 A's and 80 B's — distorting evaluation. Stratified sampling **guarantees** the 4:1 ratio in *both* subsets, ensuring the model is trained on a representative distribution and evaluated on a representative distribution. This yields more reliable performance estimates, especially for the minority class (B).

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks:**
> 1. Forgetting to **round** consistently (always use floor/ceiling that sums to $n$).
> 2. Not **verifying** the totals add up.
> 3. Failing to state that **class proportions are preserved** — this is the *defining* property of stratified sampling.
> 4. Mixing up training and test set allocations.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Working with Datasets**

- [x] A dataset is the pair $(\mathbf{X}, \mathbf{y})$ where $\mathbf{X} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times m}$ is the feature matrix and $\mathbf{y}$ is the label vector.
- [x] Features are of three primary types: **numerical** (continuous/discrete), **categorical** (nominal/ordinal), and **binary**.
- [x] The five pillars: **Inspection → Cleaning → Encoding → Scaling → Splitting**.
- [x] **Min-Max Normalization** maps to $[0, 1]$ but is sensitive to outliers.
- [x] **Z-Score Standardization** yields $\mu_z = 0$ and $\sigma_z^2 = 1$; preferred for Gaussian-like data.
- [x] **Train/Validation/Test** split prevents data leakage; common ratios are 70/15/15 or 80/10/10.
- [x] **Stratified sampling** preserves class ratio $P(y=c_k)$ in every partition — critical for imbalanced datasets.
- [x] **Outlier detection**: IQR rule ($1.5 \times \text{IQR}$) or Z-score ($\vert z \vert > 3$).
- [x] **Encoding**: Label encoding for ordinal; One-hot encoding for nominal categorical.
- [x] Always scale features *after* the train-test split using statistics (mean, std, min, max) computed from the **training set only** — to prevent **data leakage**.
- [x] Distance-based algorithms (k-NN, K-Means, SVM) **require** feature scaling; tree-based models (Decision Tree, Random Forest) do not.
- [x] For small datasets ($n < 1000$), prefer **k-fold cross-validation** over a single hold-out split.
- [x] **Entropy** and **Gini impurity** are split criteria for trees derived from dataset class distributions.
- [x] Always inspect the **shape** of $\mathbf{X}$ and the **class balance** of $\mathbf{y}$ before modeling.

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Formula Memory Aid (KTU Board Favorite):**
> - $\bar{x} = \frac{1}{n}\sum x_i$
> - $\sigma^2 = \frac{1}{n}\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2$
> - $z_i = (x_i - \mu)/\sigma$
> - $x' = (x - x_{\min})/(x_{\max} - x_{\min})$
> - $\text{IQR} = Q_3 - Q_1$
> - $\cos\theta = \frac{\mathbf{x} \cdot \mathbf{y}}{\Vert \mathbf{x} \Vert_2 \Vert \mathbf{y} \Vert_2}$
> - $H = -\sum_k p_k \log_2 p_k$
> - $G = 1 - \sum_k p_k^2$
